## DP setup
Let \(F(s,t)\) be the number of sequences of positive integers with total sum \(s\) whose left-to-right \(\circ\)-value equals \(t\).

**Initialization (one-term sequences):** for each \(p\in\{1,\dots,12\}\),
\[
F(p,p)\mathrel{+}=1.
\]

**Transition:** from a state \((s,t)\) and choosing next part \(p\ge 1\) with \(s+p\le 12\),
\[
t'=
\begin{cases}
t-p,& t\ \text{odd and }p\ \text{even}\\
t+p,& \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\qquad\Rightarrow\qquad
F(s+p,t')\mathrel{+}=F(s,t).
\]

We iterate \(s=1\) to \(11\).

A useful check is \(\sum_t F(s,t)=2^{s-1}\), the number of compositions of \(s\); this holds at each \(s\) in the computation below.

---

Listing nonzero \(F(s,t)\) for \(s=1,\dots,12\):

- \(s=6:\ \{6:24,\ 2:6,\ -2:2\}\)
- \(s=7:\ \{7:34,\ 3:17,\ -1:9,\ -5:4\}\)
- \(s=8:\ \{8:82,\ 4:29,\ 0:13,\ -4:4\}\)
- \(s=9:\ \{9:116,\ 5:70,\ 1:42,\ -3:20,\ -7:8\}\)
- \(s=10:\ \{10:280,\ 6:128,\ 2:68,\ -2:28,\ -6:8\}\)
- \(s=11:\ \{11:396,\ 7:280,\ 3:187,\ -1:101,\ -5:44,\ -9:16\}\)

Let the operation be evaluated left-to-right.  If after processing the first \(i\) terms we have value \(x\), then appending the next term \(k=a_{i+1}\) gives
\[
x\circ k=\begin{cases}
x-k,& x\text{ odd and }k\text{ even},\\
x+k,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

### DP setup
For \(1\le s\le 12\) and integer \(x\), define \(f_s(x)\) to be the number of sequences of positive integers \((a_1,\dots,a_m)\) such that

- \(a_1+\cdots+a_m=s\),
- \(a_1\circ a_2\circ\cdots\circ a_m=x\).

**Initialization (length 1):** the only sequence summing to \(s\) of length 1 is \((s)\), and its value is \(s\). Hence
\[
f_s(s)=1\quad (1\le s\le 12),
\]
and all other \(f_s(x)=0\) initially.

**Transition:** from a state \((s,x)\), append any \(k\ge 1\) with \(s+k\le 12\). Then
\[
f_{s+k}(x') \mathrel{+}= f_s(x),
\]
where \(x' = x-k\) if \(x\) is odd and \(k\) is even, and \(x'=x+k\) otherwise.

### Iteration results
Carrying out this recurrence for \(s=1,2,\dots,11\) yields the following distributions (only nonzero \(x\) shown):

\[
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &: \{1:1\}\\
f_2 &: \{2:2\}\\
f_3 &: \{3:3,\,-1:1\}\\
f_4 &: \{4:7,\,0:1\}\\
f_5 &: \{5:10,\,1:4,\,-3:2\}\\
f_6 &: \{6:24,\,2:6,\,-2:2\}\\
f_7 &: \{7:34,\,3:17,\,-1:9,\,-5:4\}\\
f_8 &: \{8:82,\,4:29,\,0:13,\,-4:4\}\\
f_9 &: \{9:116,\,5:70,\,1:42,\,-3:20,\,-7:8\}\\
f_{10} &: \{10:280,\,6:128,\,2:68,\,-2:28,\,-6:8\}\\
f_{11} &: \{11:396,\,7:280,\,3:187,\,-1:101,\,-5:44,\,-9:16\}\\
f_{12} &: \{12:956,\,8:536,\,4:323,\,0:157,\,-4:60,\,-8:16\}.
\end{aligned}
\]

(As a check, \(\sum_x f_{12}(x)=2048=2^{11}\), the total number of compositions of 12.)

We want sequences summing to \(12\) with final value \(0\), i.e. \(f_{12}(0)=157\).

Let the running value after processing the first few terms be \(x\).  When we next apply \(\circ\) with a positive integer \(t\),

\[
x\circ t=\begin{cases}
x-t,& x\text{ odd and }t\text{ even},\\
x+t,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

### DP setup
For integers \(s\ge 0\) and \(x\in\mathbb Z\), define \(F(s,x)\) to be the number of sequences \((t_1,\dots,t_k)\) of positive integers with sum \(t_1+\cdots+t_k=s\) such that, starting from value \(x\) and applying \(\circ\) left-to-right with \(t_1,\dots,t_k\), we end at \(0\).

Then:
- Base case: \(F(0,x)=1\) if \(x=0\), and \(0\) otherwise.
- Recurrence (choose the next term \(t\in\{1,\dots,s\}\)):
\[
F(s,x)=\sum_{t=1}^{s} F(s-t,\; x\circ t).
\]

Our desired answer is obtained by choosing \(a_1\) first. If \(a_1=i\), then the remaining sum is \(12-i\) and we start the DP at \(x=i\). Hence the total number is
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{12} F(12-i,i).
\]

### Computing \(F(s,x)\) for \(s\le 11\)
Using the recurrence (and noting that for each fixed \(s\), only a few \(x\) give nonzero values), we obtain the following nonzero values:

\[
\begin{array}{c|l}
s & \text{Nonzero }F(s,x)\\ \hline
0 & F(0,0)=1\\
1 & F(1,-1)=1\\
2 & F(2,-2)=2\\
3 & F(3,-3)=3,\;F(3,1)=1\\
4 & F(4,-4)=7,\;F(4,0)=1\\
5 & F(5,-5)=10,\;F(5,-1)=4,\;F(5,3)=2\\
6 & F(6,-6)=24,\;F(6,-2)=6,\;F(6,2)=2\\
7 & F(7,-7)=34,\;F(7,-3)=17,\;F(7,1)=9,\;F(7,5)=4\\
8 & F(8,-8)=82,\;F(8,-4)=29,\;F(8,0)=13,\;F(8,4)=4\\
9 & F(9,-9)=116,\;F(9,-5)=70,\;F(9,-1)=42,\;F(9,3)=20,\;F(9,7)=8\\
10& F(10,-10)=280,\;F(10,-6)=128,\;F(10,-2)=68,\;F(10,2)=28,\;F(10,6)=8\\
11& F(11,-11)=396,\;F(11,-7)=280,\;F(11,-3)=187,\;F(11,1)=101,\;F(11,5)=44,\;F(11,9)=16
\end{array}
\]

### Final sum over \(a_1\)
We need \(F(12-i,i)\) for \(i=1,\dots,12\). From the table:

- \(i=1\): \(F(11,1)=101\)
- \(i=2\): \(F(10,2)=28\)
- \(i=3\): \(F(9,3)=20\)
- \(i=4\): \(F(8,4)=4\)
- \(i=5\): \(F(7,5)=4\)
- For \(i\ge 6\), \(F(12-i,i)=0\) (those \(x\) do not appear among the nonzero states for the corresponding \(s\)).

Therefore the total number of sequences is
\[
101+28+20+4+4=157.
\]

Work modulo \(3\).  Replace each \(a_k\in\{1,2,3\}\) by its residue in \(\mathbb F_3=\{0,1,2\}\) (so \(3\equiv 0\)).  This is a bijection, so we are counting \((a_1,\dots,a_7)\in\mathbb F_3^7\) such that

\[
S_1:=a_1+\cdots+a_7\equiv 0,\qquad 
S_2:=\sum_{i=1}^7 a_i a_{i+1} a_{i+3}\equiv 0
\]
(indices mod \(7\)).

Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}\) so \(\sum_{x\in\mathbb F_3}\omega^{tx}=3\) if \(t=0\) and \(0\) otherwise.

## 1) Root-of-unity filter
The desired number is
\[
N=\frac1{9}\sum_{u,v\in\mathbb F_3}\; Z(u,v),\qquad
Z(u,v):=\sum_{a\in\mathbb F_3^7}\omega^{uS_1+vS_2}.
\]

If \(v=0\) and \(u\neq 0\), then
\[
Z(u,0)=\Big(\sum_{x\in\mathbb F_3}\omega^{ux}\Big)^7=0.
\]
So only \(v\neq 0\) matters besides \((u,v)=(0,0)\), where \(Z(0,0)=3^7=2187\).

Also, the bijection \(a_k\mapsto 2a_k\) sends \((S_1,S_2)\mapsto (2S_1,2S_2)\), hence
\[
Z(u,v)=Z(2u,2v).
\]
Thus \(Z(0,1)=Z(0,2)\), \(Z(1,1)=Z(2,2)\), \(Z(1,2)=Z(2,1)\). Therefore
\[
N=\frac19\bigl(2187+2A+2B+2C\bigr)
\]
where
\[
A:=Z(0,1),\quad B:=Z(1,1),\quad C:=Z(1,2).
\]

In all three cases we sum first over \(a_7\). Since \(a_7\) appears linearly in the exponent, this produces a factor \(3\) and one constraint (a coefficient must be \(0\)), by \(\sum_{a_7}\omega^{a_7(\cdots)}\).

Below, write \(p=a_1,q=a_3,r=a_4,s=a_5\) (so a “quadruple” \((p,q,r,s)\in\mathbb F_3^4\)), and define
\[
X=pr+qs,\quad Y=rs+pq,\quad Z=qr+ps,\quad S=p+q+r+s.
\]

---

## 2) Compute \(A=Z(0,1)=\sum\omega^{S_2}\)

Summing over \(a_7\) forces
\[
rs+a_6a_2+pq=0,
\]
and contributes a factor \(3\). Summing next over \(a_6\) and separating \(a_2=0\) vs \(a_2\in\{1,2\}\), one finds (after combining the \(a_2=1\) and \(a_2=2\) parts using \(\omega^t+\omega^{2t}=2\) if \(t=0\) else \(-1\)) that
\[
A=9N_0-72
\]
where \(N_0\) is the number of quadruples \((p,q,r,s)\in\mathbb F_3^4\) satisfying
\[
X=YZ.
\]
Fix \((p,q)\in\mathbb F_3^2\) and count \((r,s)\in\mathbb F_3^2\) solutions:

- If \((p,q)=(0,0)\): then \(X=Z=0\), so \(X=YZ\) always, giving \(9\) solutions.
- If \((p,q)\neq(0,0)\): a short case check (whether one of \(p,q\) is \(0\) or both nonzero) gives exactly \(7\) solutions for each of the other \(8\) choices of \((p,q)\).

Hence \(N_0=9+8\cdot 7=65\), so
\[
A=9\cdot 65-72=513.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(B=Z(1,1)=\sum\omega^{S_1+S_2}\)

Summing over \(a_7\) forces
\[
rs+a_6a_2+pq=2,
\]
and gives a factor \(3\). Summing over \(a_6\) and combining \(a_2=1,2\) as before yields
\[
B=9(S_A+S_B),
\]
where
\[
S_A=\sum_{\substack{Y=2\\ Z=2}}\omega^{S},\qquad
S_B=\sum_{\substack{T=0}}\omega^{S}
\]
and here \(T\) (coming from the \(a_2\in\{1,2\}\) combined condition) is
\[
T=1+X+(2-Y)(1+Z).
\]

Now compute these by fixing \((p,q)\in\mathbb F_3^2\) and scanning the \(9\) possibilities for \((r,s)\in\mathbb F_3^2\) (only \(81\) checks total):

- For \((Y,Z)=(2,2)\), the weighted sum comes out
  \[
  S_A=-5.
  \]
- For \(T=0\), the weighted sum comes out
  \[
  S_B=-16.
  \]

Therefore
\[
B=9(-5-16)=-189.
\]

(Each of these weighted sums is real because conjugate values pair up.)

---

## 4) Compute \(C=Z(1,2)=\sum\omega^{S_1+2S_2}\)

Summing over \(a_7\) forces
\[
rs+a_6a_2+pq=1,
\]
and gives a factor \(3\). Summing over \(a_6\) and combining \(a_2=1,2\) yields
\[
C=9(S_C+S_D),
\]
where
\[
S_C=\sum_{\substack{Y=1\\ Z=1}}\omega^{S},\qquad
S_D=\sum_{\substack{T'=0}}\omega^{S},
\]
and now
\[
T'=1+2X+(1-Y)(1+2Z)=2-X-Y-Z+YZ.
\]

Again enumerating the \(81\) quadruples:

- For \((Y,Z)=(1,1)\), every solution has \(\omega^{S}=1\), giving
  \[
  S_C=10.
  \]
- For \(T'=0\), the weighted sum is
  \[
  S_D=23.
  \]

Thus
\[
C=9(10+23)=297.
\]

---

## 5) Final count
\[
N=\frac19\bigl(2187+2A+2B+2C\bigr)
=\frac19\bigl(2187+2\cdot 513+2\cdot(-189)+2\cdot 297\bigr)
=\frac{3429}{9}=381.
\]

Work modulo \(3\). Since \(1\mapsto 1,\ 2\mapsto 2,\ 3\mapsto 0\) is a bijection between \(\{1,2,3\}\) and \(\mathbb F_3=\{0,1,2\}\), we may count \(7\)-tuples
\[
(x_1,\dots,x_7)\in \mathbb F_3^7
\]
satisfying

- (ii) \(\sum_{i=1}^7 x_i=0\) in \(\mathbb F_3\),
- (iii) \(F:=\sum_{i=1}^7 x_i x_{i+1} x_{i+3}=0\) in \(\mathbb F_3\) (indices mod \(7\)), since the given expression is exactly this.

Write nonzero elements of \(\mathbb F_3\) as \(\pm 1\) (where \(-1\equiv 2\pmod 3\)). Then:
- \(x_i=0\) corresponds to \(a_i=3\).
- If a term in \(F\) uses any \(x_j=0\), that term contributes \(0\).
- If all three factors are nonzero, the term is \(\pm 1\).

The \(7\) triples \((i,i+1,i+3)\) form the \(7\) “lines” of the Fano plane on points \(\{1,\dots,7\}\); we only need two facts (easy to check from the list):
1. Each point lies on exactly \(3\) lines.
2. Any two points lie on exactly one common line.

Let \(m\) be the number of nonzero \(x_i\)’s (so \(7-m\) zeros). We count by cases on \(m\).

---

### Case \(m=0\)
All \(x_i=0\). Then (ii) and (iii) hold. Count: \(1\).

### Case \(m=1\)
Sum is \(\pm 1\not\equiv 0\). Count: \(0\).

### Case \(m=2\)
No line is fully nonzero, so \(F=0\) automatically. Condition (ii) requires the two nonzeros sum to \(0\), so they must be \(+1\) and \(-1\).

Choose the 2 positions: \(\binom72=21\). For each, 2 sign assignments (\(+1,-1\) in either order).  
Count: \(21\cdot 2=42\).

### Case \(m=3\)
If the 3 nonzero positions form a line, then exactly one term of \(F\) is \(\pm 1\), so \(F\neq 0\). Thus the 3-set must **not** be a line.

There are \(\binom73=35\) triples, of which \(7\) are lines, so \(28\) are non-lines.  
If there is no full line, then \(F=0\) automatically. Condition (ii) for three signs \(\pm 1\) says the sum is divisible by \(3\), which happens only for \((+,+,+)\) or \((-,-,-)\). So 2 sign assignments.

Count: \(28\cdot 2=56\).

### Case \(m=4\)
A 4-set contains **no** line iff its complement 3-set **is** a line (because if the complement is not a line, then among the 4 points there must be a line; this is a standard small property of the Fano plane and is easily checked by counting: complements of the 7 lines give 7 such 4-sets, and the remaining \(35-7=28\) 4-sets each contain a line).

- If the 4-set contains a line, then \(F\) has at least one \(\pm 1\) term and (with only 4 nonzeros) in fact exactly one full line occurs, so \(F=\pm 1\neq 0\). So impossible.
- Thus we need a 4-set with no line: there are \(7\) such sets.

Then \(F=0\) automatically. Condition (ii) with 4 signs \(\pm 1\): integer sum \(2p-4\) divisible by \(3\) iff \(p=2\) (two \(+1\)’s and two \(-1\)’s). So \(\binom42=6\) sign assignments.

Count: \(7\cdot 6=42\).

### Case \(m=5\)
Now there are exactly \(2\) zeros. By 2-transitivity, all pairs of zero positions are equivalent; fix \(x_1=x_2=0\). Then the only lines avoiding \(\{1,2\}\) are
\[
(3,4,6),\ (4,5,7),
\]
so
\[
F = x_3x_4x_6 + x_4x_5x_7 = x_4\,(x_3x_6 + x_5x_7).
\]
Since \(x_4\neq 0\), condition \(F=0\) is equivalent to
\[
x_3x_6 + x_5x_7 = 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad x_3x_5x_6x_7 = -1.
\]
Condition (ii) with 5 signs \(\pm1\): the integer sum must be \(\pm 3\), so either exactly one \(-1\) (and four \(+1\)) or exactly one \(+1\) (and four \(-1\)).

- If exactly one \(-1\): to make \(x_3x_5x_6x_7=-1\), that lone \(-1\) must be among \(\{3,5,6,7\}\), giving \(4\) assignments.
- If exactly one \(+1\): similarly, that lone \(+1\) must be among \(\{3,5,6,7\}\), giving \(4\) assignments.

So for a fixed zero pair: \(8\) assignments. Number of zero pairs: \(\binom72=21\).  
Count: \(21\cdot 8=168\).

### Case \(m=6\)
Exactly one zero; fix \(x_1=0\). The lines avoiding point \(1\) are
\[
(2,3,5),\ (3,4,6),\ (4,5,7),\ (6,7,2),
\]
so \(F\) is a sum of 4 terms, each \(\pm1\). For \(F\equiv 0\pmod3\), the integer sum of these four \(\pm1\) must be \(0\), hence exactly two \(+1\) and two \(-1\).

Condition (ii) with 6 signs \(\pm1\): the number of \(+1\)’s must be a multiple of \(3\), i.e. \(0,3,6\). The \(0\) and \(6\) cases make all four products equal, hence \(F=\pm4\not\equiv0\). So we must have exactly three \(-1\)’s among the six nonzero entries.

Now we count sign assignments \((x_2,\dots,x_7)\in\{\pm1\}^6\) with exactly three \(-1\)’s such that among the four products
\[
x_2x_3x_5,\quad x_3x_4x_6,\quad x_4x_5x_7,\quad x_6x_7x_2
\]
exactly two are negative. There are \(\binom63=20\) choices of the 3 negative positions; a direct check shows exactly \(12\) of these 20 choices produce exactly two negative products (equivalently, 12 sign assignments work for the fixed zero at \(1\)).

Thus for each choice of the zero position: \(12\) solutions. There are \(7\) choices for the zero position.  
Count: \(7\cdot 12=84\).

### Case \(m=7\)
No zeros. Condition (ii) forces the number of \(-1\)’s to be \(2\) or \(5\) (since \(2p-7\equiv0\iff p\equiv2\pmod3\)).  

If there are exactly 2 negatives, then a line-product is negative iff the line contains exactly one of those two points. Each negative point lies on 3 lines, and the two points share exactly one line, so the number of lines containing exactly one of them is \( (3-1)+(3-1)=4\). Hence exactly \(4\) of the 7 line-products are negative, so
\[
F = (7-4) - 4 = -1 \not\equiv 0 \pmod 3.
\]
If there are 5 negatives, flipping all signs complements the set of negative line-products, giving \(3\) negative line-products, still not \(2\) or \(5\), and again \(F\not\equiv0\).  
So there are **no** solutions for \(m=7\).

Count: \(0\).

---

### Total
\[
1+0+42+56+42+168+84+0 \;=\; 393.
\]

Therefore the number of ordered \(7\)-tuples \((a_1,\dots,a_7)\) is
\[
\boxed{393}.
\]

Work in \(\Bbb Z/3\Bbb Z\).  Replace each \(a_k\in\{1,2,3\}\) by
\[
x_k \equiv a_k \pmod 3 \in\{1,2,0\}.
\]
This is a bijection \(\{1,2,3\}\leftrightarrow\{1,2,0\}\), so we count \(x=(x_1,\dots,x_7)\).

Write \(2\equiv -1\pmod 3\).  Thus each \(x_k\in\{0,\pm1\}\).

Let
\[
S:=\sum_{k=1}^7 x_k,\qquad 
Q:=\sum_{k=1}^7 x_kx_{k+1}x_{k+3}
\]
(indices mod \(7\)). The seven triples \(\{k,k+1,k+3\}\) are exactly the 7 lines of the Fano plane on points \(1,\dots,7\).  In particular:

- each term in \(Q\) is \(0\) unless all three of its entries are nonzero;
- any two lines intersect, so if the zero set \(Z=\{i:x_i=0\}\) contains a line, then every line meets \(Z\) and \(Q=0\).

Also, if we let \(n=7-|Z|\) be the number of nonzero entries and \(m\) the number of \(-1\)’s among them, then
\[
S\equiv 0 \iff \#(+1)-\#(-1)=n-2m\equiv n+m\equiv 0\pmod 3.
\]
So for each \(n\) we need \(m\equiv -n\pmod 3\).

We now classify by the zero set \(Z\) (using Fano-plane symmetry counts):
- \(|Z|=3\): either a **line** (7 choices) or a **triangle** (28 choices).
- \(|Z|=4\): either the **complement of a line** (7 choices) or “**line + point**” (28 choices).

Finally, note: if exactly one line survives in \(Q\) (i.e. exactly one term is nonzero), then \(Q=\pm1\not\equiv 0\), so there are **no solutions** in that case.

---

## Cases

### 1) \(|Z|=7\) (all zeros)
Only one tuple, and \(S=Q=0\).  Contribution: \(1\).

### 2) \(|Z|=6\) (one nonzero)
Then \(S\) cannot be \(0\) (it is \(\pm1\)). Contribution: \(0\).

### 3) \(|Z|=5\) (two nonzero)
Then \(Q=0\) automatically.  Need \(n=2\) and \(m\equiv-2\equiv 1\pmod3\), so \(m=1\): one \(+1\) and one \(-1\), i.e. \(2\) signings.
Number of zero sets: \(\binom72=21\). Contribution: \(21\cdot 2=42\).

### 4) \(|Z|=4\)
- If \(Z\) is **complement of a line** (7 choices), then \(n=3\) points form a line, so exactly one line survives in \(Q\), hence \(Q=\pm1\), impossible. Contribution: \(0\).
- If \(Z\) is “**line + point**” (28 choices), then \(n=3\) points form a triangle, so \(Q=0\) automatically. Need \(n=3\), \(m\equiv-3\equiv0\), so \(m=0\) or \(3\): all \(+1\) or all \(-1\), i.e. \(2\) signings. Contribution: \(28\cdot 2=56\).

### 5) \(|Z|=3\)
- If \(Z\) is a **triangle** (28 choices), then exactly one line is disjoint from \(Z\), so exactly one term survives in \(Q\), giving \(Q=\pm1\), impossible. Contribution: \(0\).
- If \(Z\) is a **line** (7 choices), then every line meets \(Z\), so \(Q=0\). Now \(n=4\), and \(m\equiv-4\equiv2\), so \(m=2\): choose which two are \(-1\), giving \(\binom42=6\) signings. Contribution: \(7\cdot 6=42\).

### 6) \(|Z|=2\)
By symmetry fix \(Z=\{1,2\}\). The only disjoint lines are \(\{3,4,6\}\) and \(\{4,5,7\}\), so
\[
Q=x_3x_4x_6+x_4x_5x_7.
\]
With \(x_i=\pm1\) on \(\{3,4,5,6,7\}\), this is \(0\) iff the two products are opposite, i.e.
\[
x_3x_4x_6=-x_4x_5x_7\iff x_3x_5x_6x_7=-1.
\]
So among \((x_3,x_5,x_6,x_7)\in\{\pm1\}^4\), we need product \(-1\): exactly \(8\) choices. For each such choice, the sum condition \(S=0\) uniquely forces \(x_4\) (and it always works because the partial sum is \(\pm2\), never \(0\)). Hence \(8\) signings per zero-pair.

Number of zero pairs: \(\binom72=21\). Contribution: \(21\cdot 8=168\).

### 7) \(|Z|=1\)
By symmetry fix \(Z=\{1\}\). Surviving lines are \(\{2,3,5\},\{3,4,6\},\{4,5,7\},\{6,7,2\}\), so \(Q\) is a sum of 4 signs and \(Q\equiv0\) iff (as an integer) it equals \(0\), i.e. exactly two \(+1\) and two \(-1\) among those four line-products.

Let \(a=x_2,b=x_3,c=x_4\in\{\pm1\}\) and let
\[
p_2=x_2x_3x_5,\quad p_3=x_3x_4x_6,\quad p_4=x_4x_5x_7\in\{\pm1\}.
\]
Then \(Q=0\) is equivalent to “\(p_2,p_3,p_4\) not all equal” (because the fourth product equals \(p_2p_3p_4\)). For each choice of \(a,b,c,p_2,p_3,p_4\) we can solve uniquely for \(x_5,x_6,x_7\). Checking the 8 possibilities for \((a,b,c)\), the numbers of \((p_2,p_3,p_4)\) (among the 6 allowed “not all equal”) that also make \(S=0\) are
\[
1,1,3,1,1,3,1,1
\]
summing to \(12\). Hence \(12\) solutions for each choice of the zero position.

Number of singletons \(Z\): \(7\). Contribution: \(7\cdot 12=84\).

### 8) \(|Z|=0\)
All \(x_i=\pm1\). The sum condition forces exactly 2 or 5 negatives. If exactly 2 points are negative, then exactly 4 lines contain exactly one negative point, so \(Q\) is the sum of \(3\) plus-signs and \(4\) minus-signs, i.e. \(Q=-1\not\equiv0\). Flipping all signs gives the 5-negative case, also impossible. Contribution: \(0\).

1+42+56+42+168+84 \;=\;393.

Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}\).  Work modulo \(3\).  Replace each \(a_k\in\{1,2,3\}\) by
\[
x_k \equiv a_k \pmod 3\in\{1,2,0\}=\mathbb F_3.
\]
This is a bijection between ordered \(7\)-tuples in \(\{1,2,3\}^7\) and \(\mathbb F_3^7\), so we just count \((x_1,\dots,x_7)\in\mathbb F_3^7\) such that
\[
S_1:=x_1+\cdots+x_7=0

and
\[
S_2:=x_1x_2x_4+x_2x_3x_5+x_3x_4x_6+x_4x_5x_7+x_5x_6x_1+x_6x_7x_2+x_7x_1x_3=0
\]
in \(\mathbb F_3\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 1: Use orthogonality of additive characters
For any \(y\in\mathbb F_3\),
\[
\mathbf 1_{y=0}=\frac13\sum_{t\in\mathbb F_3}\omega^{ty}.
\]
Hence the number \(N\) of solutions to \(S_1=0\) and \(S_2=0\) is
\[
N=\sum_{x\in\mathbb F_3^7}\mathbf 1_{S_1=0}\mathbf 1_{S_2=0}
=\frac19\sum_{u,v\in\mathbb F_3}\ \sum_{x\in\mathbb F_3^7}\omega^{uS_1(x)+vS_2(x)}.
\]
Define
\[
Z(u,v):=\sum_{x\in\mathbb F_3^7}\omega^{uS_1(x)+vS_2(x)},
\quad\text{so}\quad
N=\frac19\sum_{u,v\in\mathbb F_3} Z(u,v).
\]

We have immediately
\[
Z(0,0)=3^7=2187.
\]

Also, if \(v=0\) and \(u\neq 0\), then
\[
Z(u,0)=\sum_{x\in\mathbb F_3^7}\omega^{u(x_1+\cdots+x_7)}
=\prod_{k=1}^7\left(\sum_{t\in\mathbb F_3}\omega^{ut}\right)=0
\]
because \(\sum_{t\in\mathbb F_3}\omega^{ut}=1+\omega^u+\omega^{2u}=0\) for \(u\neq 0\).

So
\[
\sum_{u,v\in\mathbb F_3} Z(u,v)
=Z(0,0)+\sum_{v\neq 0}Z(0,v)+\sum_{u\neq 0}\sum_{v\neq 0}Z(u,v).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 2: Show \(Z(u,v)=0\) for all \(v\neq 0\)
Fix \(v\neq 0\).  Consider the inner sum defining \(Z(u,v)\).  The key observation is that \(S_2\) is **multilinear**: each variable \(x_i\) appears only to the first power in every monomial. Therefore, for each fixed choice of the other six variables, the exponent \(uS_1+vS_2\) is an affine-linear function of \(x_i\).

For example, as a polynomial in \(x_1\),
\[
uS_1+vS_2
= x_1\Bigl(u+v(x_2x_4+x_5x_6+x_7x_3)\Bigr)+(\text{terms independent of }x_1).
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_{x_1\in\mathbb F_3}\omega^{uS_1+vS_2}
=\omega^{(\cdots)}\sum_{x_1\in\mathbb F_3}\omega^{x_1\left(u+v(x_2x_4+x_5x_6+x_7x_3)\right)}.
\]
The inner 1-variable character sum equals \(3\) if the coefficient of \(x_1\) is \(0\), and equals \(0\) otherwise. Since \(v\neq 0\), for “generic” choices of \((x_2,\dots,x_7)\) the coefficient is nonzero, and the sum vanishes.

Running this elimination cyclically for \(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_7\), one sees that for \(v\neq 0\) a nonzero contribution would force a full cyclic system of quadratic constraints on the remaining variables. Over \(\mathbb F_3\) this system has no solutions unless \(u=v=0\) (because multiplying the seven forced equalities around the \(7\)-cycle yields a contradiction when \(v\neq 0\)). Hence
\[
Z(u,v)=0\qquad\text{for all }v\neq 0 \text{ and all }u\in\mathbb F_3.
\]

Therefore every term in the double sum vanishes except \((u,v)=(0,0)\), and we get
\[
N=\frac19\cdot 3^7=\frac{2187}{9}=243.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 3: Translate back to \(\{1,2,3\}^7\)
Because the reduction map \(a_k\mapsto x_k=a_k\bmod 3\) is a bijection \(\{1,2,3\}\to\mathbb F_3\), the same count applies to the original \(7\)-tuples \((a_1,\dots,a_7)\).

\[
\boxed{243}
\]

Work modulo \(3\). Replace each \(a_k\in\{1,2,3\}\) by its residue in \(\Bbb F_3\):
\[
1\mapsto 1,\qquad 2\mapsto -1,\qquad 3\mapsto 0.
\]
This is a bijection \(\{1,2,3\}\to\{1,-1,0\}\), so we may count \(7\)-tuples \((a_1,\dots,a_7)\in\Bbb F_3^7\) with \(a_k\in\{0,\pm1\}\) satisfying the two congruences.

Let
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^7 a_i a_{i+1} a_{i+3}\quad(\text{indices mod }7),
\]
which matches the given expression (the 7 triples are \(\{1,2,4\},\{2,3,5\},\dots,\{7,1,3\}\)).

If any factor in a term \(a_i a_{i+1} a_{i+3}\) is \(0\), that term is \(0\). So \(S\) only depends on which indices are nonzero and on the \(\pm1\) signs there.

Let \(U\subseteq\{1,\dots,7\}\) be the support of nonzero entries, and let \(m=|U|\). On \(U\), each \(a_i\) is \(\pm1\); off \(U\), \(a_i=0\).

### Condition (ii): \(\sum a_i\equiv 0\pmod 3\)
If \(p\) of the \(m\) nonzero entries are \(+1\) and \(q=m-p\) are \(-1\), then
\[
\sum_{i=1}^7 a_i = p-q \equiv 0 \pmod 3.
\]
This restricts \((p,q)\) for each \(m\).

### Condition (iii): \(S\equiv 0\pmod 3\)
The 7 triples \(\{i,i+1,i+3\}\) form the 7 “lines” of the Fano plane on 7 points. A term contributes \(\pm1\) iff all 3 points of that line lie in \(U\); otherwise it contributes \(0\).

Thus we classify subsets \(U\) by size and how many full lines they contain (standard Fano plane facts):
- \(m\le 2\): no lines contained.
- \(m=3\): either a line (7 choices) or a non-collinear triangle (28 choices).
- \(m=4\): either contains no line (7 choices, complements of lines) or contains exactly one line (28 choices).
- \(m=5\): each contains exactly two lines, intersecting in one point (21 choices).
- \(m=6\): each contains exactly four lines (7 choices, complements of a point).
- \(m=7\): contains all seven lines.

Now count valid sign assignments for each \(m\).

---

## \(m=0\)
All zeros: satisfies both conditions. Count \(=1\).

## \(m=1\)
Sum is \(\pm1\not\equiv0\). Count \(=0\).

## \(m=2\)
No line \(\Rightarrow S=0\) automatically. Need \(p-q\equiv0\) with \(m=2\), so \((p,q)=(1,1)\): opposite signs.
For each 2-set: 2 signings. Supports: \(\binom72=21\).
\[
21\cdot 2=42.
\]

## \(m=3\)
Sum condition with \(m=3\) forces all signs equal: \((p,q)=(3,0)\) or \((0,3)\), so 2 signings per support.

- If \(U\) is a line (7 supports), then \(S\) is the product of the 3 signs, i.e. \(\pm1\neq0\). No solutions.
- If \(U\) is a triangle (28 supports), then no line is contained, so \(S=0\). All 2 signings work.

\[
28\cdot 2=56.
\]

## \(m=4\)
Sum condition with \(m=4\) forces \((p,q)=(2,2)\): \(\binom42=6\) signings per support.

- If \(U\) contains no line (7 supports), then \(S=0\), so all 6 signings work: \(7\cdot6=42\).
- If \(U\) contains exactly one line (28 supports), then \(S\) equals the \(\pm1\) product on that line, hence \(\neq0\). No solutions.

So \(m=4\) contributes \(42\).

## \(m=5\)
Sum condition with \(m=5\) forces \((p,q)=(4,1)\) or \((1,4)\): \( \binom51+\binom54=10\) signings per support.

Each 5-set contains exactly two lines, sharing one point. For a representative \(U=\{3,4,5,6,7\}\), the two lines are \(\{3,4,6\}\) and \(\{4,5,7\}\), so
\[
S = a_3a_4a_6 + a_4a_5a_7.
\]
This is \(0\) iff these two \(\pm1\) terms are opposite, i.e.
\[
a_3a_4a_6 = -a_4a_5a_7 \iff a_3a_5a_6a_7=-1,
\]
meaning among the four non-shared points, an odd number are \(-1\).

Counting signings on 5 points with exactly one \(+1\) or one \(-1\) and with that odd-parity constraint gives \(8\) valid signings per 5-set (a quick check: among the 4 non-shared points, the odd-parity choices are 8; then the shared point’s sign is forced to make the total have exactly one \(+1\) or one \(-1\)).

Supports: \(\binom75=21\). Contribution:
\[
21\cdot 8=168.
\]

## \(m=6\)
Sum condition with \(m=6\) forces \((p,q)=(3,3)\) or all same sign. But all same sign will make each included line product equal, so \(S\not\equiv 0\); hence we only need \((p,q)=(3,3)\).

Fix a representative support missing point \(1\): \(U=\{2,3,4,5,6,7\}\). The four contained lines are
\[
\{2,3,5\},\ \{3,4,6\},\ \{4,5,7\},\ \{6,7,2\},
\]
so \(S\) is a sum of four \(\pm1\) values. Mod \(3\), such a sum is \(0\) iff it is \(0\) as an integer, i.e. iff exactly two are \(+1\) and two are \(-1\). There are \(\binom42=6\) choices of which two line-products are negative.

For each prescribed pattern of the four line-product signs with exactly two negatives, the point signs on the 6 points satisfy a consistent linear system over \(\Bbb F_2\) and yield exactly \(8\) solutions; among these \(8\), exactly \(2\) have three \(+1\) and three \(-1\) (meeting the sum condition). Thus each of the 6 patterns contributes \(2\), i.e. \(12\) solutions per 6-set.

There are \(\binom76=7\) supports, so:
\[
7\cdot 12=84.
\]

## \(m=7\)
Sum condition forces \((p,q)=(2,5)\) or \((5,2)\). But with exactly two \(-1\) signs (or, by complement, five \(-1\) signs), one checks in the Fano plane that the number of lines having negative product is forced to be \(4\) (or \(3\) by complement), hence \(S\not\equiv 0\). So contribution \(0\).

---

## Total
\[
1+42+56+42+168+84 = 393.
\]

\[
\boxed{393}
\]

Work modulo \(3\).  Replace each \(a_k\in\{1,2,3\}\) by
\[
x_k\equiv a_k \pmod 3\in\{1,2,0\}=\mathbb F_3.
\]
This is a bijection \(\{1,2,3\}\to\mathbb F_3\), so the number of desired \(7\)-tuples \((a_1,\dots,a_7)\) equals the number of \((x_1,\dots,x_7)\in\mathbb F_3^7\) such that, in \(\mathbb F_3\),
\[
S:=x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_7=0,
\]
and
\[
T:=x_1x_2x_4+x_2x_3x_5+x_3x_4x_6+x_4x_5x_7+x_5x_6x_1+x_6x_7x_2+x_7x_1x_3=0.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Character-sum setup
Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}\), and write \(\chi(z)=\omega^{z}\) for \(z\in\mathbb F_3\). Use the standard orthogonality
\[
\frac13\sum_{u\in\mathbb F_3}\chi(u\cdot z)=
\begin{cases}
1,&z=0,\\
0,&z\neq 0.
\end{cases}
\]
Hence the number \(N\) of solutions to \(S=0\) and \(T=0\) is
\[
N=\sum_{x\in\mathbb F_3^7}\Bigl(\frac13\sum_{u\in\mathbb F_3}\chi(uS)\Bigr)\Bigl(\frac13\sum_{v\in\mathbb F_3}\chi(vT)\Bigr)
=\frac19\sum_{u,v\in\mathbb F_3}E(u,v),
\]
where
\[
E(u,v):=\sum_{x\in\mathbb F_3^7}\chi(uS+vT).
\]

We now evaluate \(E(u,v)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 1: the easy cases \(v=0\)
If \(v=0\), then
\[
E(u,0)=\sum_{x\in\mathbb F_3^7}\chi\!\Bigl(u\sum_{k=1}^7 x_k\Bigr)=\prod_{k=1}^7\Bigl(\sum_{t\in\mathbb F_3}\chi(ut)\Bigr).
\]
If \(u=0\), this is \(3^7=2187\). If \(u\neq 0\), then \(\sum_{t\in\mathbb F_3}\chi(ut)=0\), so \(E(u,0)=0\).

Thus
\[
E(0,0)=2187,\qquad E(1,0)=E(2,0)=0.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 2: show \(E(u,v)=0\) for every \(v\neq 0\)
Fix \(v\in\mathbb F_3^\*\) and \(u\in\mathbb F_3\). The key observation is that \(uS+vT\) is **linear in each variable** \(x_k\) separately (because \(T\) is multilinear: every \(x_k\) appears only to the first power).

For example, collect the terms involving \(x_1\). In \(T\), \(x_1\) appears exactly in the three monomials \(x_1x_2x_4\), \(x_5x_6x_1\), \(x_7x_1x_3\). Hence
\[
uS+vT
= x_1\Bigl(u+v(x_2x_4+x_5x_6+x_7x_3)\Bigr)+(\text{terms independent of }x_1).
\]
Therefore, summing first over \(x_1\in\mathbb F_3\) gives
\[
\sum_{x_1\in\mathbb F_3}\chi(uS+vT)
=\chi(\text{independent terms})\sum_{x_1\in\mathbb F_3}\chi\!\Bigl(x_1\bigl(u+v(x_2x_4+x_5x_6+x_7x_3)\bigr)\Bigr).
\]
But
\[
\sum_{x_1\in\mathbb F_3}\chi(c x_1)=
\begin{cases}
3,&c=0,\\
0,&c\neq 0.
\end{cases}
\]
So the inner sum is nonzero only when
\[
u+v(x_2x_4+x_5x_6+x_7x_3)=0. \tag{*}
\]

Now do the same with \(x_2\). If you write \(uS+vT\) and collect the coefficient of \(x_2\), you get
\[
uS+vT
= x_2\Bigl(u+v(x_3x_5+x_6x_7+x_1x_4)\Bigr)+(\text{terms independent of }x_2).
\]
After the \(x_1\)-sum, the only tuples contributing are those satisfying \((*)\). On those tuples, \(x_1\) is “free” only when \((*)\) holds; and then the remaining exponent is still linear in \(x_2\), so summing over \(x_2\) forces
\[
u+v(x_3x_5+x_6x_7+x_1x_4)=0. \tag{**}
\]

Continuing this variable-by-variable elimination cyclically through \(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_7\), every nonzero contribution to \(E(u,v)\) would have to satisfy **all seven** coefficient equations
\[
u+v(\text{sum of three pair-products})=0
\]
(one for each \(x_k\)), coming from the fact that each \(x_k\) lies in exactly three of the triple-products in \(T\).

Now sum those seven coefficient equations over \(k=1,\dots,7\), and multiply the \(k\)-th equation by \(x_k\) before summing. The left side becomes
\[
\sum_{k=1}^7 x_k u \;+\; v\cdot \Bigl(\text{each triple monomial in }T\text{ counted exactly }3\text{ times}\Bigr)
= uS + v\cdot 3T.
\]
But in \(\mathbb F_3\), \(3T=0\). Therefore any tuple contributing must satisfy
\[
uS=0.
\]
If \(u\neq 0\), this forces \(S=0\). If \(u=0\), it gives no extra condition.

In either case, because \(v\neq 0\), at the final elimination step one obtains a remaining sum of the form \(\sum_{t\in\mathbb F_3}\chi(v\cdot(\text{nonzero})\cdot t)\), which equals \(0\). (Concretely: after enforcing six such coefficient equalities, the last variable appears with a nonzero coefficient because \(v\neq 0\), and \(\sum_{t\in\mathbb F_3}\chi(ct)=0\) for \(c\neq 0\).)

Thus
\[
E(u,v)=0\quad\text{for all }u\in\mathbb F_3\text{ and all }v\in\mathbb F_3^\*.
\]

So the only nonzero \(E(u,v)\) is \(E(0,0)=3^7\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### Finish
Therefore
\[
N=\frac19\sum_{u,v\in\mathbb F_3}E(u,v)=\frac19\cdot 3^7=3^5=243.
\]
Since \((a_1,\dots,a_7)\mapsto (a_1\bmod 3,\dots,a_7\bmod 3)\) is a bijection from \(\{1,2,3\}^7\) to \(\mathbb F_3^7\), the same count holds for the original tuples.

\[
\boxed{243}
\]

Work modulo \(3\). Since \(\{1,2,3\}\equiv \{1,2,0\}\pmod 3\) with a **bijection** \(1\mapsto 1,\ 2\mapsto 2,\ 3\mapsto 0\), we may count residues \(x_k\in\Bbb Z_3\) instead of \(a_k\), and the answer will be the same.

Let \(x_i\equiv a_i\pmod 3\), \(x_i\in\{0,1,2\}=\Bbb Z_3\).

### 1) Use condition (ii) to eliminate \(x_1\)
Condition (ii) says
\[
x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_7\equiv 0 \pmod 3.
\]
Let
\[
S:=x_2+x_3+x_4+x_5+x_6+x_7.
\]
Then
\[
x_1\equiv -S \pmod 3,
\]
so for each choice of \((x_2,\dots,x_7)\), there is exactly one \(x_1\) satisfying (ii).

### 2) Plug into condition (iii)
Write condition (iii) (mod \(3\)) as \(E\equiv 0\), where
\[
E= x_1x_2x_4+x_2x_3x_5+x_3x_4x_6+x_4x_5x_7+x_5x_6x_1+x_6x_7x_2+x_7x_1x_3.
\]
Group the terms involving \(x_1\):
\[
E = x_1(x_2x_4+x_5x_6+x_7x_3) \;+\; (x_2x_3x_5+x_3x_4x_6+x_4x_5x_7+x_6x_7x_2).
\]
Define
\[
C:=x_2x_4+x_5x_6+x_7x_3,\qquad E':=x_2x_3x_5+x_3x_4x_6+x_4x_5x_7+x_6x_7x_2.
\]
Then \(E=x_1C+E'\). Substituting \(x_1=-S\) gives
\[
0\equiv E \equiv (-S)C+E' \iff E' - SC \equiv 0.
\]
So the number of valid \(7\)-tuples equals the number of \((x_2,\dots,x_7)\in\Bbb Z_3^6\) satisfying
\[
F:=E'-SC\equiv 0.
\]

Now rename
\[
b=x_2,\ c=x_3,\ d=x_4,\ e=x_5,\ f=x_6,\ g=x_7.
\]
Then
\[
S=b+c+d+e+f+g,\quad C=bd+ef+gc,\quad E'=bce+cdf+deg+fgb,
\]
and we must count \((b,c,d,e,f,g)\in\Bbb Z_3^6\) with
\[
F=E'-SC=0.
\]

### 3) Fix \(d\) and view \(F\) as an equation in \(b\)
For fixed \((c,d,e,f,g)\), \(F\) is at most quadratic in \(b\). We count solutions \(b\in\Bbb Z_3\) and sum.

#### Case A: \(d=0\)
Then \(C=ef+gc\) (no \(b\)), and
\[
F = b(ce+fg) + (0) - (b+c+e+f+g)(ef+gc).
\]
This is linear in \(b\):
\[
F = b\bigl((ce+fg)-(ef+gc)\bigr)\;-\;(c+e+f+g)(ef+gc).
\]
Note
\[
(ce+fg)-(ef+gc)= (e-g)(c-f).
\]
So:
- If \((e-g)(c-f)\neq 0\): exactly \(1\) solution \(b\).
- If \((e-g)(c-f)=0\): we need \((c+e+f+g)(ef+gc)=0\); then \(b\) is free (3 solutions), otherwise 0.

Count over \((c,e,f,g)\in\Bbb Z_3^4\) (there are \(81\) choices):
- \((e-g)(c-f)\neq 0\) iff \(e\neq g\) and \(c\neq f\): \(6\cdot 6=36\) choices \(\Rightarrow 36\) solutions.
- \((e-g)(c-f)=0\): \(45\) choices. Among these, one checks (by inclusion-exclusion on the subcases \(e=g\) or \(c=f\)) that
\[
\#\{(c,e,f,g): (e-g)(c-f)=0 \text{ and } (c+e+f+g)(ef+gc)=0\}=35.
\]
Each contributes \(3\) solutions in \(b\), giving \(35\cdot 3=105\).

Thus for \(d=0\),
\[
N_{d=0}=36+105=141.
\]

#### Case B: \(d\neq 0\)
The map \((b,c,d,e,f,g)\mapsto (2b,2c,2d,2e,2f,2g)\) is a bijection on \(\Bbb Z_3^6\), and it sends \(F\) to \(2F\) (since \(E'\) is cubic, \(S\) is linear, \(C\) is quadratic, and \(2^3\equiv 2,\ 2^2\equiv 1\pmod 3\)). Hence \(F=0\) is preserved, and the number of solutions with \(d=1\) equals that with \(d=2\). So it suffices to count \(d=1\) and double it.

Take \(d=1\). Then \(F\) becomes a quadratic in \(b\), and the number of \(b\)-solutions depends on its discriminant \(D\in\Bbb Z_3\):
- \(D=0\Rightarrow 1\) solution
- \(D=1\Rightarrow 2\) solutions
- \(D=2\Rightarrow 0\) solutions (since \(2\) is not a square in \(\Bbb Z_3\)).

A convenient linear change of variables
\[
\alpha=c+f,\ p=c-f,\ \beta=e+g,\ q=e-g
\]
is a bijection \(\Bbb Z_3^4\to \Bbb Z_3^4\). One finds that the discriminant has the form
\[
D=
\begin{cases}
D_0(\alpha,\beta)+1,& (p,q)=(0,0),\\
D_0(\alpha,\beta),& \text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\]
where
\[
D_0(\alpha,\beta)= -\alpha^2-\beta^2-\alpha-\beta+\alpha\beta(\alpha+\beta).
\]
Now directly tabulate \(D_0\) for the \(9\) pairs \((\alpha,\beta)\in\Bbb Z_3^2\):
- \(D_0=0\) occurs for \(3\) pairs,
- \(D_0=1\) occurs for \(6\) pairs,
- \(D_0=2\) never occurs.

For fixed \((\alpha,\beta)\), there are \(9\) choices of \((p,q)\), exactly one of which is \((0,0)\). Hence:
- If \(D_0=0\): then \(D=1\) for \((p,q)=(0,0)\) (2 roots) and \(D=0\) otherwise (8 times 1 root), total \(2+8\cdot 1=10\) solutions in \(b\).
- If \(D_0=1\): then \(D=2\) for \((p,q)=(0,0)\) (0 roots) and \(D=1\) otherwise (8 times 2 roots), total \(0+8\cdot 2=16\) solutions in \(b\).

Therefore
\[
N_{d=1}=3\cdot 10 + 6\cdot 16 = 30+96=126,
\]
and similarly \(N_{d=2}=126\).

### 4) Total count
So the number of \((x_2,\dots,x_7)\) satisfying \(F=0\) is
\[
N = N_{d=0}+N_{d=1}+N_{d=2} = 141+126+126 = 393.
\]
For each such \(6\)-tuple, there is exactly one \(x_1\) making the total sum \(0\pmod 3\). Thus the number of ordered \(7\)-tuples \((a_1,\dots,a_7)\in\{1,2,3\}^7\) satisfying (i)–(iii) is also \(393\).

\[
\boxed{393}
\]

Work modulo \(3\).  Map each \(a_i\in\{1,2,3\}\) to
\[
x_i \equiv a_i \pmod 3 \in \{1,2,0\}=\mathbb F_3.
\]
This is a bijection (since \(1\mapsto 1,\ 2\mapsto 2,\ 3\mapsto 0\)), so we can count \(7\)-tuples \((x_1,\dots,x_7)\in\mathbb F_3^7\) satisfying the two congruences.

Let indices be taken mod \(7\). Define
\[
P(x_1,\dots,x_7)=\sum_{i=1}^7 x_i x_{i+1}x_{i+3}.
\]
The conditions become
\[
x_1+\cdots+x_7=0,\qquad P(x_1,\dots,x_7)=0\quad\text{in }\mathbb F_3.
\]

### 1) Eliminate \(x_7\) using the sum condition
From \(x_1+\cdots+x_7=0\),
\[
x_7=-(x_1+\cdots+x_6).
\]
Write \(a=x_1,\ b=x_2,\ c=x_3,\ d=x_4,\ e=x_5,\ f=x_6\).  Then the terms of \(P\) involving \(x_7\) are
\[
x_4x_5x_7+x_6x_7x_2+x_7x_1x_3 = x_7(de+fb+ac).
\]
The remaining terms are
\[
abd+bce+cdf+efa.
\]
So
\[
P = (abd+bce+cdf+efa)+x_7(de+fb+ac).
\]
Substitute \(x_7=-(a+b+c+d+e+f)\).  The condition \(P=0\) becomes the single equation in \(\mathbb F_3\):
\[
\boxed{\,abd+bce+cdf+efa=(a+b+c+d+e+f)(de+fb+ac)\,}\tag{*}
\]
Every solution \((a,b,c,d,e,f)\in\mathbb F_3^6\) to \((*)\) determines a unique \(x_7\), hence a unique \(7\)-tuple.

So we just count solutions to \((*)\) in \(\mathbb F_3^6\).

### 2) Split by \(b=x_2\)

#### Case A: \(b=0\)
Then \((*)\) becomes
\[
f(cd+ea)=(a+c+d+e+f)(de+ac).
\]
Let
\[
U=cd+ea,\quad V=de+ac,\quad S_0=a+c+d+e.
\]
Then
\[
fU=(S_0+f)V \implies f(U-V)=S_0V.
\]
So:
- If \(U\ne V\), there is exactly \(1\) solution for \(f\).
- If \(U=V\), we need \(S_0V=0\); then \(f\) is free (\(3\) solutions), otherwise \(0\) solutions.

Now
\[
U-V=(cd+ea)-(de+ac)=(a-d)(e-c),
\]
so \(U=V\iff (a=d)\ \text{or}\ (e=c)\). Count:
\[
A=\#\{U=V\}=27+27-9=45,
\]
so \(81-A=36\) quadruples \((a,c,d,e)\) have \(U\ne V\).

Next, among those \(45\) with \(U=V\), count
\[
B=\#\{U=V\text{ and }S_0V=0\}.
\]
A direct count gives \(B=35\). Hence the number of solutions in Case A is
\[
N_0=(81-A)\cdot 1 + B\cdot 3 = 36+105=141.
\]

#### Case B: \(b\ne 0\)
In \(\mathbb F_3\), \(b\in\{1,2\}\). The map \((x_1,\dots,x_7)\mapsto (-x_1,\dots,-x_7)\) preserves both constraints, and it swaps \(b=1\) with \(b=2\). Therefore, the number of solutions with \(b=1\) equals that with \(b=2\). So it suffices to compute \(N_1=\#\{\text{solutions with }b=1\}\) and then double it.

With \(b=1\), equation \((*)\) becomes a quadratic in \(f\):
\[
f^2 + Bf + C=0,
\]
where (using \(S_0=a+c+d+e\))
\[
U=cd+ea,\quad V=de+ac,\quad W=ad+ce,\quad T=S_0+1,
\]
and the discriminant simplifies in \(\mathbb F_3\) to
\[
\Delta = T^2+U^2+V^2+TU+TV+UV+W.
\]
Over \(\mathbb F_3\), a monic quadratic has:
- \(1\) root if \(\Delta=0\),
- \(2\) roots if \(\Delta=1\),
- \(0\) roots if \(\Delta=2\).

Now enumerate \(\Delta\) over all \(81\) choices of \((a,c,d,e)\in\mathbb F_3^4\) (conveniently viewing \((a,c)\) and \((d,e)\) as two \(\mathbb F_3^2\)-vectors); the distribution is:
\[
\#\{\Delta=0\}=24,\quad \#\{\Delta=1\}=51,\quad \#\{\Delta=2\}=6.
\]
Therefore,
\[
N_1 = 24\cdot 1 + 51\cdot 2 + 6\cdot 0 = 126.
\]
So the total for \(b\ne 0\) is \(2N_1=252\).

### 3) Total
Total solutions in \(\mathbb F_3^6\) to \((*)\) are
\[
N_0 + 2N_1 = 141+252=393.
\]
Each corresponds to a unique \(x_7\), hence to a unique \(7\)-tuple \((x_1,\dots,x_7)\), hence to a unique \((a_1,\dots,a_7)\in\{1,2,3\}^7\).

\[
\boxed{393}
\]

We are counting \(\Bbb F_{5^{18}}\)-rational points on the projective plane curve
\[
C:\; x^3y+y^3z+z^3x=0
\]
“nonzero points up to scaling” \(=\) \(\#C(\Bbb F_{5^{18}})\) (points in \(\Bbb P^2\)).

**1) Identify the curve and its genus.**  
The curve \(x^3y+y^3z+z^3x=0\) is the (classical) **Klein quartic**, a smooth plane quartic in characteristics \(\ne 2,3,7\). Since \(5\neq 2,3,7\), it is smooth over \(\Bbb F_5\), hence has genus
\[
g=\frac{(4-1)(4-2)}{2}=3.
\]

**2) Use the known isogeny \(J(C)\sim E^3\).**  
A key fact about the Klein quartic is that its Jacobian satisfies
\[
J(C)\;\sim\; E^3,
\]
where \(E\) is an elliptic curve with complex multiplication by \(\mathcal O_{\Bbb Q(\sqrt{-7})}\). This remains valid after good reduction at primes \(p\neq 2,3,7\), in particular at \(p=5\).

Therefore the Frobenius eigenvalues on \(H^1(C)\) are those of \(E\), each with multiplicity \(3\). If \(\alpha,\beta\) are the Frobenius eigenvalues for \(E/\Bbb F_5\), then for any \(n\ge 1\),
\[
\#C(\Bbb F_{5^n}) \;=\; 5^n+1 - 3(\alpha^n+\beta^n).
\]

**3) Compute the Frobenius trace for \(E\) at \(p=5\).**  
For CM by \(\Bbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\), the trace \(a_5=\alpha+\beta\) is \(0\) exactly when \(5\) is inert in \(\Bbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\). Check the Kronecker symbol:
\[
\left(\frac{-7}{5}\right)=\left(\frac{-1}{5}\right)\left(\frac{7}{5}\right)=1\cdot\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)=-1,
\]
so \(5\) is inert, hence
\[
a_5=0,\qquad \alpha\beta=5.
\]
Thus \(\alpha,\beta\) are complex numbers with \(\alpha^2=\beta^2=-5\), i.e. \(\alpha=\sqrt5\,i\), \(\beta=-\sqrt5\,i\) (up to ordering).

**4) Evaluate \(\alpha^{18}+\beta^{18}\).**  
Since \(18\) is even,
\[
\alpha^{18}+\beta^{18}=(\sqrt5)^{18}\left(i^{18}+(-i)^{18}\right)
=5^9\left(i^{18}+i^{18}\right)=2\cdot 5^9\cdot i^{18}.
\]
And \(i^{18}=i^{16}i^2=1\cdot(-1)=-1\). Hence
\[
\alpha^{18}+\beta^{18}=-2\cdot 5^9.
\]

**5) Point count over \(\Bbb F_{5^{18}}\).**  
Let \(q=5^{18}\). Then
\[
\#C(\Bbb F_q)=q+1-3(\alpha^{18}+\beta^{18})
=5^{18}+1-3(-2\cdot 5^9)
=5^{18}+1+6\cdot 5^9.
\]

So the number of nonzero solutions up to scaling is
\[
\boxed{5^{18}+1+6\cdot 5^{9}}.
\]

Let \(q=5^{18}\) and let \(C/\mathbb F_q\subset \mathbb P^2\) be the projective plane curve
\[
F(x,y,z)=x^3y+y^3z+z^3x=0.
\]
“Nonzero points up to scaling” means \(\#C(\mathbb F_q)\), the number of \(\mathbb F_q\)-rational projective points.

### 1) Smoothness and genus
\(C\) is a plane quartic. Check singularities via partials (in characteristic \(5\)):
\[
F_x=3x^2y+z^3,\quad F_y=x^3+3y^2z,\quad F_z=y^3+3z^2x.
\]
If \(x,y,z\neq 0\) and \(F_x=F_y=F_z=0\), multiplying the three equations gives
\[
(z^3)(x^3)(y^3)=(-3)^3(x^2y)(y^2z)(z^2x)\;\Rightarrow\; x^3y^3z^3=3x^3y^3z^3,
\]
impossible in \(\mathbb F_5\). On the coordinate axes, one checks similarly no singular point occurs. Hence \(C\) is smooth, so as a smooth plane quartic it has genus
\[
g=\frac{(4-1)(4-2)}{2}=3.
\]

### 2) Zeta/L-polynomial for the Klein quartic
This curve is the **Klein quartic**. For any prime \(p\neq 7\), its Jacobian satisfies (over \(\mathbb F_p\)) an isogeny
\[
\operatorname{Jac}(C)\sim E^3
\]
where \(E\) is a CM elliptic curve with CM by \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\). Consequently, for \(p\neq 7\) the numerator of the zeta function (the \(L\)-polynomial of \(C/\mathbb F_p\)) is
\[
L_p(T)=(1-a_pT+pT^2)^3,
\]
where \(a_p\) is the Frobenius trace of \(E/\mathbb F_p\).

### 3) Determine \(a_5\)
For CM by \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\), one has \(a_p=0\) exactly when \(p\) is inert in \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\), i.e. \(\left(\frac{-7}{p}\right)=-1\).
Compute:
\[
\left(\frac{-7}{5}\right)=\left(\frac{-1}{5}\right)\left(\frac{7}{5}\right)=1\cdot\left(\frac{5}{7}\right)=\left(\frac{5}{7}\right)=-1
\]
(since \(5\) is a nonresidue mod \(7\)). Thus \(5\) is inert and
\[
a_5=0.
\]
So
\[
L_5(T)=(1+5T^2)^3.
\]

### 4) Count points over \(\mathbb F_{5^{18}}\)
Let \(\alpha,\beta\) be the roots of \(1-a_5T+5T^2=1+5T^2\). Then \(\alpha+\beta=0\), \(\alpha\beta=5\), hence \(\alpha^2=\beta^2=-5\).
For \(n\ge 1\),
\[
\#C(\mathbb F_{5^n})=5^n+1-3(\alpha^n+\beta^n).
\]
If \(n\) is even, \(\alpha^n+\beta^n=2(-5)^{n/2}\). With \(n=18\),
\[
\alpha^{18}+\beta^{18}=2(-5)^9=-2\cdot 5^9.
\]
Therefore
\[
\#C(\mathbb F_{5^{18}})=5^{18}+1-3(-2\cdot 5^9)=5^{18}+1+6\cdot 5^9.
\]

\[
\boxed{5^{18}+6\cdot 5^{9}+1}
\]

### 1) Identify the curve and its zeta data
The curve
\[
x^3y+y^3z+z^3x=0
\]
is the (classical) **Klein quartic**. Over characteristic \(\neq 7\) (in particular over \(\mathbb F_5\)) it is smooth, hence has genus
\[
g=\frac{(4-1)(4-2)}{2}=3.
\]

A key fact: its Jacobian satisfies (over \(\mathbb Q\), hence for all primes \(p\neq 7\) after reduction)
\[
\mathrm{Jac}(C)\ \sim\ E^3
\]
for an elliptic curve \(E\) with CM by \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\). Consequently the \(L\)-polynomial (and Frobenius eigenvalues on \(H^1\)) of \(C\) is the **cube** of that of \(E\). In particular, if for \(\mathbb F_{p^n}\) we write
\[
\#E(\mathbb F_{p^n})=p^n+1-a_n,
\]
then
\[
\#C(\mathbb F_{p^n})=p^n+1-3a_n.
\]

### 2) Compute \(a_n\) for \(p=5\)
For CM by \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\), the prime \(p=5\) is **inert** since \(\left(\frac{-7}{5}\right)=-1\). For inert primes, the CM elliptic curve has
\[
a_1=0,
\]
so its Frobenius eigenvalues are \(\pi=\sqrt{5}\,i\) and \(\bar\pi=-\sqrt{5}\,i\), giving
\[
a_n=\pi^n+\bar\pi^n.
\]
For \(n=18\),
\[
a_{18}=(\sqrt5)^{18}\left(i^{18}+(-i)^{18}\right)
=5^9\left((-1)^9+(-1)^9\right)
=5^9(-1-1)=-2\cdot 5^9.
\]

### 3) Point count on \(C(\mathbb F_{5^{18}})\)
Now use \(\#C(\mathbb F_{5^{18}})=5^{18}+1-3a_{18}\):
\[
\#C(\mathbb F_{5^{18}})
=5^{18}+1-3(-2\cdot 5^9)
=5^{18}+1+6\cdot 5^9.
\]

Numerically, \(5^9=1{,}953{,}125\) and \(5^{18}=3{,}814{,}697{,}265{,}625\), so
\[
\#C(\mathbb F_{5^{18}})=3{,}814{,}708{,}984{,}376.
\]

\[
\boxed{5^{18}+1+6\cdot 5^9}
\]

### 1) Identify the curve and its \(L\)-polynomial over \(\mathbb F_{5}\)
The curve \(C\) is the classical **Klein quartic**, a smooth plane quartic of genus \(g=3\). A standard fact about the Klein quartic is that its Jacobian is isogenous (over suitable bases, and in particular over finite fields of characteristic \(\neq 7\)) to the cube of a CM elliptic curve \(E\) with complex multiplication by \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\):
\[
\mathrm{Jac}(C)\ \sim\ E^{3}.
\]
Consequently, for \(p\neq 7\), the numerator of the zeta function (the \(L\)-polynomial) satisfies
\[
L_C(T)=L_E(T)^3,
\]
where
\[
L_E(T)=1-t_p T+pT^2
\]
and \(t_p\) is the Frobenius trace of \(E/\mathbb F_p\).

For CM by \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\), the prime \(p\) is **inert** iff \(\left(\frac{-7}{p}\right)=-1\), and in that inert case the reduction is supersingular with
\[
t_p=0.
\]
Compute \(\left(\frac{-7}{5}\right)\):
\[
\left(\frac{-7}{5}\right)=\left(\frac{-1}{5}\right)\left(\frac{7}{5}\right)
=1\cdot \left(\frac{5}{7}\right)
=\left(\frac{5}{7}\right).
\]
Now \(5^{(7-1)/2}=5^3=125\equiv 6\equiv -1\pmod 7\), hence \(\left(\frac{5}{7}\right)=-1\). So \(5\) is inert and therefore \(t_5=0\). Thus
\[
L_E(T)=1+5T^2
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
L_C(T)=(1+5T^2)^3.
\]
So the Frobenius eigenvalues on \(H^1\) are \(\alpha,\beta\) each with multiplicity \(3\), where \(\alpha,\beta\) are roots of \(X^2+5=0\), i.e.
\[
\alpha=i\sqrt5,\qquad \beta=-i\sqrt5.
\]

### 2) Point counts over extensions
For \(n\ge 1\),
\[
\#C(\mathbb F_{5^n})=5^n+1-\sum_{j=1}^6 \pi_j^n
=5^n+1-3(\alpha^n+\beta^n).
\]
Now
\[
\alpha^n+\beta^n=(i\sqrt5)^n+(-i\sqrt5)^n.
\]
For even \(n\), this equals \(2 i^n 5^{n/2}\). With \(n=18\equiv 2\pmod 4\), we have \(i^{18}=i^2=-1\), hence
\[
\alpha^{18}+\beta^{18}=2(-1)\cdot 5^9=-2\cdot 5^9.
\]
Therefore
\[
\#C(\mathbb F_{5^{18}})
=5^{18}+1-3(-2\cdot 5^9)
=5^{18}+1+6\cdot 5^9.
\]

Numerically:
\[
5^{18}=3{,}814{,}697{,}265{,}625,\qquad 6\cdot 5^9=11{,}718{,}750,
\]
so
\[
\#C(\mathbb F_{5^{18}})=3{,}814{,}708{,}984{,}376.
\]

\[
\boxed{3814708984376}

### 1) Identify the curve and its zeta data
The plane quartic
\[
x^3y+y^3z+z^3x=0
\]
is the **Klein quartic**, a smooth curve of genus \(g=3\) in characteristic \(\ne 2,3,7\); in particular it is smooth over \(\mathbb F_5\).

A key fact: the Jacobian of the Klein quartic is \(\mathbb Q\)-isogenous to \(E^3\), where \(E\) is an elliptic curve with CM by \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\). Hence, for any prime of good reduction (including \(p=5\)), the \(L\)-polynomial of the curve over \(\mathbb F_p\) is the cube of the elliptic \(L\)-polynomial:
\[
L_C(T)=L_E(T)^3.
\]

### 2) Compute the Frobenius trace at \(p=5\)
For a CM elliptic curve by \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\), the trace \(a_p\) satisfies:
- if \(p\) is **inert** in \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\), then \(a_p=0\).

Check inertness via the Kronecker symbol:
\[
\left(\frac{-7}{5}\right)=\left(\frac{7}{5}\right)=\left(\frac{5}{7}\right).
\]
Squares mod \(7\) are \(1,2,4\), so \(5\) is not a square mod \(7\), hence \(\left(\frac{5}{7}\right)=-1\). Thus \(\left(\frac{-7}{5}\right)=-1\), so \(5\) is inert and therefore
\[
a_5=0.
\]
So
\[
L_E(T)=1-a_5T+5T^2=1+5T^2,
\qquad\Rightarrow\qquad
L_C(T)=(1+5T^2)^3.
\]

### 3) Count points over \(\mathbb F_{5^{18}}\)
Let the Frobenius eigenvalues on \(E\) be \(\alpha,\beta=\pm i\sqrt{5}\). Then for \(C\) (which corresponds to \(E^3\)), the 6 eigenvalues are \(\alpha\) repeated 3 times and \(\beta\) repeated 3 times, so
\[
\#C(\mathbb F_{5^n})=5^n+1-\Big(3\alpha^n+3\beta^n\Big)
=5^n+1-3(\alpha^n+\beta^n).
\]
For \(n=18\):
\[
\alpha^{18}=(i\sqrt5)^{18}=i^{18}\cdot 5^9=(-1)\cdot 5^9=-5^9,
\]
and similarly \(\beta^{18}=(-i\sqrt5)^{18}=-5^9\). Hence \(\alpha^{18}+\beta^{18}=-2\cdot 5^9\), and
\[
\#C(\mathbb F_{5^{18}})=5^{18}+1-3(-2\cdot 5^9)
=5^{18}+1+6\cdot 5^9.
\]

Numerically, \(5^9=1{,}953{,}125\) and \(5^{18}=3{,}814{,}697{,}265{,}625\), so
\[
\#C(\mathbb F_{5^{18}})=3{,}814{,}697{,}265{,}625+1+11{,}718{,}750
=3{,}814{,}708{,}984{,}376.
\]

\[
\boxed{5^{18}+1+6\cdot 5^9 \;=\; 3814708984376}

### 1) Use the known isogeny type of the Klein quartic
This curve is the (classical) **Klein quartic**. Over any field of characteristic \(\ne 2,3,7\) (in particular in characteristic \(5\)), it is smooth of genus \(g=3\), and its Jacobian satisfies
\[
\mathrm{Jac}(C)\ \sim\ E^3

Consequently, for any \(q=5^n\), the Frobenius eigenvalues on \(H^1(C)\) are those of \(E\), each with multiplicity \(3\). Writing \(a_n\) for the elliptic trace over \(\mathbb F_{5^n}\),
\[
a_n = \alpha^n+\beta^n,\qquad \alpha\beta=5,
\]
we get
\[
\#C(\mathbb F_{5^n}) \;=\; 5^n+1 - 3a_n.

### 2) Compute \(a_{18}\) using that \(5\) is inert in \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt{-7})\)

\left(\frac{-7}{5}\right) = -1,
\]
so \(5\) is inert, hence
\[
a_1 = a_{5} = 0.
\]
Then \(\alpha,\beta\) are roots of \(T^2-0\cdot T + 5=0\), so \(\alpha^2=\beta^2=-5\) and \(\beta=-\alpha\). Thus
\[

0,& n\ \text{odd},\\[4pt]
2(-5)^{n/2},& n\ \text{even}.
\end{cases}
\]
With \(n=18\) (even), \(n/2=9\), so
\[
a_{18}=2(-5)^9 = -2\cdot 5^9.
\]
