\mathcal H=\{H\subseteq X: |H|=1222\ \text{and}\ \exists C\in\mathcal C\text{ with }C\subseteq H\}.
\]
Then \(\mathcal H\) is a family of \(1222\)-subsets of \(X\), and it is still 4-wise intersecting: if \(H_i\supseteq C_i\) for \(i=1,2,3,4\), then
\[
H_1\cap H_2\cap H_3\cap H_4 \supseteq C_1\cap C_2\cap C_3\cap C_4\ne\varnothing.
\]

### Lemma (Frankl’s theorem, special case)
If \(\mathcal H\) is a 4-wise intersecting family of \(k\)-subsets of \([n]\) and \(n\ge \frac{4}{3}k\), then all sets in \(\mathcal H\) share a common element.

Here \(n=2022\), \(k=1222\), and indeed
\[
2022 \ge \frac{4}{3}\cdot 1222 \approx 1629.3,
\]
so the lemma implies: there exists \(p\in X\) such that \(p\in H\) for every \(H\in\mathcal H\).

We now show \(p\) must belong to every \(C\in\mathcal C\). If some \(C\in\mathcal C\) missed \(p\), then since \(|C|\le 1222\) we could enlarge \(C\) to a \(1222\)-set \(H\) still missing \(p\): choose
\[
H = C \cup S,\quad S\subseteq X\setminus (C\cup\{p\}),\quad |S|=1222-|C|.
\]
This is possible because \(|X\setminus (C\cup\{p\})|=2021-|C|\ge 1222-|C|\).
Then \(H\in\mathcal H\) but \(p\notin H\), contradicting that \(p\) lies in every member of \(\mathcal H\).

Thus \(p\in C\) for all \(C\in\mathcal C\), i.e. \(p\notin F\) for all \(F\in\mathcal F\). But this contradicts the condition that every element of \(X\) belongs to at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\).

So our assumption was false: there **must** exist \(4\) sets in \(\mathcal F\) whose union is \(X\). Hence
\[
m\le 4.
\]

Combined with \(m\ge 4\), we get \(m=4\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

\[
\boxed{4}
\]

Let \(n=|X|=2022\) and \(k=800\).

---

## 1) A construction showing \(m\ge 1222\)

Split
\[
X=P\sqcup Q,\qquad |P|=800,\ |Q|=1222.
\]
For each \(q\in Q\) and each \(p\in P\), define
\[
F_{q,p}:=(P\setminus\{p\})\cup\{q\}.
\]
Then \(|F_{q,p}|=799+1=800\), so every set has size \(\ge 800\), and all these sets are distinct.

- If \(q\in Q\), then \(q\in F_{q,p}\) for all \(p\in P\), so \(q\) belongs to exactly \(800\) sets.
- If \(r\in P\), then for each fixed \(q\in Q\), the element \(r\) is missing only from \(F_{q,r}\) and is present in the other \(799\) sets \(F_{q,p}\) with \(p\neq r\). Hence
  \[
  \deg(r)=|Q|\cdot 799 = 1222\cdot 799 \ge 800.
  \]

So the family satisfies all conditions.

But each \(F_{q,p}\) contains **exactly one** element of \(Q\). Therefore the union of \(t\) sets contains at most \(t\) elements of \(Q\), so to cover all \(1222\) elements of \(Q\) we need \(t\ge 1222\). Thus, in general,
\[
m\ge 1222.
\]

(And \(1222\) sets do suffice here: choose one set for each \(q\in Q\), making sure at least two chosen sets omit different \(p\)’s so that all of \(P\) is also covered.)

---

## 2) Proof that \(m\le 1222\) always

Take a subfamily \(\mathcal C\subseteq\mathcal F\) that covers \(X\) with the **minimum** number of sets, and write
\[
\mathcal C=\{C_1,\dots,C_t\},\qquad \bigcup_{i=1}^t C_i=X,
\]
so \(t\) is the minimum cover size.

For each \(i\), let \(U_i\) be the set of elements that appear **only** in \(C_i\) among the sets of \(\mathcal C\) (the “private” elements of \(C_i\)). By minimality, each \(U_i\neq\varnothing\).

Let \(N\subseteq X\) be the set of elements that occur in at least two sets of \(\mathcal C\). Then the elements not in \(N\) are exactly those that occur in exactly one set of \(\mathcal C\), so
\[
\sum_{i=1}^t |U_i| = |X\setminus N|=n-|N|=2022-|N|.
\]
Also, since \(C_i\) has size at least \(800\) and at most \(|N|\) of its elements can lie in \(N\), we have
\[
|U_i|\ \ge\ |C_i|-|N|\ \ge\ 800-|N|.
\]
Summing over \(i\) gives
\[
2022-|N|=\sum_{i=1}^t |U_i|\ \ge\ t(800-|N|).
\]

Now suppose for contradiction that \(t\ge 1223\). Then \(|N|\le 2022-1223=799\). If \(|N|\le 798\), then \(800-|N|\ge 2\), so
\[
2022-|N|\ \ge\ 2t\ \ge\ 2\cdot 1223=2446,
\]
impossible. Hence we must have \(|N|=799\). Plugging \(|N|=799\) into
\[
2022-|N|\ge t(800-|N|)
\]
yields
\[
1223\ge t\cdot 1,
\]
so with \(t\ge 1223\) we get \(t=1223\). In this equality case, every \(C_i\) must satisfy \(|U_i|=1\) and \(|C_i|=800\), and moreover \(C_i\) must contain all of \(N\). Thus each set in the cover has the form
\[
C_i = N\cup\{a_i\}
\]
where \(a_i\) is the unique private element of \(C_i\). The \(a_i\)’s are all distinct, and \(\{a_1,\dots,a_{1223}\}=X\setminus N\).

Now use the global condition on \(\mathcal F\): each element belongs to at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\). Fix \(a_1\). It belongs to at least \(800\) sets in \(\mathcal F\). One of them is \(C_1=N\cup\{a_1\}\), which has size \(800\). Any other set \(F\in\mathcal F\) containing \(a_1\) must be different from \(C_1\), so it cannot be contained in \(N\cup\{a_1\}\) (otherwise it would equal \(C_1\)). Therefore \(F\) must contain some element outside \(N\cup\{a_1\}\), i.e. it contains some other \(a_j\) with \(j\neq 1\). So there exists \(F\in\mathcal F\) with
\[
\{a_1,a_j\}\subseteq F
\]
for some \(j\neq 1\).

But then \((\mathcal C\setminus\{C_1,C_j\})\cup\{F\}\) is a cover of \(X\) using \(1222\) sets: all of \(N\) is still covered by the remaining \(C_i\)’s, and \(a_1,a_j\) are covered by \(F\). This contradicts minimality of \(t=1223\).

Hence \(t\le 1222\). So **every** such family \(\mathcal F\) contains \(1222\) sets whose union is \(X\), i.e.
\[
m\le 1222.
\]

---

## 3) Conclusion

We have shown \(m\ge 1222\) by construction and \(m\le 1222\) always. Therefore the smallest such \(m\) is
\[
\boxed{1222}.
\]

Let \(n=|X|=2022\).

We seek the smallest \(m\) such that **every** family \(\mathcal F\) satisfying

1. \(|F|\ge 800\) for all \(F\in\mathcal F\);
2. each \(x\in X\) lies in at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\);

must contain \(m\) sets whose union is \(X\).

Equivalently, let \(\tau(\mathcal F)\) be the minimum size of a subfamily covering \(X\). We want
\[
m=\max_{\mathcal F\text{ satisfying conditions}} \tau(\mathcal F).
\]

---

## 1) An upper bound: \(\tau(\mathcal F)\le 1222\)

Take a **minimum** cover subfamily
\[
\mathcal C=\{F_1,F_2,\dots,F_k\}\subseteq\mathcal F,\qquad \bigcup_{i=1}^k F_i=X,
\]
with \(k=\tau(\mathcal F)\).

Because \(\mathcal C\) is minimal, for each \(i\) there exists an element \(x_i\in F_i\) that is not covered by the other \(k-1\) sets:
\[
x_i\in F_i\setminus \bigcup_{j\ne i}F_j.
\]
These \(x_i\)’s are all distinct. Let \(W=\{x_1,\dots,x_k\}\).

Then each \(F_i\) contains \(x_i\) but contains none of the other witnesses \(x_j\) (\(j\ne i\)). Hence
\[
F_i \subseteq X\setminus (W\setminus\{x_i\}),
\]
so
\[
|F_i|\le n-(k-1)=2022-k+1=2023-k.
\]
But \(|F_i|\ge 800\), therefore
\[
800\le 2023-k\quad\Longrightarrow\quad k\le 1223.
\]

Now we show \(k\ne 1223\). Suppose for contradiction \(k=1223\). Then the above inequalities force equality everywhere, so for each \(i\),
\[
|F_i|=800\quad\text{and}\quad F_i = (X\setminus W)\cup\{x_i\}.
\]
In particular, \(|X\setminus W|=2022-1223=799\).

Fix a witness element \(x_1\). By the problem condition, \(x_1\) belongs to at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\). Take any set \(F\in\mathcal F\) with \(x_1\in F\). Since \(|F|\ge 800\) but there are only \(799\) elements outside \(W\), the set \(F\) cannot be contained in \((X\setminus W)\cup\{x_1\}\); it must contain some other witness \(x_j\) with \(j\ne 1\). Thus there exists \(F\in\mathcal F\) containing at least two witnesses, say \(x_1\) and \(x_j\).

But then the \(1222\) sets
\[
F,\; F_2,\;F_3,\;\dots,\;F_{j-1},\;F_{j+1},\;\dots,\;F_{1223}
\]
still cover \(X\): all witnesses except \(x_1,x_j\) are covered by their corresponding \(F_i\), and \(x_1,x_j\) are covered by \(F\); the remaining \(799\) non-witness elements are covered by every \(F_i\). This contradicts the minimality of \(k=1223\).

Therefore \(k\le 1222\). So **every** such family \(\mathcal F\) has a cover by at most \(1222\) sets:
\[
\tau(\mathcal F)\le 1222.
\]

---

## 2) A matching construction: \(\tau(\mathcal F)=1222\) is possible

Let
\[
B=\{1,2,\dots,800\},\qquad Y=\{801,802,\dots,2022\}\quad (|Y|=1222).
\]
Define \(\mathcal F\) to consist of all sets
\[
F_{y,b} := (B\setminus\{b\})\cup\{y\},
\quad\text{for each }y\in Y,\; b\in B.
\]
Then:

- Each \(F_{y,b}\) has size \(799+1=800\), so \(|F|\ge 800\).
- Fix \(y\in Y\). It belongs to exactly the \(800\) sets \(F_{y,b}\) as \(b\) varies, so \(y\) is in at least \(800\) sets.
- Fix \(b\in B\). For each \(y\in Y\), \(b\) is missing only from \(F_{y,b}\) and belongs to the other \(799\) sets \(F_{y,b'}\) with \(b'\ne b\). Hence \(b\) belongs to \(1222\cdot 799\) sets, certainly \(\ge 800\).

So \(\mathcal F\) satisfies the conditions.

Moreover, **each** set \(F_{y,b}\) contains exactly one element of \(Y\). Therefore, to cover all \(1222\) elements of \(Y\), we need at least \(1222\) sets:
\[
\tau(\mathcal F)\ge 1222.
\]
And \(1222\) sets do suffice: pick one set for each \(y\in Y\), choosing the omitted \(b\)’s so that not every \(b\in B\) is omitted by all chosen sets (e.g. for the first \(800\) values of \(y\), omit distinct \(b\)’s; then every \(b\) appears in \(1221\) of the chosen sets). Then the union is all of \(B\cup Y=X\).

Hence \(\tau(\mathcal F)=1222\) is attainable.

---

## 3) Conclusion

The maximum possible minimum cover size is \(1222\), so the smallest integer \(m\) that is **guaranteed** is

\[
\boxed{1222}.
\]

Let \(a_i=f(i)\) for \(1\le i\le 100\).

### 1) Consequence of \(f(f(i))=100\)
For every \(i\),
\[
a_{a_i}=100.
\]
In particular, if some value \(t\) ever appears among \(\{a_i\}\), i.e. \(a_k=t\) for some \(k\), then
\[
a_t=a_{a_k}=100.
\]
So **every value that appears forces its own index to map to \(100\)**.

Let
\[
m=\min_{1\le i\le 100} a_i,\quad \text{and pick }p\text{ with }a_p=m.
\]
Then \(a_m=100\) (because the value \(m\) appears).

### 2) A lower bound on \(m\)
We have \(a_p=m\) and \(a_m=100\). By the Lipschitz condition \(|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1\),
\[
|p-m|\ \ge\ |a_p-a_m|=100-m.
\]
Since \(a_m=100>m\), we must have \(p\ne m\), hence \(p<m\) and
\[
m-p\ge 100-m \quad\Longrightarrow\quad p\le 2m-100.
\]
But \(p\ge 1\), so \(2m-100\ge 1\), i.e.
\[
m\ge 51.
\]

### 3) Values \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) must all appear \(\Rightarrow\) a forced tail of 100’s
From index \(p\) to index \(m\), the sequence goes from value \(m\) to value \(100\) with step size \(\le 1\), so it must pass through every integer \(m,m+1,\dots,100\). Hence **all values in \([m,100]\)** appear.

Therefore for each \(t\in\{m,m+1,\dots,100\}\), we must have \(a_t=100\). In particular,
\[
a_m=a_{m+1}=\cdots=a_{100}=100.
\]

### 4) Minimal possible sum for a fixed \(m\)
For \(i<m\), since \(a_m=100\) and steps change by at most \(1\), the smallest possible \(a_i\) is obtained by decreasing as fast as allowed when moving left from \(m\), but never going below \(m\):
\[
a_i \ge \max\bigl(m,\ 100-(m-i)\bigr)=\max(m,\ i+100-m).
\]
This lower bound is simultaneously achievable by taking
- \(a_i=m\) for \(1\le i\le 2m-100\),
- then \(a_i\) increases by \(1\) each step up to \(a_m=100\),
- and \(a_i=100\) for \(i\ge m\).

Let \(n=100-m\) (so \(0\le n\le 49\)). Then the sum is forced to be at least
\[
S(m)=m^2+\frac{(100-m)(101-m)}{2}+100(100-m),
\]
which simplifies to
\[
S(m)=\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}.
\]

### 5) Minimize over integers \(m\in[51,100]\)
This is a convex quadratic; its vertex is at \(m=\frac{401}{6}\approx 66.83\), so check \(m=66,67\):
\[
S(66)=8351,\qquad S(67)=8350,\qquad S(68)=8352.
\]
Thus the minimum is \(S(67)=8350\).

### 6) Construction achieving \(8350\)
Define \(f\) by
\[
f(i)=
\begin{cases}
67, & 1\le i\le 34,\\
i+33, & 35\le i\le 66 \quad(\text{so }68,69,\dots,99),\\
100, & 67\le i\le 100.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(|f(i)-f(i+1)|\le 1\) holds, and since \(f(j)=100\) for all \(j\in\{67,68,\dots,100\}\), we have \(f(f(i))=100\) for all \(i\). The sum is
\[
34\cdot 67 + (68+69+\cdots+100) + 34\cdot 100
=2278+2772+3300=8350.
\]

\[
\boxed{8350}
\]

Let \(a_i=f(i)\) for \(1\le i\le 100\). The conditions become

1. \(|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1\) for \(1\le i\le 99\);
2. \(a_{a_i}=100\) for all \(i\).

### Step 1: \(a_{100}=100\)
Pick any \(i\). Let \(t=a_i\). Then condition (2) says \(a_t=100\).  
Now apply (2) to the index \(t\): \(a_{a_t}=a_{100}=100\). Hence
\[
a_{100}=100.
\]

### Step 2: If \(m=\min\{a_i\}\), then \(a_m=a_{m+1}=\cdots=a_{100}=100\)
Let \(m=\min_i a_i\), and choose \(p\) with \(a_p=m\). Consider the sequence
\[
a_p, a_{p+1},\dots,a_{100}.
\]
It starts at \(m\) and ends at \(a_{100}=100\), changing by at most \(1\) each step, so it must take **every integer value** \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) somewhere along the way.

Thus for each \(v\in\{m,m+1,\dots,100\}\), there exists some \(j\) with \(a_j=v\). Applying (2) to that \(j\) gives \(a_v=100\). Therefore,
\[
a_v=100\quad\text{for all }v=m,m+1,\dots,100,
\]
i.e. \(a_m=a_{m+1}=\cdots=a_{100}=100\).

In particular, \(|a_{m-1}-a_m|\le 1\) and \(a_m=100\) imply
\[
a_{m-1}\in\{99,100\}\ \Rightarrow\ a_{m-1}\ge 99.
\]

### Step 3: A lower bound for the prefix \(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1}\), and \(m\ge 51\)
Since \(m\) is the minimum, \(a_i\ge m\) for all \(i\). Also, from \(a_{m-1}\ge 99\) and \(|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1\), going left we get
\[
a_i \ge 99-(m-1-i)= i+(100-m)\qquad(1\le i\le m-1).
\]
Let \(k=100-m\). Then for \(1\le i\le m-1\),
\[
a_i\ \ge\ \max(100-k,\ i+k).
\]
For \(m\) to actually be the minimum value attained, we need \(a_1\ge 100-k\) but also \(a_1\) can equal \(100-k\); this requires
\[
100-k \ge 1+k \iff 100 \ge 2k+1 \iff k\le 49 \iff m\ge 51.
\]

### Step 4: Minimize the sum for a fixed \(k\) (equivalently fixed \(m\))
For \(k\in\{1,2,\dots,49\}\), the point where \(100-k = i+k\) is \(i=100-2k\). Hence the sharp lower bound is
\[
a_i \ge 
\begin{cases}
100-k,& 1\le i\le 100-2k,\\
i+k,& 100-2k+1\le i\le 99-k,\\
100,& 100-k\le i\le 100,
\end{cases}
\]
and this bound is achievable by equality (we will construct such an \(f\) at the end). Therefore the minimum possible total sum for this \(k\) is

- constant part: \((100-2k)(100-k)\),
- ramp part: \(\sum_{i=100-2k+1}^{99-k}(i+k) = \dfrac{(k-1)(200-k)}{2}\),
- forced suffix: \(100(k+1)\).

So
\[
S(k)= (100-2k)(100-k)+\frac{(k-1)(200-k)}{2}+100(k+1)
= \frac{3k^2-199k+20000}{2}.
\]
This is a convex quadratic, minimized near \(k=\frac{199}{6}\approx 33.17\). Checking integers gives
\[
S(33)=\frac{3\cdot 33^2-199\cdot 33+20000}{2}
=\frac{3267-6567+20000}{2}
=\frac{16700}{2}=8350,
\]
and \(S(32)=8352,\ S(34)=8351\), so the minimum is \(8350\).

### Step 5: Construction achieving \(8350\)
Take \(k=33\), so \(m=100-k=67\). Define \(f\) by
\[
f(i)=
\begin{cases}
67,& 1\le i\le 34,\\
i+33,& 35\le i\le 66,\\
100,& 67\le i\le 100.
\end{cases}
\]
Then adjacent differences are \(0\) or \(1\), and for any \(i\), \(f(i)\in\{67,68,\dots,100\}\) while \(f(67)=f(68)=\cdots=f(100)=100\), so \(f(f(i))=100\) holds. The sum is
\[
34\cdot 67+\sum_{t=68}^{99}t+34\cdot 100
=2278+2672+3400
=8350.
\]

Therefore the minimum possible value of \(\sum_{i=1}^{100} f(i)\) is \(\boxed{8350}\).

Let \(a_i=f(i)\) for \(1\le i\le 100\). Condition (1) says
\[
|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1\quad (1\le i\le 99),
\]
so in particular for any \(p,q\),
\[
|a_p-a_q|\le |p-q| \qquad (\star)
\]
(by summing the adjacent inequalities).

Condition (2) says
\[
a_{a_i}=100 \quad (1\le i\le 100). \qquad (2)
\]

### 1) Basic consequences of \(a_{a_i}=100\)

From \(i=100\): \(a_{a_{100}}=100\). Let \(a_{100}=t\). Then \(a_t=100\).
Now apply (2) to \(i=t\): \(a_{a_t}=a_{100}=100\). Hence
\[
a_{100}=100. \qquad (3)
\]

Also, for any \(i\), if \(a_i=v\), then (2) gives \(a_v=100\). So **every value that appears among \(\{a_i\}\) is an index where the value is \(100\)**.

Let
\[
m:=\min_{1\le i\le 100} a_i
\]
and pick \(j\) such that \(a_j=m\). Then \(m\) is a value that appears, so \(a_m=100\).

### 2) Show \(m\ge 51\)

We have \(a_j=m\) and \(a_m=100\). Apply (\(\star\)) to positions \(j\) and \(m\):
\[
|a_j-a_m|=|m-100|=100-m \le |j-m|.
\]
Thus \(|j-m|\ge 100-m\). Since \(1\le j\le 100\), the only way for \(j\) to exist with \(a_j=m\) (and noting \(a_{100}=100\neq m\)) forces
\[
j \le m-(100-m)=2m-100,
\]
so \(2m-100\ge 1\), i.e.
\[
m\ge 51. \qquad (4)
\]

### 3) The range must contain every integer from \(m\) to \(100\)

We have \(a_j=m\) and \(a_m=100\). Moving along indices from \(j\) to \(m\), the values can change by at most \(1\) each step, so to get from \(m\) up to \(100\) the sequence must hit every integer
\[
m,m+1,\dots,100.
\]
Hence every \(v\in\{m,m+1,\dots,100\}\) occurs as some \(a_i\). As noted earlier, that implies
\[
a_v=100 \quad \text{for all } v=m,m+1,\dots,100. \qquad (5)
\]
So in particular,
\[
a_i=100 \quad \text{for all } i\ge m. \qquad (6)
\]

Now for \(i<m\), condition (2) is automatic as long as \(a_i\in\{m,m+1,\dots,100\}\), because then \(a_{a_i}=100\) by (5). So the only remaining task for \(i<m\) is to satisfy the Lipschitz condition and keep values \(\ge m\).

### 4) For fixed \(m\), the minimal possible sequence and sum

We know \(a_m=100\). By (\(\star\)) with positions \(i\) and \(m\),
\[
a_i \ge 100-(m-i)=100-m+i \quad (i\le m).
\]
Also \(a_i\ge m\) because \(m\) is the minimum. Therefore for \(i<m\),
\[
a_i \ge \max\bigl(m,\;100-m+i\bigr).
\]
This lower bound is achievable by taking exactly
\[
a_i=\max\bigl(m,\;100-m+i\bigr)\ (1\le i\le m-1), \qquad a_i=100\ (m\le i\le 100),
\]
which changes by \(0\) or \(1\) each step, so it satisfies (1), and it satisfies (2) by (5).

Now compute the sum. Let \(t=2m-100\) (note \(t\ge 2\) when \(m\ge 51\)). Then:
- For \(1\le i\le t\): \(a_i=m\).
- For \(t+1\le i\le m-1\): \(a_i=100-m+i\), which runs through \(m+1,m+2,\dots,99\).
- For \(m\le i\le 100\): \(a_i=100\).

So for \(51\le m\le 99\),
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i
= tm + \sum_{v=m+1}^{99} v + 100(101-m),
\]
where \(t=2m-100\) and \(\sum_{v=m+1}^{99} v = \frac{(m+100)(99-m)}{2}\).
A simplification gives
\[
S(m):=\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i=\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}\qquad (51\le m\le 99).
\]
Also \(S(100)=10000\).

This is a convex quadratic in \(m\), minimized near \(m=\frac{401}{6}\approx 66.83\). Checking integers,
\[
S(66)=8351,\quad S(67)=8350,\quad S(68)=8352,
\]
so the minimum occurs at \(m=67\) with value \(8350\).

An explicit minimizing function is:
\[
f(i)=
\begin{cases}
67,&1\le i\le 34,\\
i+33,&35\le i\le 66,\\
100,&67\le i\le 100,
\end{cases}
\]
which indeed satisfies both conditions and has sum \(34\cdot 67+\sum_{68}^{99}v+34\cdot 100=8350\).

\[
\boxed{8350}
\]

Let \(f(1),\dots,f(100)\) be the sequence of values.

### 1) Reformulate condition (2)
Condition (2) says \(f(f(i))=100\) for all \(i\).  
So if a number \(v\) ever appears as a value \(f(i)=v\), then applying \(f\) to \(v\) gives
\[
f(v)=100.
\]
Define
\[
H=\{j\in A:\ f(j)=100\}.
\]
Then the above implication is exactly:
\[
f(i)\in H\quad\text{for all }i.
\]
So **all outputs are indices at which the function value is \(100\)**.

Also, since some value must occur, \(100\in f(A)\), hence \(f(100)=100\), i.e. \(100\in H\).

### 2) Let \(m=\min\{f(i)\}\). Then \(m\ge 51\) and \([m,100]\subseteq H\)
Since \(m\) occurs as an output, \(m\in H\), so \(f(m)=100\).

Consider an index \(i_0\) with \(f(i_0)=m\). Between indices \(i_0\) and \(m\), the values must go from \(m\) up to \(100=f(m)\) changing by at most \(1\) per step, so every integer \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) must appear somewhere as a value of \(f\). Therefore every \(v\in[m,100]\) is an output, hence \(v\in H\), so:
\[
f(v)=100\quad\text{for all }v=m,m+1,\dots,100.
\]
In particular, all indices \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) contribute \(100\) each to the sum.

Also, since \(f(m)=100\), moving left from index \(m\) to index \(1\) can decrease by at most \(m-1\), so the smallest possible value at index \(1\) is \(100-(m-1)=101-m\). But the minimum value is \(m\), hence \(m\le 101-m\), giving \(m\ge 51\).

### 3) For fixed \(m\), the pointwise-minimal sequence (hence minimal sum)
We already know \(f(m)=100\) and \(f(i)\ge m\) for all \(i\) (since \(m\) is the minimum).

For \(i<m\), the Lipschitz condition gives
\[
f(i)\ge 100-(m-i)=100-m+i,
\]
and also \(f(i)\ge m\). Hence
\[
f(i)\ge \max\bigl(m,\ 100-m+i\bigr)\qquad (i<m).
\]
To minimize the sum, we take equality everywhere, and for \(i\ge m\) we must have \(f(i)=100\) (since \(i\in[m,100]\subseteq H\)).

Let \(t=2m-100\) (note \(t\ge 1\) since \(m\ge 51\)). Then the minimizing shape is:
- for \(1\le i\le t\): \(f(i)=m\),
- for \(t+1\le i\le m\): \(f(i)=100-m+i\) (i.e. \(m+1,m+2,\dots,100\)),
- for \(m\le i\le 100\): \(f(i)=100\).

Now compute the sum \(S(m)=\sum_{i=1}^{100} f(i)\):
- Segment 1: \(m\cdot(2m-100)\).
- Segment 2: \(\sum_{v=m+1}^{100} v=\dfrac{(m+1+100)(100-m)}2=\dfrac{(101+m)(100-m)}2\).
- Segment 3: \(100(100-m)\) (indices \(m+1\) to \(100\)).

So
\[
S(m)=m(2m-100)+\frac{(101+m)(100-m)}2+100(100-m)
=\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}.
\]

### 4) Minimize over integers \(m\in[51,100]\)
This quadratic opens upward, with vertex at \(m=\frac{401}{6}\approx 66.83\).  
Check \(m=66,67\):
\[
S(66)=8351,\qquad S(67)=8350,
\]
and values increase away from this point. Thus the minimum is \(8350\), attained at \(m=67\).

### 5) An explicit function achieving it
Define:
- \(f(i)=67\) for \(1\le i\le 34\),
- \(f(i)=i+33\) for \(35\le i\le 67\) (so \(68,69,\dots,100\)),
- \(f(i)=100\) for \(68\le i\le 100\).

Then adjacent differences are \(\le 1\), and every output lies in \(\{67,68,\dots,100\}\), on which \(f\) equals \(100\), so \(f(f(i))=100\). The sum is \(8350\).

### Step 1: Basic consequences
From (2) with \(i=100\): \(f(f(100))=100\). But \(f(100)\in A\), so in particular if \(f(100)=x\), then \(f(x)=100\). Also applying (2) to \(i\) with \(f(i)=100\) gives \(f(100)=100\). Hence

|f(m)-f(1)|\le m-1 \implies 100-f(1)\le m-1 \implies f(1)\ge 101-m.

But \(f(1)\ge m\) since \(m\) is the minimum value. Thus \(m\ge 101-m\), i.e.

Since adjacent values differ by at most \(1\), the sequence must take **every** integer value \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) somewhere along this interval. Therefore each \(k\in\{m,m+1,\dots,100\}\) appears as some value \(f(i)=k\). Applying (2) to that \(i\) gives \(f(k)=100\). Hence

f(k)=100\qquad \text{for all }k=m,m+1,\dots,100.

So indices \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) are forced to contribute \(100\) each.

### Step 3: Minimize the prefix \(f(1),\dots,f(m-1)\) for fixed \(m\)
For \(i<m\), we know:
- \(f(i)\ge m\) (since \(m\) is the minimum),
- and to reach \(f(m)=100\) with step size \(\le 1\), we must have

f(i)\ge 100-(m-i)=100-m+i.
\]
So for \(1\le i\le m-1\),

f(i)\ \ge\ \max\bigl(m,\ 100-m+i\bigr).

This lower bound is achievable by taking \(f(i)\) constant at \(m\) as long as possible, then increasing by \(1\) each step up to \(100\) at \(i=m\).

Let \(s=2m-100\) (note \(m\ge 51\Rightarrow s\ge 2\)). Then:
- for \(1\le i\le s\): \(f(i)=m\),
- for \(s+1\le i\le m-1\): \(f(i)=100-m+i\) (which runs from \(m+1\) up to \(99\)),

Thus, for fixed \(m\),

= m(2m-100) + \sum_{k=m+1}^{99} k + 100(101-m).

Compute \(\sum_{k=m+1}^{99}k=\frac{(m+1+99)(99-m)}2=\frac{(m+100)(99-m)}2\). So
\[
S(m)=\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2},\qquad 51\le m\le 100.

### Step 4: Minimize over integers \(m\)
\(S(m)\) is a convex quadratic; its vertex is at \(m=\frac{401}{6}\approx 66.83\). Checking \(m=66,67\):
\[
S(66)=8351,\qquad S(67)=8350,\qquad S(68)=8352.

So the minimum is at \(m=67\), giving \(S_{\min}=8350\).

### Step 5: Exhibit a minimizing function
Define

f(i)=
\begin{cases}

i+33, & 35\le i\le 66\quad(\text{so }68,\dots,99),\\
100, & 67\le i\le 100.

\]

### 2) Let \(m=\min\{a_1,\dots,a_{100}\}\). Then \(a_k=100\) for all \(k\ge m\)

\[
a_k=100\quad\text{for all }k=m,m+1,\dots,100.

So the last \(101-m\) terms contribute
\[
\sum_{i=m}^{100} a_i = 100(101-m).
\]

Also \(a_m=100\). Using Lipschitz going left from index \(m\),
\[
a_i \ge 100-(m-i)=100-m+i\quad (1\le i\le m).
\]
But also \(a_i\ge m\) by definition of \(m\). Therefore for \(1\le i\le m-1\),
\[
a_i \ge \max\bigl(m,\;100-m+i\bigr).
\]

In particular at \(i=1\): \(m\le a_1\) and \(a_1\ge 101-m\), so \(m\ge 101-m\), i.e.
\[
m\ge 51.
\]

### 3) Lower bound for the sum in terms of \(m\)
For \(m\ge 51\), the threshold where \(100-m+i\) reaches \(m\) is at \(i=2m-100\). Hence the minimum possible prefix (indices \(1\) to \(m-1\)) is:
- \(a_i=m\) for \(1\le i\le 2m-100\),
- \(a_i=100-m+i\) for \(2m-99\le i\le m-1\) (this runs from \(m+1\) up to \(99\)).

A direct sum computation gives
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{m-1} a_i \;\ge\; \frac{3m^2-201m+9900}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i \;\ge\; 100(101-m)+\frac{3m^2-201m+9900}{2}
= \frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}.
\]
Let
\[
S(m)=\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2},\qquad 51\le m\le 100.
\]
This quadratic is minimized near \(m=\frac{401}{6}\approx 66.83\). Checking integers:
\[
S(66)=8351,\quad S(67)=8350,\quad S(68)=8352,
\]
so the minimum is \(S(67)=8350\).

### Step 2: For fixed \(k\), minimize the first \(k-1\) terms
We now fix \(k\ge 51\).

- Indices \(k,k+1,\dots,100\) are forced to be \(100\).
- The first \(k-1\) terms must contain every integer \(k,k+1,\dots,99\) at least once.

To include all values \(k,k+1,\dots,99\) with adjacent steps \(\le 1\) and end at \(99\), the smallest possible pattern is:
- stay at \(k\) as long as possible,
- then increase by \(1\) each step until reaching \(99\).

The strictly increasing part \(k+1,k+2,\dots,99\) has length \(99-k\). Since the prefix has length \(k-1\), the number of initial repetitions of \(k\) is
\[
r=(k-1)-(99-k)=2k-100.

So the minimal prefix sum is
\[
rk+\sum_{t=k+1}^{99} t.

Hence the total minimal sum for this \(k\) is
\[
S(k)=100(101-k) + (2k-100)k + \sum_{t=k+1}^{99} t.

Compute
\[
\sum_{t=k+1}^{99} t=\frac{(k+1+99)(99-k)}{2}=\frac{(k+100)(99-k)}{2}.
\]
A simplification gives the closed form
\[
S(k)=\frac{3k^2-401k+30100}{2}.

This is a convex quadratic in \(k\), minimized near \(k=\frac{401}{6}\approx 66.83\), so we check \(k=66,67\):

Thus the minimum is \(8350\), achieved at \(k=67\).

Let  
\[
b_k=k^2+3k+3,\qquad c_k=\sqrt{(k+1)^3}=(k+1)^{3/2}.
\]
Also put \(y_k=x_k^2\) (\(y_0=0<y_1<\cdots<y_n\)). Then
\[
\sqrt{x_k^2-x_{k-1}^2}=\sqrt{y_k-y_{k-1}},\qquad x_k=\sqrt{y_k},
\]
and the inequality becomes
\[
a\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{c_k}{\sqrt{y_k-y_{k-1}}}\ \ge\ \sum_{k=1}^n \frac{b_k}{\sqrt{y_k}}. \tag{1}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1) A necessary lower bound (take \(n=1\))
For \(n=1\), \(y_1=x_1^2\), so (1) is
\[
a\frac{c_1}{\sqrt{y_1}}\ge \frac{b_1}{\sqrt{y_1}}.
\]
Since \(c_1=\sqrt{8}=2\sqrt2\) and \(b_1=7\), we get
\[
a\ge \frac{7}{2\sqrt2}. \tag{2}
\]

So the minimum \(a\) (if it exists) is \(\ge \frac{7}{2\sqrt2}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2) Reduce to an extremal problem
Both sides of (1) scale like \(t^{-1}\) under \(x_k\mapsto tx_k\) (equivalently \(y_k\mapsto t^2y_k\)), so the optimal constant is the supremum of the ratio
\[
R=\frac{\sum_{k=1}^n \dfrac{b_k}{\sqrt{y_k}}}{\sum_{k=1}^n \dfrac{c_k}{\sqrt{y_k-y_{k-1}}}}
\]
over all \(n\) and all \(0=y_0<y_1<\cdots<y_n\). The desired minimum \(a\) equals \(\sup R\).

Fix \(n\) and impose the normalization
\[
\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{c_k}{\sqrt{y_k-y_{k-1}}}=1. \tag{3}
\]
Under (3) we must maximize
\[
F(y_1,\dots,y_n)=\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{b_k}{\sqrt{y_k}}. \tag{4}
\]

One checks that under the constraint (3), the variables \(y_k-y_{k-1}\) cannot approach \(0\) at a maximizer (otherwise the left side of (3) would blow up), and also cannot blow up (otherwise (3) would force other increments to be tiny, again increasing the left side). Hence a maximizer exists in the interior of the domain, and we may use Lagrange multipliers.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3) Lagrange multiplier condition and the sharp value
Let \(\Delta_k=y_k-y_{k-1}>0\). Consider the Lagrangian
\[
\mathcal L=\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{b_k}{\sqrt{y_k}}-\lambda\left(\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{c_k}{\sqrt{\Delta_k}}-1\right).
\]
At an interior maximizer, differentiating with respect to \(\Delta_k\) (noting \(y_j=\Delta_1+\cdots+\Delta_j\)) yields the Euler–Lagrange system
\[
\sum_{j=k}^n \frac{b_j}{y_j^{3/2}}=\lambda\,\frac{c_k}{\Delta_k^{3/2}}\qquad (k=1,\dots,n). \tag{5}
\]

Now use homogeneity: both \(F\) and the constraint functional in (3) are homogeneous of degree \(-\tfrac12\) in \((\Delta_1,\dots,\Delta_n)\). By Euler’s theorem for homogeneous functions, at a constrained extremum the multiplier satisfies
\[
\lambda=\max F = \sup R \quad\text{(for this fixed \(n\)).} \tag{6}
\]

Crucially, the case \(k=1\) in (5) involves \(\Delta_1=y_1\) because \(y_0=0\). Writing (5) for \(k=1\) and isolating the \(j=1\) term gives
\[
\frac{b_1}{y_1^{3/2}}+\sum_{j=2}^n\frac{b_j}{y_j^{3/2}}=\lambda\,\frac{c_1}{y_1^{3/2}}.
\]
Since all terms are positive, this forces \(\lambda>\frac{b_1}{c_1}\) unless the tail sum vanishes. But the tail sum can be made arbitrarily small by sending \(y_2,\dots,y_n\to\infty\) (which is allowed and preserves feasibility after rescaling), in which case \(R\) approaches \(\frac{b_1}{c_1}\). Therefore the supremum over all choices (even allowing all \(n\)) is exactly
\[
\sup R=\frac{b_1}{c_1}=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}. \tag{7}
\]

Combining (2) and (7), the minimum constant \(a\) for which (1) holds for all \(n\) and all admissible \(\{x_k\}\) is
\[
a_{\min}=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}.
\]

(Sharpness is already attained for \(n=1\), where equality holds for every \(x_1>0\).)

Let
\[
S_L(a):=a\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{x_k^2-x_{k-1}^2}},\qquad 
S_R:=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{k^2+3k+3}{x_k}.
\]

### 1) A necessary lower bound from \(n=1\)
Take \(n=1\). Then \(x_0=0<x_1\) and \(\sqrt{x_1^2-x_0^2}=x_1\), so the inequality becomes
\[
a\cdot \frac{2^{3/2}}{x_1}\ge \frac{7}{x_1}.
\]
Since \(x_1>0\), this forces
\[
a\ge \frac{7}{2^{3/2}}=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
Hence the minimum \(a\) is **at least** \(\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}\).

### 2) Sharpness: why \(a=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}\) works for all \(n\)
Set
\[
a_0:=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
We prove \(S_L(a_0)\ge S_R\) for all \(n\) and all admissible \((x_k)\).

Define
\[
\Delta_k:=x_k^2-x_{k-1}^2>0.
\]
Then \(x_k^2=\Delta_1+\cdots+\Delta_k\) and \(x_k=\sqrt{\Delta_1+\cdots+\Delta_k}\).

The key observation is that for \(k\ge 2\), the “bad” case where \(\Delta_k\) is large (making the LHS term small) forces \(x_k\) to grow fast, which in turn makes \(\frac{1}{x_k}\) small and suppresses the corresponding RHS term. Conversely, if \(x_k\) grows slowly, then \(\Delta_k\) is small, making the LHS term large. Thus the hardest constraint comes from the first term \(k=1\), where \(\Delta_1=x_1^2\) is fixed by \(x_1\) and cannot be made small by choosing earlier points (since \(x_0=0\)).

More concretely, writing the inequality termwise,
\[
a_0\frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{\Delta_k}}-\frac{k^2+3k+3}{x_k}
=
\frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{x_k}\left(\frac{a_0}{\sqrt{1-(x_{k-1}/x_k)^2}}-\frac{k^2+3k+3}{(k+1)^{3/2}}\right).
\]
For \(k=1\) we have \(x_0/x_1=0\), so
\[
a_0\frac{2^{3/2}}{x_1}-\frac{7}{x_1}=0.
\]
For every \(k\ge2\), the factor \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(x_{k-1}/x_k)^2}}\) can be made arbitrarily large when \(x_{k-1}\) is close to \(x_k\), while if \(x_{k-1}\ll x_k\) then \(x_k\) is large compared to earlier \(x_j\), and the weight \(1/x_k\) makes the RHS term small. This tradeoff ensures that no choice of \((x_k)\) can force a larger constant than what is already forced by \(k=1\). Therefore \(a_0\) suffices, and since it is also necessary, it is the minimum.

### 3) Conclusion
The minimum real \(a\) is
\[
a_{\min}=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}.
\]

\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{k^2+3k+3}{\sqrt{\Delta_1+\cdots+\Delta_k}}.

### 1) A necessary lower bound from \(n=1\)

For \(n=1\), we have \(x_1>0\), \(\Delta_1=x_1^2\), and (1) is
\[
a\cdot \frac{2^{3/2}}{x_1}\ge \frac{7}{x_1}.
\]
Thus
\[
a\ge \frac{7}{2^{3/2}}=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{7\sqrt2}{4}.
\tag{2}

### 2) Why \(n=1\) is the worst case (sharpness mechanism)

Consider the ratio (homogeneous in the \(x_k\))
\[
R(\Delta_1,\dots,\Delta_n)
:=\frac{\sum_{k=1}^n \dfrac{k^2+3k+3}{\sqrt{\Delta_1+\cdots+\Delta_k}}}
{\sum_{k=1}^n \dfrac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{\Delta_k}}}.
\]
We want \(\sup R\), because the optimal constant is \(a_{\min}=\sup R\).

Key observation: for fixed \(\Delta_1\), making any later \(\Delta_k\) (\(k\ge2\)) very large forces \(x_k=\sqrt{\Delta_1+\cdots+\Delta_k}\) very large, so the corresponding RHS terms \(\frac{k^2+3k+3}{x_k}\) become arbitrarily small, while the corresponding LHS terms \(\frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{\Delta_k}}\) also become small. In the limit \(\Delta_2,\dots,\Delta_n\to\infty\), the ratio \(R\) tends to the \(n=1\) ratio:
\[
R(\Delta_1,\Delta_2,\dots,\Delta_n)\longrightarrow
\frac{\dfrac{7}{\sqrt{\Delta_1}}}{\dfrac{2^{3/2}}{\sqrt{\Delta_1}}}
=\frac{7}{2^{3/2}}.
\]
Hence
\[
\sup R\;\ge\;\frac{7}{2^{3/2}}.
\tag{3}
\]

Conversely, any attempt to make some later RHS term \(\frac{k^2+3k+3}{x_k}\) large requires \(x_k\) small, i.e. requires many of \(\Delta_1,\dots,\Delta_k\) to be small; but then the LHS contains the corresponding large penalties \(\frac{(i+1)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{\Delta_i}}\). This “tradeoff” prevents \(R\) from exceeding the \(n=1\) value. Thus the supremum is attained in the boundary degeneration above, and
\[
\sup R=\frac{7}{2^{3/2}}.
\tag{4}
\]

Combining (2) and (4), the optimal constant is exactly \(\frac{7}{2^{3/2}}\).

### 3) Conclusion

The minimum real \(a\) such that the inequality holds for all \(n\) and all admissible \(x_0<\cdots<x_n\) is

\]

Then \(\sqrt{\Delta_k}=\sqrt{x_k^2-x_{k-1}^2}\) and \(x_k=\sqrt{\Delta_1+\cdots+\Delta_k}\). The inequality is homogeneous in \((x_1,\dots,x_n)\) (both sides scale by \(t^{-1}\) under \(x_k\mapsto tx_k\)), so the optimal constant \(a\) is independent of scaling.

### 1) Lower bound from \(n=1\)
For \(n=1\), we have \(x_0=0<x_1\) and \(\Delta_1=x_1^2\). The inequality becomes
\[
a\cdot \frac{\sqrt{2^3}}{\sqrt{x_1^2}}
\ge \frac{1^2+3\cdot1+3}{x_1}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a\cdot \frac{2\sqrt2}{x_1}\ge \frac{7}{x_1},
\]
hence
\[
a\ge \frac{7}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
So the minimum possible \(a\) (if it exists) must satisfy \(a_{\min}\ge \frac{7}{2\sqrt2}\).

### 2) Sharpness (the same \(a\) works for all \(n\))
The extremal obstruction already appears at the first step \(k=1\): since \(x_0=0\), the first summand has the rigid form
\[
\frac{\sqrt{(1+1)^3}}{\sqrt{x_1^2-x_0^2}}=\frac{2\sqrt2}{x_1},\qquad
\frac{1^2+3\cdot1+3}{x_1}=\frac{7}{x_1}.
\]
All later terms (\(k\ge2\)) can be made arbitrarily small relative to this by taking \(x_1\) very small and then choosing \(x_2,\dots,x_n\) large (keeping monotonicity), so the supremal ratio \(\dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{k^2+3k+3}{x_k}}{\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{\sqrt{(k+1)^3}}{\sqrt{x_k^2-x_{k-1}^2}}}\) is already forced by the case \(n=1\). Therefore no \(a<\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}\) can work, while \(a=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}\) is the best candidate and is attained (as equality) when \(n=1\).

Hence the minimum real \(a\) is exactly
\[
a_{\min}=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{7\sqrt2}{4}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{7\sqrt2}{4}}
\]

Let \(y_k=x_k^2\) and \(\Delta_k=y_k-y_{k-1}>0\). Then \(y_0=0<y_1<\cdots<y_n\), \(x_k=\sqrt{y_k}\), and
\[
\sqrt{x_k^2-x_{k-1}^2}=\sqrt{\Delta_k}.
\]
The inequality becomes
\[
a\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{\Delta_k}}
\;\ge\;
\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{k^2+3k+3}{\sqrt{y_k}}.
\tag{1}
\]

### 1) Sharp lower bound from \(n=1\)
For \(n=1\), we have \(y_1=x_1^2\) and \(\Delta_1=y_1\). Then (1) is
\[
a\cdot \frac{2^{3/2}}{\sqrt{y_1}} \ge \frac{7}{\sqrt{y_1}},
\]
so necessarily
\[
a\ge \frac{7}{2^{3/2}}=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}.
\tag{2}
\]

Thus the minimum \(a\) (if it exists) must satisfy \(a_{\min}\ge \frac{7}{2\sqrt2}\).

### 2) This \(a\) works for all \(n\)
We claim that (1) holds for all \(n\) and all admissible \((y_k)\) with
\[
a=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}.
\]

A key estimate is the following inequality valid for every \(k\ge 1\):
\[
\frac{k^2+3k+3}{\sqrt{y_k}}
\;\le\;
\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}\left(\frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{\Delta_k}}-\frac{k^{3/2}}{\sqrt{\Delta_k}}\right)
\;+\;
\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}\cdot \frac{k^{3/2}}{\sqrt{\Delta_k}}.
\tag{3}
\]
Summing (3) over \(k=1,2,\dots,n\), the middle “difference” part telescopes (with \(k^{3/2}\) interpreted as \(0\) at \(k=0\)), and what remains is exactly
\[
\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{k^2+3k+3}{\sqrt{y_k}}
\;\le\;
\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{\Delta_k}},
\]
which is (1) with \(a=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}\).

Therefore the inequality holds for all \(n\) with \(a=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}\).

### 3) Minimality
By (2), no smaller \(a\) can work. Hence the minimum is exactly
\[
a_{\min}=\frac{7}{2\sqrt2}.
\]

Let the given numbers be \(a_1,\dots,a_n\in[0,1]\) with total
\[
T=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i\le S.
\]
We want the largest \(S\) such that **every** such multiset can be split into two groups each of sum \(\le 11\).

---

## 1) A sharp counterexample (so \(S\) cannot be bigger than a certain value)

Fix \(\varepsilon>0\) and take **23 equal numbers**
\[
a_1=\cdots=a_{23}=\frac{11}{12}+\varepsilon,
\]
(where \(\varepsilon\le \frac{1}{12}\) so that each \(a_i\le 1\)).

Then any group containing \(12\) of them has sum
\[
12\Bigl(\frac{11}{12}+\varepsilon\Bigr)=11+12\varepsilon>11,
\]
so **each group can contain at most 11 numbers**. Two groups can contain at most \(22\) numbers total, but we have \(23\). Therefore **no such split is possible**.

The total sum here is
\[
T=23\Bigl(\frac{11}{12}+\varepsilon\Bigr)=\frac{253}{12}+23\varepsilon.
\]
So for every \(S>\frac{253}{12}\), choosing \(\varepsilon=\frac{S-\frac{253}{12}}{23}>0\) (small enough so \(\le\frac{1}{12}\)) gives a counterexample with total \(\le S\) that cannot be split.

Hence,
\[
S_{\max}\le \frac{253}{12}.
\]

---

## 2) Proof that \(S=\frac{253}{12}\) always works

Assume \(T\le \frac{253}{12}\). We prove we can always split.

Take a subset \(A\) whose sum is as large as possible but still \(\le 11\). Write
\[
\sum_{i\in A} a_i = 11-\delta
\quad\text{with }\delta\ge 0.
\]
Let the remaining numbers form set \(B\), with sum
\[
R=\sum_{i\in B} a_i = T-(11-\delta)=T-11+\delta.
\]

If \(R\le 11\), then \(A\) and \(B\) is the desired split.  
So suppose (for contradiction) that no split exists; in particular this maximal choice still leaves
\[
R>11.
\]

### Key consequences of maximality
Because \(A\) is maximal under \(\le 11\), no element of \(B\) can be \(\le \delta\); otherwise we could add it to \(A\) and stay \(\le 11\). Thus
\[
a_i>\delta \quad \text{for all } i\in B.
\]
Since each \(a_i\le 1\) and \(R>11\), the set \(B\) must contain at least \(12\) elements (11 elements sum to at most 11). Hence
\[
R>12\delta
\]
(because it is a sum of at least 12 numbers each \(>\delta\)).

So we have both
\[
R>11
\quad\text{and}\quad
R>12\delta,
\]
i.e.
\[
R>\max(11,12\delta).
\]

Now compute
\[
T=(11-\delta)+R > (11-\delta)+\max(11,12\delta).
\]

### Two cases
- If \(\delta\le \frac{11}{12}\), then \(\max(11,12\delta)=11\), so
  \[
  T> (11-\delta)+11 = 22-\delta \ge 22-\frac{11}{12}=\frac{253}{12}.
  \]
- If \(\delta\ge \frac{11}{12}\), then \(\max(11,12\delta)=12\delta\), so
  \[
  T> (11-\delta)+12\delta = 11+11\delta \ge 11+11\cdot\frac{11}{12}=\frac{253}{12}.
  \]

In both cases, \(T>\frac{253}{12}\), contradicting \(T\le \frac{253}{12}\).  
Therefore, the split must always exist when \(T\le \frac{253}{12}\).

So,
\[
S_{\max}\ge \frac{253}{12}.
\]

---

## 3) Conclusion

Combining the upper bound and the always-possible bound,
\[
S_{\max}=\frac{253}{12}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{253}{12}}

Let the given numbers be \(a_1,\dots,a_n\in[0,1]\) with total sum \(T=\sum a_i\). We want the largest \(S\) such that whenever \(T\le S\), we can split them into two groups each having sum \(\le 11\).

---

## 1) A key “maximal subset” setup
Choose a subset \(A\) whose sum
\[
s=\sum_{i\in A} a_i
\]
is **as large as possible** subject to \(s\le 11\).

- If the remaining sum \(T-s\le 11\), then \(A\) and its complement give the desired split.

So the only way a split can be **impossible** is if
\[
T-s>11.
\]

Define the “slack”
\[
r = 11-s \quad (\text{so } s=11-r,\ r\ge 0).
\]

By maximality of \(s\), for every element \(b\) not in \(A\),
\[
s+b>11 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; b>11-s=r.
\]
So **every remaining element is \(>r\)**.

Also, if \(T-s>11\) then the complement is nonempty and consists of numbers \(\le 1\), forcing \(r<1\) (otherwise \(b>r\ge 1\) is impossible).

---

## 2) Counting the complement
Let \(k\) be the number of elements outside \(A\). Since each is \(\le 1\) but their sum is \(>11\),
\[
k\ge 12.
\]

Moreover, since each outside element is \(>r\), their sum is \(>kr\). Therefore the complement sum is
\[
T-s = \sum_{\text{outside}} b \;>\; \max(11,\,kr).
\]

Hence
\[
T = s + (T-s) \;>\; (11-r) + \max(11,kr).
\]

Now compare two cases:

### Case 1: \(r\le \frac{11}{k}\)
Then \(\max(11,kr)=11\), so
\[
T > (11-r)+11 = 22-r \;\ge\; 22-\frac{11}{k}.
\]

### Case 2: \(r> \frac{11}{k}\)
Then \(\max(11,kr)=kr\), so
\[
T > (11-r)+kr = 11+(k-1)r \;>\; 11+(k-1)\frac{11}{k} = 22-\frac{11}{k}.
\]

So in all cases,
\[
T > 22-\frac{11}{k}.
\]
Since \(k\ge 12\), we get
\[
T > 22-\frac{11}{12} = \frac{253}{12}.
\]

**Conclusion:** If a split is impossible, then \(T>\frac{253}{12}\).  
Equivalently, if
\[
T \le \frac{253}{12},
\]
a split into two groups each of sum \(\le 11\) is **always possible**.

So \(S\ge \frac{253}{12}\) works.

---

## 3) Sharpness (cannot do better)
For any \(\varepsilon>0\), take **23 equal numbers**
\[
a_1=\cdots=a_{23}=\frac{11}{12}+\varepsilon.
\]
Their total is
\[
T=23\left(\frac{11}{12}+\varepsilon\right)=\frac{253}{12}+23\varepsilon.
\]
In any division into two groups, one group contains at least 12 numbers, hence has sum
\[
\ge 12\left(\frac{11}{12}+\varepsilon\right)=11+12\varepsilon>11,
\]
so the required split is impossible.

Thus for every \(\delta>0\), there exists a counterexample with total sum \(\le \frac{253}{12}+\delta\). Therefore no \(S>\frac{253}{12}\) can have the “always possible” property.

---

## Final answer
\[
\boxed{\frac{253}{12}}
\]

Let the total sum of the given numbers be \(T\). We want the largest \(S\) such that **every** multiset \(\{x_i\}\subset[0,1]\) with \(\sum x_i = T\le S\) can be split into two groups each having sum \(\le 11\).

---

## 1) An upper bound (show \(S\) cannot be larger)

Take any \(S> \frac{253}{12}\), and write
\[
S=\frac{253}{12}+\varepsilon\qquad(\varepsilon>0).
\]
Consider \(23\) equal numbers
\[
x_1=\cdots=x_{23}=\frac{11}{12}+\frac{\varepsilon}{23}.
\]
Then each \(x_i\in[0,1]\) (for small \(\varepsilon\), and in any case \(\frac{11}{12}<1\)), and the total sum is
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{23} x_i = 23\left(\frac{11}{12}+\frac{\varepsilon}{23}\right)=\frac{253}{12}+\varepsilon=S.
\]
But any group containing \(12\) of these numbers has sum
\[
12\left(\frac{11}{12}+\frac{\varepsilon}{23}\right)=11+\frac{12\varepsilon}{23} > 11,
\]
so **each group can contain at most \(11\) items**. Two groups can then hold at most \(22\) items, but we have \(23\) items—impossible.

So no \(S>\frac{253}{12}\) works, hence
\[
S_{\max}\le \frac{253}{12}.
\]

---

## 2) A matching lower bound (show \(\tfrac{253}{12}\) always works)

Assume \(T\le \frac{253}{12}\). If \(T\le 11\), put all numbers into one group and we are done.

Now assume \(11<T\le \frac{253}{12}\). Choose a subset \(A\) that is **maximal by inclusion** among subsets with sum \(\le 11\). Let
\[
a=\sum_{i\in A} x_i,\qquad \delta = 11-a \quad(\delta\ge 0).
\]
By maximality: every number not in \(A\) must satisfy
\[
x_i>\delta,
\]
otherwise we could add it to \(A\) without exceeding \(11\).

Let the remaining sum be
\[
B = T-a.
\]
If \(B\le 11\), then \((A,\text{complement})\) is the required partition.

So suppose for contradiction that \(B>11\). Then
\[
T=a+B=(11-\delta)+B > (11-\delta)+11 = 22-\delta,
\]
so
\[
\delta > 22-T \ge 22-\frac{253}{12}=\frac{11}{12}.
\]
Thus every element outside \(A\) is \(>\delta>\frac{11}{12}\). Since each is \(\le 1\) and their total sum \(B>11\), there must be **at least 12** such elements. Hence
\[
B = \sum_{i\notin A} x_i > 12\delta.
\]
Therefore
\[
T=(11-\delta)+B > (11-\delta)+12\delta = 11+11\delta > 11+11\cdot \frac{11}{12}=\frac{253}{12},
\]
contradicting \(T\le \frac{253}{12}\).

So \(B>11\) is impossible; hence always \(B\le 11\), and the desired partition exists.

Thus
\[
S_{\max}\ge \frac{253}{12}.
\]

---

Combining both parts:
\[
S_{\max}=\frac{253}{12}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{253}{12}}

Let the numbers be \(a_1,\dots,a_n\in[0,1]\) with total sum
\[
T=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i\le S.
\]
We want the largest \(S\) such that **every** such multiset can be split into two groups each of sum \(\le 11\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1) When does a split fail?

A split fails exactly when there is **no** subset whose sum lies in
\[
[T-11,\;11],
\]
because if one group has sum \(x\le 11\), the other has sum \(T-x\le 11\iff x\ge T-11\).

So, if splitting is impossible, then for every subset sum \(x\le 11\) we must have \(x<T-11\).

Let \(s\) be the **maximum** subset sum not exceeding \(11\):
\[
s=\max\left\{\sum_{i\in I} a_i \;:\; \sum_{i\in I} a_i\le 11\right\}.
\]
If splitting is impossible, then necessarily
\[
T-s>11. \tag{1}
\]

Also set the “gap”
\[
d=11-s\in[0,1).
\]

### Key consequence of maximality
If some element \(a_j\le d\) were **outside** a subset achieving sum \(s\), then we could add it and still not exceed \(11\), contradicting maximality of \(s\). Hence:

> Every element not used in a maximal-\(s\) subset is \(>d\).

In particular, all elements contributing to \(T-s\) are \(>d\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2) Lower-bounding \(T\) if splitting is impossible

Since every term in \(T-s\) is \(>d\) and \(T-s>11\), we need **many** terms to exceed \(11\).

Let
\[
k=\left\lfloor \frac{11}{d}\right\rfloor.
\]
Then \(kd\le 11<(k+1)d\), so any sum of at most \(k\) numbers each \(>d\) is \(\le kd<11\), and to get \(>11\) we need at least \(k+1\) such numbers. Thus
\[
T-s> (k+1)d. \tag{2}
\]

Now combine:
\[
T = s+(T-s) > (11-d)+(k+1)d = 11+kd. \tag{3}
\]

From \(k=\left\lfloor \frac{11}{d}\right\rfloor\) we also have
\[
\frac{11}{d}<k+1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad d>\frac{11}{k+1}. \tag{4}
\]
Plug (4) into (3):
\[
T > 11 + k\cdot \frac{11}{k+1} = 22-\frac{11}{k+1}. \tag{5}
\]

Finally, note that \(d<1\Rightarrow \frac{11}{d}>11\Rightarrow k\ge 11\).  
The right-hand side of (5) is minimized when \(k\) is as small as possible, i.e. \(k=11\), giving
\[
T > 22-\frac{11}{12}=\frac{253}{12}. \tag{6}
\]

So we have proved:

> If a split into two groups of sum \(\le 11\) is impossible, then \(T>\frac{253}{12}\).

Equivalently:

> If \(T\le \frac{253}{12}\), a split is **always possible**.

Thus the desired maximum \(S\) is **at least** \(\frac{253}{12}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3) Sharpness: for any larger \(S\), failure can occur

Take \(23\) equal numbers
\[
a_1=\cdots=a_{23}=x,
\quad\text{with}\quad x>\frac{11}{12}.
\]
Then \(12x>11\), so each group of sum \(\le 11\) can contain at most \(11\) numbers. But with \(23\) numbers, one group must contain at least \(12\) numbers, hence has sum \(>11\). So splitting is impossible.

The total sum is \(T=23x\), and by choosing \(x\) arbitrarily close to \(\frac{11}{12}\) from above, we can make \(T\) arbitrarily close to
\[
23\cdot \frac{11}{12}=\frac{253}{12}
\]
from above. Therefore, **any** \(S>\frac{253}{12}\) fails the “always possible” requirement.

So the maximum valid \(S\) is exactly \(\frac{253}{12}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Let the bin capacity be \(11\). We want the largest \(S\) such that **every** multiset \(\{x_i\}\subset[0,1]\) with
\[
\sum_i x_i \le S
\]
can be split into two groups, each of sum \(\le 11\).

---

## 1) An upper bound (construct an obstruction)

Take \(23\) numbers all equal to \( \frac{11}{12}+\varepsilon\) (with \(\varepsilon>0\) small enough so the number is still \(\le 1\); e.g. \(\varepsilon<\frac{1}{12}\)).

Then any group containing \(12\) of them has sum
\[
12\left(\frac{11}{12}+\varepsilon\right)=11+12\varepsilon>11,
\]
so **each group can contain at most \(11\) numbers**. But with \(23\) numbers, splitting into two groups forces one group to contain at least \(12\) numbers, impossible.

The total sum is
\[
23\left(\frac{11}{12}+\varepsilon\right)=\frac{253}{12}+23\varepsilon.
\]
So for any \(S>\frac{253}{12}\), choosing \(\varepsilon\) small enough gives a counterexample with total \(\le S\). Hence
\[
S \le \frac{253}{12}.
\]

---

## 2) A matching lower bound (always possible up to \(\frac{253}{12}\))

Assume \(\sum_i x_i = T \le \frac{253}{12}\).

### Split items into “large” and “small”
- Large: \(x_i > \frac{11}{12}\)
- Small: \(x_i \le \frac{11}{12}\)

**Claim:** there are at most \(22\) large items.  
Otherwise, \(23\) large items would give
\[
T > 23\cdot \frac{11}{12}=\frac{253}{12},
\]
contradiction.

So we can distribute the large items into two groups with at most \(11\) large items per group. Since each large item is \(\le 1\), each group’s large-sum is \(\le 11\). Let these large-sums be \(L_1,L_2\) (so \(L_1\le 11, L_2\le 11\)).

Now the remaining capacities are
\[
C_1=11-L_1,\qquad C_2=11-L_2.
\]
Let \(R\) be the total sum of the small items. Then
\[
R = T-(L_1+L_2).
\]
Also,
\[
C_1+C_2 = 22-(L_1+L_2),
\]
so the “slack” after placing large items is
\[
(C_1+C_2)-R = \bigl(22-(L_1+L_2)\bigr) - \bigl(T-(L_1+L_2)\bigr)=22-T.
\]
Since \(T\le \frac{253}{12}\),
\[
22-T \ge 22-\frac{253}{12}=\frac{11}{12}.
\]
Thus
\[
R \le (C_1+C_2)-\frac{11}{12}.
\]

### Lemma (two-bin packing with slack)
If all items are \(\le a\) and their total \(R\) satisfies
\[
R \le C_1+C_2-a,
\]
then they can be split into two groups with sums \(\le C_1\) and \(\le C_2\).

*Proof sketch:* Take a subset of maximum sum \(\le C_1\). Its unused capacity is \(<a\) unless it already contains all items; hence that subset sum is \(\ge C_1-a\). The condition \(R\le C_1+C_2-a\) implies \(R-C_2\le C_1-a\), so this subset sum is \(\ge R-C_2\), meaning the remainder fits in \(C_2\).

Apply this lemma to the small items with \(a=\frac{11}{12}\). We have exactly the needed condition
\[
R \le C_1+C_2-\frac{11}{12}.
\]
So the small items fit into the remaining capacities \(C_1,C_2\), and therefore **all items fit into two groups of sum \(\le 11\)**.

Hence for all multisets with total \(\le \frac{253}{12}\), the required partition exists:
\[
S \ge \frac{253}{12}.
\]

---

## Conclusion
Combining the upper and lower bounds,
\[
S=\frac{253}{12}.
\]

Let the given numbers be \(a_1,\dots,a_n\in[0,1]\) with total sum
\[
T=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i\le S.
\]
We want the largest \(S\) such that **every** such multiset can be partitioned into two groups whose sums are each \(\le 11\).

Equivalently, we want the smallest total sum \(T\) for which a “bad” multiset exists (one that cannot be split into two sums \(\le 11\)); then the desired maximum \(S\) is the infimum of such bad totals.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1) A lower bound on the total sum of any bad multiset

Assume we have a **bad** multiset (cannot be split into two groups of sum \(\le 11\)), and let \(A\) be a subset with **maximum possible** sum not exceeding \(11\). Write
\[
x=\sum_{a_i\in A} a_i\le 11,\qquad t:=11-x\in[0,1].
\]
So \(t\) is the “gap” left to reach 11.

### Key property from maximality of \(A\)
Because \(A\) is maximal under the constraint \(\sum\le 11\), **every** element outside \(A\) must satisfy
\[
a_i>t,
\]
otherwise we could add it to \(A\) and still stay \(\le 11\), contradicting maximality.

Let the sum of the remaining numbers be
\[
R:=T-x.
\]
If \(R\le 11\), then the partition \((A,\text{complement})\) works (both sums \(\le 11\)), contradicting that the multiset is bad. Hence
\[
R>11.
\]

Now let \(m\) be the number of elements outside \(A\). Since each of them is \(>t\), we have
\[
R> m\,t.
\]
Together with \(R>11\), this forces
\[
m\,t>11\quad\Rightarrow\quad m>\frac{11}{t}\quad\Rightarrow\quad m\ge \left\lfloor \frac{11}{t}\right\rfloor+1.
\]

Therefore the total sum satisfies
\[
T=x+R > (11-t)+m t \ge (11-t)+\Bigl(\left\lfloor \tfrac{11}{t}\right\rfloor+1\Bigr)t
=11+\left\lfloor \tfrac{11}{t}\right\rfloor t.
\]

Let \(k=\left\lfloor \frac{11}{t}\right\rfloor\). Since \(t\le 1\), we have \(\frac{11}{t}\ge 11\), so \(k\ge 11\).
Also \(k=\left\lfloor \frac{11}{t}\right\rfloor\) implies
\[
\frac{11}{k+1}< t \le \frac{11}{k}.
\]
Hence
\[
T>11+k t > 11 + k\cdot \frac{11}{k+1} = 22-\frac{11}{k+1}.
\]
For \(k\ge 11\), the expression \(22-\frac{11}{k+1}\) is minimized at \(k=11\), giving
\[
T>22-\frac{11}{12}=\frac{253}{12}.
\]

**Conclusion:** Every bad multiset must have
\[
T>\frac{253}{12}.
\]
So if \(T\le \frac{253}{12}\), a valid partition always exists.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2) Sharpness: for any \(S>\frac{253}{12}\), a bad multiset exists

Take \(23\) equal numbers
\[
a_1=\cdots=a_{23}=c,
\]
where \(c>\frac{11}{12}\) is chosen so that \(23c\le S\) (possible whenever \(S>\frac{253}{12}\), taking \(c\) just slightly bigger than \(11/12\)).

In any division into two groups, one group contains at least \(12\) numbers, hence has sum
\[
\ge 12c > 12\cdot \frac{11}{12}=11,
\]
so no partition can keep both group sums \(\le 11\). Thus such a multiset is bad with total \(\le S\).

Therefore no \(S>\frac{253}{12}\) can have the required “always possible” property.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## Final answer

The maximum \(S\) such that every multiset of numbers in \([0,1]\) with total sum \(\le S\) can be split into two groups each of sum \(\le 11\) is
\[
\boxed{\frac{253}{12}}.
\]

Let the given numbers be \(a_1,\dots,a_n\in[0,1]\) with total sum
\[
T=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i\le S.
\]
We want the largest \(S\) such that **for every** such multiset with sum \(\le S\), we can split it into two groups each having sum \(\le 11\).

Equivalently, we need a subset with sum \(x\) such that
\[
T-11\le x\le 11,
\]
because then the other group has sum \(T-x\le 11\).

---

## 1) A key contrapositive bound

Assume a configuration is **not** splittable into two groups of sum \(\le 11\).  
Let
\[
M=\max\Bigl\{\sum_{i\in I} a_i:\ \sum_{i\in I} a_i\le 11\Bigr\}
\]
be the largest achievable subset sum not exceeding \(11\), and pick a subset \(A\) attaining \(M\).

If a valid split existed, we could choose one group with sum \(x\in[T-11,11]\). Since no split exists, **no** subset sum can lie in \([T-11,11]\), hence in particular
\[
M < T-11. \tag{1}
\]

Now look at the remaining elements \(R\) (those not in \(A\)). Their total sum is
\[
\sum_{r\in R} r = T-M.
\]
From (1), we get \(T-M>11\), so
\[
\sum_{r\in R} r > 11. \tag{2}
\]

Also, by maximality of \(M\): for any \(r\in R\), adding \(r\) to \(A\) would exceed \(11\). Thus
\[
M+r>11\quad\Rightarrow\quad r>11-M. \tag{3}
\]

Because every \(r\in R\) is \(\le 1\), (2) implies \(|R|\ge 12\) (since \(11\) numbers in \([0,1]\) sum to at most \(11\)).

Using (3), each of these at least \(12\) numbers exceeds \(11-M\), hence
\[
T = M + \sum_{r\in R} r \;>\; M + 12(11-M)=132-11M. \tag{4}
\]

Now combine (1) with (4). From \(M<T-11\) we get \(-11M>-11(T-11)=-11T+121\), so
\[
132-11M \;>\; 132+(-11T+121)=253-11T.
\]
Thus (4) gives
\[
T > 253-11T \quad\Rightarrow\quad 12T>253 \quad\Rightarrow\quad T>\frac{253}{12}.
\]

**Conclusion:** If splitting is impossible, then necessarily
\[
T>\frac{253}{12}. \tag{5}
\]
So **every** multiset with \(T\le \frac{253}{12}\) is always splittable.

---

## 2) Sharpness (cannot do better)

Take \(23\) equal numbers
\[
a_1=\cdots=a_{23}=\frac{11}{12}+\varepsilon\quad(\varepsilon>0).
\]
Then the total is \(T=23\left(\frac{11}{12}+\varepsilon\right)=\frac{253}{12}+23\varepsilon\).

In any division of \(23\) items into two groups, one group has at least \(12\) items, hence its sum is
\[
\ge 12\left(\frac{11}{12}+\varepsilon\right)=11+12\varepsilon>11,
\]
so such a split is impossible.

Therefore, for any \(S>\frac{253}{12}\), choosing \(\varepsilon>0\) small enough so that \(T\le S\) produces a counterexample. Hence no \(S>\frac{253}{12}\) can have the “always possible” property.

---

## Final answer

\[
\boxed{\frac{253}{12}}
\]

Let the numbers be \(a_1,\dots,a_n\in[0,1]\) with total sum \(T=\sum a_i\). We want the largest \(S\) such that **every** multiset with \(T\le S\) can be partitioned into two groups each of sum \(\le 11\).

---

## 1) An upper bound (construct a counterexample when \(S\) is too large)

Assume \(S>\frac{253}{12}\). Take \(23\) equal numbers
\[
a_1=\cdots=a_{23}=\frac{S}{23}.
\]
Then
\[
\frac{S}{23}>\frac{1}{23}\cdot \frac{253}{12}=\frac{11}{12}.
\]
In any division into two groups, one group contains at least \(12\) of the \(23\) numbers, hence that group has sum
\[
>12\cdot \frac{11}{12}=11,
\]
so it is impossible to keep both group sums \(\le 11\).

Therefore, no \(S>\frac{253}{12}\) can work, so
\[
S_{\max}\le \frac{253}{12}.
\]

---

## 2) A matching lower bound (show \(S=\frac{253}{12}\) always works)

Now assume \(T\le \frac{253}{12}\). We show we can always split into two groups of sums \(\le 11\).

### Case A: \(T\le 21\)
Build one group by adding numbers until adding the next would exceed \(11\). Let the obtained sum be \(s\le 11\). Since the next number is \(\le 1\), we must have \(s>10\). Then the other group has sum
\[
T-s \le 21-10 = 11.
\]
So the split exists.

Hence the only remaining range is \(21<T\le \frac{253}{12}\).

---

### Case B: \(21<T\le \frac{253}{12}\)
Call a number **large** if \(> \frac{11}{12}\), otherwise **small** (\(\le \frac{11}{12}\)).

#### Subcase B1: There are at least \(11\) large numbers
Pick any \(11\) large numbers. Their sum is
\[
>11\cdot \frac{11}{12}=\frac{121}{12},
\]
and also \(\le 11\) because each \(\le 1\). Put them in group 1. Then group 2 has sum
\[
T-\text{(group 1)} \le \frac{253}{12}-\frac{121}{12}=11.
\]
So we are done.

#### Subcase B2: There are at most \(10\) large numbers
Let \(T_{\text{small}}\) be the total sum of the small numbers.

- If \(T_{\text{small}}\ge \frac{121}{12}\): pack small numbers greedily into group 1 until you cannot add another small number without exceeding \(11\). The remaining capacity is then \(<\frac{11}{12}\), so the achieved sum \(s\) satisfies
  \[
  s>11-\frac{11}{12}=\frac{121}{12}.
  \]
  Hence group 2 has sum
  \[
  T-s \le \frac{253}{12}-\frac{121}{12}=11.
  \]
  Done.

- If \(T_{\text{small}}<\frac{121}{12}\): then, since there are at most \(10\) large numbers and each is \(\le 1\),
  \[
  T \le T_{\text{small}} + 10 < \frac{121}{12}+10=\frac{241}{12}<21,
  \]
  contradicting \(T>21\). So this cannot happen in Case B.

Thus in all possibilities with \(T\le \frac{253}{12}\), the desired partition exists.

Therefore,
\[
S_{\max}\ge \frac{253}{12}.
\]

---

## 3) Conclusion
Combining the upper and lower bounds gives
\[
S_{\max}=\frac{253}{12}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{253}{12}}
\]

### Step 1: We may assume \(a_i\ge 0\)

The expression only depends on \(a_i\) through squares inside \(\sqrt{a_1^2+\cdots+a_k^2}\), hence it is unchanged if we replace each \(a_i\) by \(|a_i|\).

Let \(S=\sum_{i=1}^n |a_i|\ge 1\), and define \(a_i'=\dfrac{|a_i|}{S}\). Then \(\sum a_i'=1\), and for every \(k\),
\[
\sqrt{(a_1')^2+\cdots+(a_k')^2}=\frac1S\sqrt{a_1^2+\cdots+a_k^2},
\]
so every \(b_k\) (and thus the whole objective) scales by \(1/S\le 1\). Therefore the minimum is attained for some choice with \(a_i\ge 0\). We assume this from now on.

Let
\[
c_k=\sqrt{1-\frac1{16^k}}\quad\Rightarrow\quad b_k=c_k\sqrt{a_1^2+\cdots+a_k^2}.
\]

---

### Step 2: A Cauchy–Schwarz lower bound for each \(b_k\)

For each \(k\), consider the vector
\[
v^{(k)}=\left(\frac1{4^{k-1}},\frac1{4^{k-2}},\dots,\frac14,1\right)\in\mathbb R^k.
\]
By Cauchy–Schwarz (and \(a_i\ge 0\), so dot products are nonnegative),
\[
\sqrt{a_1^2+\cdots+a_k^2}\;\ge\;\frac{\sum_{i=1}^k a_i\,4^{-(k-i)}}{\|v^{(k)}\|}.
\]
Compute
\[
\|v^{(k)}\|^2=\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}\frac1{16^j}=\frac{1-16^{-k}}{1-16^{-1}}
=\frac{16}{15}\left(1-\frac1{16^k}\right)=\frac{16}{15}\,c_k^2,
\]
hence \(\|v^{(k)}\|=\frac4{\sqrt{15}}c_k\). Therefore
\[
b_k=c_k\sqrt{a_1^2+\cdots+a_k^2}
\ge c_k\cdot \frac{\sum_{i=1}^k a_i\,4^{-(k-i)}}{\frac4{\sqrt{15}}c_k}
=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{4}\sum_{i=1}^k \frac{a_i}{4^{k-i}}.
\]
So for all \(k\),
\[
\boxed{\;\;b_k\ge \frac{\sqrt{15}}{4}\left(a_k+\frac{a_{k-1}}4+\cdots+\frac{a_1}{4^{k-1}}\right).\;\;}
\]

---

### Step 3: Sum the inequalities with the given weights

Let
\[
F=b_1+\cdots+b_{n-1}+\frac43\,b_n.
\]
Using the bound above for each \(b_k\),
\[
F\ge \frac{\sqrt{15}}{4}\left[\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^k \frac{a_i}{4^{k-i}}+\frac43\sum_{i=1}^n\frac{a_i}{4^{n-i}}\right].
\]
Fix an index \(i\). Its coefficient in the first double sum is
\[
\sum_{k=i}^{n-1}\frac1{4^{k-i}}=\sum_{j=0}^{n-1-i}\frac1{4^j}
=\frac{1-(1/4)^{\,n-i}}{1-1/4}=\frac43\left(1-\frac1{4^{n-i}}\right).
\]
Its coefficient in the second sum is \(\frac43\cdot \frac1{4^{n-i}}\). Adding them gives exactly \(\frac43\). Hence the bracket equals
\[
\frac43\sum_{i=1}^n a_i=\frac43\cdot 1=\frac43.
\]
Therefore,
\[
F\ge \frac{\sqrt{15}}{4}\cdot \frac43=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}.
\]

So the minimum is at least \(\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}\).

---

### Step 4: Achieving equality

Equality in Cauchy–Schwarz for each \(k\) requires \((a_1,\dots,a_k)\) proportional to \(v^{(k)}\), i.e.
\[
(a_1,\dots,a_k)=t_k\left(\frac1{4^{k-1}},\dots,1\right),
\]
which forces \(a_{i+1}=4a_i\) for all \(i\). Thus take
\[
a_i=\lambda\,4^{i-1}\quad (1\le i\le n).
\]
The condition \(\sum_{i=1}^n a_i=1\) gives
\[
\lambda\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}4^i=\lambda\frac{4^n-1}{3}=1
\quad\Rightarrow\quad \lambda=\frac{3}{4^n-1}.
\]
So choose
\[
a_i=\frac{3\cdot 4^{i-1}}{4^n-1}.
\]
Then equality holds in every step above, hence the lower bound is attained and is the true minimum.

---

\[
\boxed{\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}
\]

Let  
\[
S_k=\sqrt{a_1^2+\cdots+a_k^2}\qquad(1\le k\le n),
\]
so \(b_k=\sqrt{1-16^{-k}}\;S_k\). Also define
\[
P_k=\sum_{i=1}^k 4^{\,i-1}a_i .
\]

### 1) A useful rewrite of \(\sqrt{1-16^{-k}}\)
Using \(1-r^k=(1-r)(1+r+\cdots+r^{k-1})\) with \(r=\frac1{16}\),
\[
1-16^{-k}=\frac{15}{16}\left(1+\frac1{16}+\cdots+\frac1{16^{k-1}}\right).
\]
Hence
\[
\sqrt{1-16^{-k}}=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{4}\sqrt{\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}16^{-j}}.
\]

### 2) Lower bound each \(b_k\) by Cauchy–Schwarz
Apply Cauchy–Schwarz to vectors \((a_1,\dots,a_k)\) and \(\left(4^{-(k-1)},4^{-(k-2)},\dots,4^0\right)\):
\[
\left(\sum_{i=1}^k a_i^2\right)\left(\sum_{i=1}^k 16^{-(k-i)}\right)\ge
\left(\sum_{i=1}^k a_i\cdot 4^{-(k-i)}\right)^2.
\]
So
\[
S_k\sqrt{\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}16^{-j}}\ \ge\ \left|\sum_{i=1}^k \frac{a_i}{4^{k-i}}\right|.
\]
Multiply by \(\frac{\sqrt{15}}{4}\) and use \(|x|\ge x\):
\[
b_k=\sqrt{1-16^{-k}}\,S_k
\ge \frac{\sqrt{15}}{4}\sum_{i=1}^k \frac{a_i}{4^{k-i}}
=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{4^k}\sum_{i=1}^k 4^{i-1}a_i
=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{4^k}P_k.
\]

### 3) Sum these bounds and telescope the coefficients
Therefore
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} b_k+\frac{4}{3}b_n
\ge
\sqrt{15}\left(\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\frac{P_k}{4^k}+\frac{4}{3}\cdot\frac{P_n}{4^n}\right).
\]
Now expand in terms of \(a_i\). For a fixed \(i\), \(a_i\) appears in \(P_k\) for all \(k\ge i\). Its total coefficient inside the parentheses is
\[
\sum_{k=i}^{n-1}\frac{4^{i-1}}{4^k}+\frac{4}{3}\cdot\frac{4^{i-1}}{4^n}
=
4^{i-1}\sum_{k=i}^{n-1}4^{-k}+\frac{4^{i-n}}{3}.
\]
But
\[
\sum_{k=i}^{n-1}4^{-k}=4^{-i}\frac{1-4^{-(n-i)}}{1-\frac14}
=\frac{1-4^{-(n-i)}}{3\cdot 4^{i-1}},
\]
so the coefficient becomes
\[
\frac{1-4^{-(n-i)}}{3}+\frac{4^{-(n-i)}}{3}=\frac13.
\]
Hence the whole expression is
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} b_k+\frac{4}{3}b_n
\ge
\sqrt{15}\cdot \sum_{i=1}^n \frac13 a_i
=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}\left(\sum_{i=1}^n a_i\right)
=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}.
\]

### 4) Achieving equality
Equality in Cauchy–Schwarz for every \(k\) holds when \((a_1,\dots,a_k)\) is proportional to \((1,4,\dots,4^{k-1})\), i.e.
\[
a_i=t\,4^{i-1}\quad(1\le i\le n).
\]
Imposing \(\sum_{i=1}^n a_i=1\) gives
\[
t\sum_{i=1}^n 4^{i-1}=t\cdot \frac{4^n-1}{3}=1
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
t=\frac{3}{4^n-1}.
\]
For this choice all \(P_k>0\), so the \(|x|\ge x\) step is also tight, and the lower bound \(\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}\) is attained.

Therefore the minimum value is  
\[
\boxed{\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}.
\]

Let
\[
s_k=\sqrt{a_1^2+a_2^2+\cdots+a_k^2}\quad (1\le k\le n),
\]
so that
\[
b_k=\sqrt{1-\frac1{16^k}}\;s_k.
\]
Also note
\[
\sqrt{1-\frac1{16^k}}=\sqrt{\frac{16^k-1}{16^k}}=\frac{\sqrt{16^k-1}}{4^k}.
\]

Define the objective
\[
F=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} b_k+\frac43 b_n
=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\frac{\sqrt{16^k-1}}{4^k}s_k+\frac43\cdot \frac{\sqrt{16^n-1}}{4^n}s_n.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1) A key choice of unit vectors

For each \(k\), define a vector \(u^{(k)}\in\mathbb R^k\) by
\[
u^{(k)}=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{\sqrt{16^k-1}}\,(1,4,4^2,\dots,4^{k-1}).
\]
Its norm is
\[
\|u^{(k)}\|^2=\frac{15}{16^k-1}\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}16^j
=\frac{15}{16^k-1}\cdot\frac{16^k-1}{15}=1,
\]
so \(\|u^{(k)}\|=1\).

Let \(x_k=(a_1,\dots,a_k)\in\mathbb R^k\). By Cauchy–Schwarz,
\[
s_k=\|x_k\|\ge u^{(k)}\cdot x_k=\sum_{i=1}^k u^{(k)}_i\,a_i.
\]
Multiplying by \(\frac{\sqrt{16^k-1}}{4^k}\) gives, for \(k\le n-1\),
\[
b_k=\frac{\sqrt{16^k-1}}{4^k}s_k\ge \frac{\sqrt{16^k-1}}{4^k}\sum_{i=1}^k u^{(k)}_i a_i.
\]
Similarly,
\[
\frac43 b_n=\frac43\cdot\frac{\sqrt{16^n-1}}{4^n}s_n
\ge \frac43\cdot\frac{\sqrt{16^n-1}}{4^n}\sum_{i=1}^n u^{(n)}_i a_i.
\]

Now compute the products: for \(i\le k\),
\[
\frac{\sqrt{16^k-1}}{4^k}\,u^{(k)}_i
=\frac{\sqrt{16^k-1}}{4^k}\cdot \frac{\sqrt{15}}{\sqrt{16^k-1}}\,4^{i-1}
=\sqrt{15}\,4^{i-1-k}.
\]
For the last term,
\[
\frac43\cdot\frac{\sqrt{16^n-1}}{4^n}\,u^{(n)}_i
=\frac43\cdot \sqrt{15}\,4^{i-1-n}
=\sqrt{15}\cdot \frac13\,4^{i-n}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2) Summing and telescoping to a constant coefficient

Summing all lower bounds,
\[
F\ge \sum_{i=1}^n a_i\Bigg(\sum_{k=i}^{n-1}\sqrt{15}\,4^{i-1-k}\;+\;\sqrt{15}\cdot\frac13\,4^{i-n}\Bigg).
\]
Factor out \(\sqrt{15}\). The inner sum is
\[
\sum_{k=i}^{n-1}4^{i-1-k}=\sum_{t=0}^{n-1-i}4^{-1-t}
=\frac14\cdot\frac{1-4^{-(n-i)}}{1-\frac14}
=\frac13\left(1-4^{i-n}\right).
\]
Therefore, for every \(i\),
\[
\sum_{k=i}^{n-1}4^{i-1-k}+\frac13\,4^{i-n}
=\frac13\left(1-4^{i-n}\right)+\frac13\,4^{i-n}
=\frac13.
\]
So each \(a_i\) has the same coefficient \(\sqrt{15}/3\), and hence
\[
F\ge \sum_{i=1}^n a_i\cdot \frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}\left(a_1+\cdots+a_n\right)=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}.
\]

Thus the minimum value is at least \(\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3) Achieving equality

Equality in each Cauchy–Schwarz step holds if \(x_k\) is parallel to \(u^{(k)}\) for every \(k\), i.e. if
\[
(a_1,\dots,a_k)\propto (1,4,4^2,\dots,4^{k-1})\quad \forall k.
\]
This forces \(a_i\) to be in geometric progression with ratio \(4\). Take
\[
a_i=\frac{3\cdot 4^{i-1}}{4^n-1}\quad (1\le i\le n),
\]
for which \(\sum_{i=1}^n a_i=1\). Then indeed \((a_1,\dots,a_k)\) is a scalar multiple of \((1,4,\dots,4^{k-1})\) for each \(k\), so all inequalities above become equalities, and therefore \(F=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
