### 1) Basic consequences of the condition
Given \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\) for all \(i,j\):

- Taking \(i=j\) gives \(|A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i\).  
  So each \(A_i\) has exactly \(i\) elements.

- If \(i\mid j\), then \(\gcd(i,j)=i\), so
  \[
  |A_i\cap A_j|=i=|A_i|\implies A_i\subseteq A_j.
  \]
  In particular, for divisors of \(250\), we have nesting along divisibility.

Let \(m_d=\max A_d\) for \(d\mid 250\). We want to minimize \(\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d\).

The divisors of \(250\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

Also, always \(m_d\ge d\) because \(A_d\) has \(d\) distinct positive integers.

---

### 2) Lower bounds that force the sum to be at least \(499\)

#### (a) Trivial terms
\[
m_1\ge 1,\qquad m_{250}\ge 250.
\]

#### (b) Bounding \(m_2+m_5\)
We know \(|A_2\cap A_5|=\gcd(2,5)=1\), so
\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=|A_2|+|A_5|-|A_2\cap A_5|=2+5-1=6.
\]
All elements of \(A_2\cup A_5\) are \(\le \max(m_2,m_5)\), so a set of 6 distinct positive integers forces
\[
\max(m_2,m_5)\ge 6.
\]
Since \(m_2\ge 2\) and at least one of \(m_2,m_5\) is \(\ge 6\), the minimum possible sum is
\[
m_2+m_5\ge 2+6=8.
\]

#### (c) Bounding \(m_{10}+m_{25}\)
Here \(|A_{10}\cap A_{25}|=\gcd(10,25)=5\), hence
\[
|A_{10}\setminus A_{25}|=|A_{10}|-|A_{10}\cap A_{25}|=10-5=5.
\]
Let \(m_{25}=M\). Then \(A_{25}\subseteq [M]\) has 25 elements, so among \(\{1,2,\dots,M\}\) there are only \(M-25\) numbers **not** in \(A_{25}\). Those are the only candidates \(\le M\) for elements of \(A_{10}\setminus A_{25}\).

- If \(M\ge 30\), then \(m_{10}\ge 10\) gives \(m_{10}+m_{25}\ge 10+30=40\).
- If \(M<30\), then \(M-25<5\), so at least
  \[
  5-(M-25)=30-M
  \]
  elements of \(A_{10}\setminus A_{25}\) must exceed \(M\). The smallest possible way to place \(30-M\) distinct integers above \(M\) forces
  \[
  m_{10}\ge M+(30-M)=30,
  \]
  hence \(m_{10}+m_{25}\ge 30+25=55>40\).

So in all cases,
\[
m_{10}+m_{25}\ge 40.
\]

#### (d) Bounding \(m_{50}+m_{125}\)
Similarly, \(|A_{50}\cap A_{125}|=\gcd(50,125)=25\), so
\[
|A_{50}\setminus A_{125}|=50-25=25.
\]
Let \(m_{125}=N\). Then \(A_{125}\subseteq [N]\) has 125 elements, leaving only \(N-125\) numbers \(\le N\) outside \(A_{125}\) to accommodate \(A_{50}\setminus A_{125}\).

- If \(N\ge 150\), then \(m_{50}\ge 50\) gives \(m_{50}+m_{125}\ge 50+150=200\).
- If \(N<150\), then \(N-125<25\), so at least \(25-(N-125)=150-N\) elements of \(A_{50}\setminus A_{125}\) must exceed \(N\), forcing \(m_{50}\ge 150\), hence \(m_{50}+m_{125}>200\).

Therefore,
\[
m_{50}+m_{125}\ge 200.
\]

#### (e) Combine all bounds
Group the desired sum as
\[
(m_1+m_{250})+(m_2+m_5)+(m_{10}+m_{25})+(m_{50}+m_{125}).
\]
Using the bounds:
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d \;\ge\; (1+250)+8+40+200=499.
\]

So the minimum possible value is **at least \(499\)**.

---

### 3) Construction achieving \(499\)
We now exhibit sets meeting the gcd-intersection rule and attaining sum \(499\).

Partition \(\{1,2,\dots,250\}\) into disjoint blocks of sizes \(\varphi(d)\) for \(d\mid 250\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\} &&(|B_1|=1=\varphi(1))\\
B_2&=\{2\} &&(|B_2|=1=\varphi(2))\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\} &&(|B_5|=4=\varphi(5))\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\} &&(|B_{10}|=4=\varphi(10))\\
B_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots,30\} &&(|B_{25}|=20=\varphi(25))\\
B_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots,50\} &&(|B_{50}|=20=\varphi(50))\\
B_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots,150\} &&(|B_{125}|=100=\varphi(125))\\
B_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots,250\} &&(|B_{250}|=100=\varphi(250)).
\end{aligned}
\]

Define, for every \(n\),
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]
Then for any \(i,j\),
\[
A_i\cap A_j=\bigcup_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} B_d
\]
(disjoint union), so
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} |B_d|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j),
\]
using \(\sum_{d\mid n}\varphi(d)=n\).

Now compute maxima for \(d\mid 250\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
\max A_1&=1\\
\max A_2&=2\\
\max A_5&=6\\
\max A_{10}&=10\\
\max A_{25}&=30\\
\max A_{50}&=50\\
\max A_{125}&=150\\
\max A_{250}&=250.
\end{aligned}
\]
Sum:
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This matches the lower bound, so it is minimal.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

### 1) Basic consequences of the intersection condition
Given \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\), in particular \(|A_n|=\gcd(n,n)=n\).

Also, if \(a\mid b\), then \(\gcd(a,b)=a\), so
\[
|A_a\cap A_b|=a=|A_a|\implies A_a\subseteq A_b.
\]
Hence for the divisors of \(250\),
\[
A_1\subset A_2\subset A_{10}\subset A_{50}\subset A_{250},
\]
\[
A_1\subset A_5\subset A_{25}\subset A_{125}\subset A_{250},
\]
and also \(A_{25}\subset A_{50}\).

Let \(M_n:=\max A_n\).

Trivially, since \(A_n\) has \(n\) distinct positive integers,
\[
M_n\ge n\quad \text{for all }n.
\]
So \(M_{250}\ge 250\), hence in any minimum we must have \(M_{250}=250\).

---

### 2) A useful counting lemma
If a set \(X\) has \(|X|=m\) and \(\max X=M\), then among \(\{1,2,\dots,M\}\) there are exactly \(M-m\) integers **not** in \(X\).

Now let \(Y\) be another set, and suppose \(Y\) has \(r\) elements outside \(X\), i.e. \(|Y\setminus X|=r\). At most \(M-m\) of those \(r\) elements can lie in \(\{1,2,\dots,M\}\) (because there are only \(M-m\) such numbers not in \(X\)). Therefore at least
\[
r-(M-m)
\]
elements of \(Y\) must exceed \(M\), forcing
\[
\max Y \;\ge\; M + \max\bigl(0,\; r-(M-m)\bigr).
\]

We apply this repeatedly with \(X\) being the “smaller” set.

---

### 3) Forced lower bounds for \(M_5, M_{25}, M_{125}\)

#### (a) Bounding \(M_5\) using \(A_2\)
We have \(|A_2|=2\), \(|A_5|=5\), and \(|A_2\cap A_5|=\gcd(2,5)=1\), so
\[
|A_5\setminus A_2|=5-1=4.
\]
Let \(M_2=\max A_2\). By the lemma with \(X=A_2\), \(m=2\), \(M=M_2\), \(r=4\):
\[
M_5 \ge M_2 + \max(0,\,4-(M_2-2))=M_2+\max(0,\,6-M_2).
\]
If \(M_2\le 6\), this gives \(M_5\ge 6\). If \(M_2\ge 7\), then already \(M_2+M_5\ge 7+5>2+6\), so the best occurs with \(M_2\) minimal, i.e. \(M_2=2\), and then \(M_5\ge 6\).

So in any minimum:
\[
M_2\ge 2,\qquad M_5\ge 6.
\]

#### (b) Bounding \(M_{25}\) using \(A_{10}\)
\(|A_{10}|=10\), \(|A_{25}|=25\), and \(|A_{10}\cap A_{25}|=\gcd(10,25)=5\), so
\[
|A_{25}\setminus A_{10}|=25-5=20.
\]
Let \(M_{10}=\max A_{10}\). Lemma with \(X=A_{10}\), \(m=10\), \(M=M_{10}\), \(r=20\):
\[
M_{25}\ge M_{10}+\max(0,\,20-(M_{10}-10))=M_{10}+\max(0,\,30-M_{10}).
\]
If \(M_{10}\le 30\), then \(M_{25}\ge 30\). If \(M_{10}\ge 31\), then \(M_{10}+M_{25}\ge 31+25>10+30\), so the best occurs at \(M_{10}=10\) and then forces \(M_{25}\ge 30\).

Thus:
\[
M_{10}\ge 10,\qquad M_{25}\ge 30.
\]

#### (c) Bounding \(M_{125}\) using \(A_{50}\)
\(|A_{50}|=50\), \(|A_{125}|=125\), and \(|A_{50}\cap A_{125}|=\gcd(50,125)=25\), so
\[
|A_{125}\setminus A_{50}|=125-25=100.
\]
Let \(M_{50}=\max A_{50}\). Lemma with \(X=A_{50}\), \(m=50\), \(M=M_{50}\), \(r=100\):
\[
M_{125}\ge M_{50}+\max(0,\,100-(M_{50}-50))=M_{50}+\max(0,\,150-M_{50}).
\]
If \(M_{50}\le 150\), then \(M_{125}\ge 150\). If \(M_{50}\ge 151\), then \(M_{50}+M_{125}\ge 151+125>50+150\), so the best occurs at \(M_{50}=50\), forcing \(M_{125}\ge 150\).

Thus:
\[
M_{50}\ge 50,\qquad M_{125}\ge 150.
\]

---

### 4) Lower bound for the required sum
Collecting the best-possible lower bounds:
\[
M_1\ge 1,\; M_2\ge 2,\; M_5\ge 6,\; M_{10}\ge 10,\; M_{25}\ge 30,\; M_{50}\ge 50,\; M_{125}\ge 150,\; M_{250}\ge 250.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} \max A_d \;\ge\; 1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250 \;=\; 499.
\]

---

### 5) Construction achieving 499 (so it is minimal)
Use the standard totient-block construction: create disjoint blocks \(B_d\) of sizes \(|B_d|=\varphi(d)\) for each \(d\mid 250\), and define
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]
Then \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j)\).

For \(250\), the divisors and totients are:
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\ \varphi(2)=1,\ \varphi(5)=4,\ \varphi(10)=4,\ \varphi(25)=20,\ \varphi(50)=20,\ \varphi(125)=100,\ \varphi(250)=100.
\]
Label the blocks by actual integers as:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&B_1=\{1\},\quad B_2=\{2\},\quad B_5=\{3,4,5,6\},\quad B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
&B_{25}=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\quad B_{50}=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
&B_{125}=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\quad B_{250}=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Then:
- \(A_1=B_1\Rightarrow \max A_1=1\)
- \(A_2=B_1\cup B_2\Rightarrow \max A_2=2\)
- \(A_5=B_1\cup B_5\Rightarrow \max A_5=6\)
- \(A_{10}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}=\{1,\dots,10\}\Rightarrow \max A_{10}=10\)
- \(A_{25}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\Rightarrow \max A_{25}=30\)
- \(A_{50}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50}=\{1,\dots,50\}\Rightarrow \max A_{50}=50\)
- \(A_{125}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125}\Rightarrow \max A_{125}=150\)
- \(A_{250}=\{1,\dots,250\}\Rightarrow \max A_{250}=250\)

So the sum is exactly \(499\), matching the lower bound.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let the circle \(\Omega\) have center \(O\) and radius \(11\). The three congruent circles have radius \(2\), so if their centers are \(A,B,C\), then
\[
OA=OB=OC=11-2=9.
\]
Thus \(A,B,C\) lie on the circle of radius \(9\) centered at \(O\), i.e. \(O\) is the circumcenter of \(\triangle ABC\) and its circumradius is \(9\).

### 1) Convert the given chord lengths to distances from \(O\)
For any chord of \(\Omega\) of length \(L\), if its perpendicular distance from \(O\) is \(d\), then
\[
L = 2\sqrt{11^2-d^2}.
\]
Given \(L=4\sqrt{30}\), we get
\[
4\sqrt{30}=2\sqrt{121-d^2}\;\Rightarrow\;2\sqrt{30}=\sqrt{121-d^2}
\]
\[
120=121-d^2\;\Rightarrow\;d^2=1\;\Rightarrow\;d=1.
\]
So each of the two known chords is at distance \(1\) from \(O\).

### 2) Relate each chord to the corresponding side of \(\triangle ABC\)
Consider the chord tangent to the two small circles centered at \(B\) and \(C\). Since the two small circles have equal radius \(2\), their common internal tangent (the one forming the given triangle containing \(O\)) is **parallel to** \(BC\) and at perpendicular distance \(2\) from both \(B\) and \(C\). Because \(B\) and \(C\) lie on the line \(BC\), this means the chord’s supporting line is parallel to \(BC\) and shifted inward by \(2\).

Let \(h_a\) be the perpendicular distance from \(O\) to the line \(BC\). Then the distance from \(O\) to the corresponding chord is
\[
d_a = h_a - 2.
\]

But in a triangle with circumradius \(R\), the distance from the circumcenter to side \(BC\) equals \(R\cos A\). Here \(R=9\), so
\[
h_a = 9\cos A \quad\Rightarrow\quad d_a = 9\cos A -2.
\]
Similarly,
\[
d_b=9\cos B-2,\qquad d_c=9\cos C-2.
\]

### 3) Use the two equal chords
The two chords of length \(4\sqrt{30}\) have distance \(1\) from \(O\), so for two of the angles (say \(A\) and \(B\)):
\[
9\cos A -2 = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; 9\cos A = 3 \;\Rightarrow\; \cos A=\frac13,
\]
and similarly \(\cos B=\frac13\). Hence \(A=B\).

Then \(C=180^\circ-2A\), so
\[
\cos C = \cos(180^\circ-2A) = -\cos(2A).
\]
Using \(\cos(2A)=2\cos^2A-1\) with \(\cos A=\frac13\):
\[
\cos(2A)=2\cdot\frac{1}{9}-1=\frac{2}{9}-1=-\frac{7}{9},
\]
\[
\cos C = -\left(-\frac{7}{9}\right)=\frac{7}{9}.
\]

Thus
\[
d_c = 9\cos C -2 = 9\cdot\frac{7}{9}-2 = 7-2=5.
\]

### 4) Convert back to the third chord length
\[
L_3 = 2\sqrt{11^2-5^2} = 2\sqrt{121-25} = 2\sqrt{96}=8\sqrt6.
\]

\[
\boxed{8\sqrt6}
\]

Let the circle \(\Omega\) have center \(O=(0,0)\) and radius \(R=11\). Each small circle has radius \(r=2\), and since each is internally tangent to \(\Omega\), each small-circle center lies on the circle of radius
\[
R-r=9
\]
about \(O\).

For a chord of \(\Omega\) at distance \(d\) from \(O\), its length is
\[
L=2\sqrt{R^2-d^2}=2\sqrt{121-d^2}.
\]
Given \(L=4\sqrt{30}\),
\[
4\sqrt{30}=2\sqrt{121-d^2}\implies 2\sqrt{30}=\sqrt{121-d^2}\implies d^2=1\implies d=1.
\]
So the two given chords each have distance \(1\) from \(O\).

---

### 1) Fix the first chord and find the two corresponding circle centers
Choose coordinates so that one of the given chords is the line
\[
\ell_1:\ y=1
\]
(distance \(1\) from \(O\)). This chord is tangent to two radius-2 circles; their centers must be distance \(2\) from \(\ell_1\) on the same side as \(O\) (so that the circles lie inside the triangle containing \(O\)). Hence their centers have \(y\)-coordinate
\[
y=1-2=-1.
\]
Intersecting with \(x^2+y^2=81\) gives
\[
x^2+(-1)^2=81 \implies x^2=80 \implies x=\pm 4\sqrt5.
\]
So the two centers are
\[
A=(4\sqrt5,-1),\qquad B=(-4\sqrt5,-1).
\]

---

### 2) Use the second given chord to determine the third center \(C\)
Let the second given chord be \(\ell_2\), also at distance \(1\) from \(O\). Write it in unit-normal form
\[
n_2\cdot x = 1,\quad \|n_2\|=1.
\]
Because \(\ell_2\) is tangent to the circles centered at \(B\) and \(C\), those centers lie on the parallel line \(n_2\cdot x = 1-2=-1\). In particular,
\[
n_2\cdot B=-1.
\]
Let \(n_2=(u,v)\) with \(u^2+v^2=1\). Using \(B=(-4\sqrt5,-1)\),
\[
u(-4\sqrt5)+v(-1)=-1\ \Longrightarrow\ 4\sqrt5\,u+v=1.
\]
With \(v=1-4\sqrt5\,u\) and \(u^2+v^2=1\),
\[
u^2+(1-4\sqrt5\,u)^2=1
\]
\[
u^2+1-8\sqrt5\,u+80u^2=1 \implies 81u^2-8\sqrt5\,u=0.
\]
Discarding the solution \(u=0\) (which would make \(\ell_2\) parallel to \(\ell_1\) and reuse \(A\)), we get
\[
u=\frac{8\sqrt5}{81},\qquad v=1-4\sqrt5\cdot\frac{8\sqrt5}{81}=1-\frac{160}{81}=-\frac{79}{81}.
\]
Thus
\[
n_2=\left(\frac{8\sqrt5}{81},-\frac{79}{81}\right).
\]
Now \(C\) is the *other* point (besides \(B\)) on \(x^2+y^2=81\) satisfying \(n_2\cdot (x,y)=-1\), i.e.
\[
\frac{8\sqrt5}{81}x-\frac{79}{81}y=-1\ \Longleftrightarrow\ 8\sqrt5\,x-79y=-81.
\]
Solving with \(x^2+y^2=81\) yields the second intersection
\[
C=\left(\frac{308\sqrt5}{81},\frac{239}{81}\right).
\]

---

### 3) Find the third chord distance \(d_3\)
The third chord \(\ell_3\) is tangent to the circles centered at \(A\) and \(C\). For equal radii, a common external tangent chord is parallel to \(\overline{AC}\), so its normal \(n_3\) is perpendicular to \(AC\).

Compute
\[
C-A=\left(\frac{308\sqrt5}{81}-4\sqrt5,\ \frac{239}{81}+1\right)
=\left(-\frac{16\sqrt5}{81},\frac{320}{81}\right),
\]
so a direction vector for \(AC\) is \((-\sqrt5,20)\), hence a perpendicular normal is \((20,\sqrt5)\). Normalize:
\[
\|(20,\sqrt5)\|=\sqrt{400+5}= \sqrt{405}=9\sqrt5,
\]
\[
n_3=\left(\frac{20}{9\sqrt5},\frac{\sqrt5}{9\sqrt5}\right)=\left(\frac{4\sqrt5}{9},\frac{1}{9}\right).
\]
For the chord line \(n_3\cdot x = d_3\), tangency to circles of radius \(2\) means
\[
n_3\cdot A = d_3-2 \quad (\text{and similarly for }C).
\]
Compute
\[
n_3\cdot A=\frac{4\sqrt5}{9}(4\sqrt5)+\frac{1}{9}(-1)=\frac{80}{9}-\frac{1}{9}=\frac{79}{9}.
\]
So
\[
d_3 = \frac{79}{9}+2 = \frac{97}{9}.
\]

---

### 4) Convert \(d_3\) to the third chord length
\[
L_3=2\sqrt{121-\left(\frac{97}{9}\right)^2}
=2\sqrt{\frac{9801-9409}{81}}
=2\sqrt{\frac{392}{81}}
=2\cdot \frac{14\sqrt2}{9}
=\frac{28\sqrt2}{9}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{28\sqrt2}{9}}
\]

Let the three radius–2 circles have centers \(A,B,C\).  
Since each is internally tangent to \(\Omega\) (radius \(11\), center \(O\)), we have
\[
OA=OB=OC=11-2=9,
\]
so \(A,B,C\) lie on the circle of radius \(9\) centered at \(O\).

For a pair, say \(A,B\), let \(\angle AOB=\alpha\). The chord of \(\Omega\) tangent to both circles is part of a line \(\ell_{AB}\) tangent to the two radius–2 circles.

### 1) Distance from \(O\) to \(\ell_{AB}\)
Because the two small circles have equal radii, a common (direct) tangent line to both circles is parallel to \(AB\), hence its normal is perpendicular to \(AB\). In the isosceles triangle \(AOB\), the perpendicular to \(AB\) through its midpoint is the angle bisector, so the normal to \(\ell_{AB}\) is along the bisector of \(\angle AOB\).

Let \(M\) be the midpoint of chord \(AB\) in the radius–9 circle. Then
\[
OM=9\cos\frac{\alpha}{2}.
\]
The line through \(A,B\) parallel to \(\ell_{AB}\) is \(AB\) itself, so \(\ell_{AB}\) is obtained by shifting \(AB\) toward \(O\) by \(2\) (since the line is tangent to the radius–2 circles). Thus the perpendicular distance from \(O\) to \(\ell_{AB}\) is
\[
d_{AB}=OM-2=9\cos\frac{\alpha}{2}-2.
\]

### 2) Use the given chord lengths
A chord of \(\Omega\) at distance \(d\) from \(O\) has length
\[
L=2\sqrt{11^2-d^2}=2\sqrt{121-d^2}.
\]
Given \(L=4\sqrt{30}\) for two chords:
\[
2\sqrt{121-d^2}=4\sqrt{30}\;\Rightarrow\;\sqrt{121-d^2}=2\sqrt{30}
\]
\[
121-d^2=120\;\Rightarrow\;d=1.
\]
So for those two pairs (say \(AB\) and \(BC\)),
\[
9\cos\frac{\alpha}{2}-2=1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \cos\frac{\alpha}{2}=\frac{1}{3},
\]
and similarly \(\cos\frac{\beta}{2}=\frac{1}{3}\) where \(\beta=\angle BOC\). Hence \(\alpha=\beta=2\arccos(1/3)\).

Let \(\gamma=\angle COA\). Since \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=2\pi\),
\[
\gamma=2\pi-2\alpha \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{\gamma}{2}=\pi-\alpha.
\]
Therefore
\[
\cos\frac{\gamma}{2}=\cos(\pi-\alpha)=-\cos\alpha.
\]
But with \(\cos(\alpha/2)=1/3\),
\[
\cos\alpha=\cos(2\cdot \alpha/2)=2\cos^2\frac{\alpha}{2}-1=2\cdot\frac{1}{9}-1=-\frac{7}{9}.
\]
So
\[
\cos\frac{\gamma}{2}=-\left(-\frac{7}{9}\right)=\frac{7}{9}.
\]

Thus the distance from \(O\) to the third chord-line \(\ell_{CA}\) is
\[
d_{CA}=9\cos\frac{\gamma}{2}-2=9\cdot\frac{7}{9}-2=5.
\]

### 3) Compute the third chord length
\[
L_3=2\sqrt{121-5^2}=2\sqrt{121-25}=2\sqrt{96}=8\sqrt{6}.
\]

\[
\boxed{8\sqrt{6}}
\]

Let the three small circles (radius \(2\)) have centers \(A,B,C\). Since each is internally tangent to \(\Omega\) (radius \(11\)) centered at \(O\),
\[
OA=OB=OC=11-2=9,
\]
so \(A,B,C\) lie on the circle of radius \(9\) centered at \(O\).

### 1) Convert chord length to distance from \(O\)
For any chord of \(\Omega\) at distance \(d\) from \(O\), its length is
\[
L=2\sqrt{11^2-d^2}.
\]
Given \(L=4\sqrt{30}\) for two chords,
\[
4\sqrt{30}=2\sqrt{121-d^2}\;\Rightarrow\;2\sqrt{30}=\sqrt{121-d^2}
\]
\[
120=121-d^2\;\Rightarrow\;d^2=1\;\Rightarrow\;d=1.
\]
So the two given chords are each at distance \(1\) from \(O\).

### 2) Relate \(d\) to the central angle between the two circle-centers it is tangent to
Consider a chord line \(\ell\) tangent to the two small circles centered at (say) \(A\) and \(B\). Since \(O\) lies inside the triangle formed by the three chord-lines, each chord-line has \(O\) on one side and the two tangent circles on the other side, so along the line’s outward unit normal \(\mathbf n\),
- the signed distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\) is \(d\),
- the signed distance from \(A\) (and from \(B\)) to \(\ell\) is \(2\) more, i.e. \(d+2\).

Thus
\[
\mathbf n\cdot A=\mathbf n\cdot B=d+2.
\]
But \(\mathbf n\cdot A = OA\cos\left(\frac{\angle AOB}{2}\right)=9\cos\left(\frac{\angle AOB}{2}\right)\) (the line’s normal bisects the angle between \(OA\) and \(OB\)). Hence, for \(\theta=\angle AOB\),
\[
9\cos\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=d+2.
\]

For each of the two known chords, \(d=1\), so
\[
9\cos\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=3 \;\Rightarrow\; \cos\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=\frac13.
\]
So for those two pairs of centers, the central angle equals
\[
\theta=2\arccos\left(\frac13\right).
\]

### 3) Find the remaining central angle and the remaining \(d\)
Around point \(O\), the three central angles between consecutive centers sum to \(2\pi\). Since two of them are \(\theta\), the third is
\[
\theta_3 = 2\pi - 2\theta.
\]
Then
\[
\frac{\theta_3}{2} = \pi - \theta \quad\Rightarrow\quad \cos\left(\frac{\theta_3}{2}\right)=\cos(\pi-\theta)=-\cos\theta.
\]
Compute \(\cos\theta\) from \(\cos(\theta/2)=1/3\):
\[
\cos\theta = 2\cos^2\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)-1 = 2\left(\frac13\right)^2-1=\frac{2}{9}-1=-\frac79.
\]
Therefore
\[
\cos\left(\frac{\theta_3}{2}\right)=-\cos\theta=\frac79.
\]
Use \(9\cos(\theta_3/2)=d_3+2\):
\[
9\cdot \frac79 = d_3+2 \;\Rightarrow\; 7=d_3+2 \;\Rightarrow\; d_3=5.
\]

### 4) Convert \(d_3\) to the third chord length
\[
L_3 = 2\sqrt{121-d_3^2}=2\sqrt{121-25}=2\sqrt{96}=8\sqrt6.
\]

\[
\boxed{8\sqrt6}
\]

Let the radius of \(\Omega\) be \(R=11\). A chord at (perpendicular) distance \(p\) from \(O\) has length
\[
L=2\sqrt{R^2-p^2}=2\sqrt{121-p^2}.
\]
So for \(L=4\sqrt{30}\),
\[
4\sqrt{30}=2\sqrt{121-p^2}\;\Longrightarrow\;121-p^2=120\;\Longrightarrow\;p=1.
\]

Each small circle has radius \(2\), so its center is \(9\) from \(O\). Thus the three small-circle centers \(A,B,C\) lie on the circle \(\omega\) of radius \(9\) centered at \(O\).

Because \(O\) is inside the triangle formed by the three chord-lines, each chord-line is a **common external** tangent to the two small circles it touches (both circles lie on the same side of that line, inside the triangle).

---

### 1) Translate a tangent line to a line through the two centers
If a chord-line \(\ell\) is tangent to the two radius-2 circles with centers \(X,Y\), then the line \(\ell'\) parallel to \(\ell\) passing through \(X\) and \(Y\) is exactly \(2\) units from \(\ell\). Hence, with respect to \(O\),
\[
\text{dist}(O,\ell')=\bigl|\text{dist}(O,\ell)-2\bigr|=|p-2|.
\]
But \(X,Y\in \omega\) (radius \(9\)), so \(XY\) is a chord of \(\omega\) cut by \(\ell'\), giving
\[
XY = 2\sqrt{9^2-\text{dist}(O,\ell')^2}
=2\sqrt{81-(p-2)^2}.
\]

For the two given chords, \(p=1\), so \(|p-2|=1\), and therefore their corresponding center-distances are
\[
XY = 2\sqrt{81-1}=2\sqrt{80}=8\sqrt5.
\]
WLOG, assume these two chords are tangent to the pairs \((A,B)\) and \((A,C)\). Then
\[
AB=AC=8\sqrt5.
\]

---

### 2) Place coordinates and find \(B,C\)
Put \(O=(0,0)\) and \(A=(9,0)\). Let \(\angle AOB=\theta\). Since \(AB\) is a chord of radius \(9\),
\[
AB = 18\sin\frac{\theta}{2} = 8\sqrt5
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\sin\frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{4\sqrt5}{9}.
\]
Then
\[
\cos\frac{\theta}{2}=\sqrt{1-\frac{80}{81}}=\frac{1}{9},
\]
so
\[
\cos\theta=\cos^2\frac{\theta}{2}-\sin^2\frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{1}{81}-\frac{80}{81}=-\frac{79}{81},\qquad
\sin\theta=2\sin\frac{\theta}{2}\cos\frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{8\sqrt5}{81}.
\]
Thus we may take (symmetric about the \(x\)-axis)
\[
B=\bigl(9\cos\theta,\,9\sin\theta\bigr)=\left(-\frac{79}{9},\,\frac{8\sqrt5}{9}\right),\quad
C=\left(-\frac{79}{9},\,-\frac{8\sqrt5}{9}\right).
\]

Hence \(BC\) is vertical and
\[
BC=\frac{16\sqrt5}{9}.
\]

---

### 3) The third chord-line and its distance from \(O\)
A common external tangent to the circles centered at \(B\) and \(C\) must be parallel to \(BC\), hence it is a vertical line \(x=k\). Tangency to the radius-2 circles means its distance to \(B\) (or \(C\)) is \(2\):
\[
|k - x_B| = 2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\left|k+\frac{79}{9}\right|=2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
k=-\frac{79}{9}\pm 2 \in \left\{-\frac{61}{9},\,-\frac{97}{9}\right\}.
\]
If \(k=-\frac{97}{9}\), this line lies to the left of the intersection of the other two sides and cannot form, together with them, a bounded triangle containing \(O\). Therefore we must take
\[
k=-\frac{61}{9}.
\]
So the distance from \(O\) to the third chord-line is
\[
p_3=\left|-\frac{61}{9}\right|=\frac{61}{9}.
\]

---

### 4) Convert \(p_3\) to the chord length
\[
L_3 = 2\sqrt{121-\left(\frac{61}{9}\right)^2}
=2\sqrt{121-\frac{3721}{81}}
=2\sqrt{\frac{9801-3721}{81}}
=2\sqrt{\frac{6080}{81}}
=\frac{2}{9}\sqrt{6080}.
\]
Since \(6080=64\cdot 95\), \(\sqrt{6080}=8\sqrt{95}\), so
\[
L_3=\frac{16\sqrt{95}}{9}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{16\sqrt{95}}{9}}
\]

Let the radius of \(\Omega\) be \(R=11\), and each small circle have radius \(r=2\).  
Since each small circle is internally tangent to \(\Omega\), the centers of the small circles lie on the circle of radius
\[
R-r=9
\]
centered at \(O\).

For any chord of \(\Omega\) cut by a line at distance \(d\) from \(O\), its length is
\[
L=2\sqrt{R^2-d^2}.
\]

### 1) Convert the two given chord lengths into distances from \(O\)
Given \(L=4\sqrt{30}\),
\[
4\sqrt{30}=2\sqrt{121-d^2}\;\Rightarrow\;2\sqrt{30}=\sqrt{121-d^2}
\]
\[
121-d^2=120\;\Rightarrow\;d^2=1\;\Rightarrow\;d=1.
\]
So the two given chords lie on lines at distance \(1\) from \(O\).

### 2) Use tangency to relate chord-lines to the circle centers
A chord-line tangent to a small circle of radius \(2\) means the center of that small circle lies on a line parallel to the chord-line, at perpendicular distance \(2\) from it (on the interior side).

Thus, if a chord-line is at distance \(1\) from \(O\), then the line parallel to it containing the two relevant small-circle centers is at distance \(1\) from \(O\) on the other side (since it is shifted by \(2\)). Concretely, we may place coordinates so that one chord-line is
\[
x=1,
\]
hence the two centers tangent to it lie on
\[
x=-1.
\]
Intersecting with \(x^2+y^2=81\) gives two centers:
\[
B=(-1,4\sqrt5),\quad C=(-1,-4\sqrt5).
\]

Now take the second given chord-line (also at distance \(1\) from \(O\)). Let its outward unit normal be
\[
n_2=(\cos\varphi,\sin\varphi),
\]
so the line containing the two corresponding centers is
\[
\cos\varphi\,x+\sin\varphi\,y=-1.
\]
Since this line passes through \(C=(-1,-4\sqrt5)\), solving yields
\[
\cos\varphi=-\frac{79}{81},\qquad \sin\varphi=\frac{8\sqrt5}{81},
\]
hence the line is
\[
-\frac{79}{81}x+\frac{8\sqrt5}{81}y=-1
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
-79x+8\sqrt5\,y=-81.
\]
Intersecting this with \(x^2+y^2=81\) gives the other point besides \(C\), namely
\[
A=\left(\frac{239}{81},\frac{308\sqrt5}{81}\right).
\]

So the three small-circle centers are \(A,B,C\) on the circle radius \(9\).

### 3) Find the third chord-line distance from \(O\)
The third chord-line is tangent to the circles centered at \(A\) and \(B\), so it is parallel to line \(AB\) and at perpendicular distance \(2\) from \(AB\) outward.

Compute direction of \(AB\):
\[
B-A=\left(-1-\frac{239}{81},\,4\sqrt5-\frac{308\sqrt5}{81}\right)
=\left(-\frac{320}{81},\,\frac{16\sqrt5}{81}\right),
\]
so a direction vector is \((-20,\sqrt5)\). A normal vector is therefore \((\sqrt5,20)\), whose unit version is
\[
n_3=\frac{1}{9\sqrt5}(\sqrt5,20)=\left(\frac19,\frac{20}{9\sqrt5}\right).
\]
Then the line \(AB\) has equation \(n_3\cdot x=k_3\) where
\[
k_3=n_3\cdot B=\frac19(-1)+\frac{20}{9\sqrt5}(4\sqrt5)=\frac{-1+80}{9}=\frac{79}{9}.
\]
Shifting outward by \(2\) gives the chord-line (tangent to the two circles):
\[
n_3\cdot x = k_3+2=\frac{79}{9}+2=\frac{97}{9}.
\]
Hence the distance from \(O\) to the third chord-line is
\[
d_3=\frac{97}{9}.
\]

### 4) Convert \(d_3\) into the third chord length
\[
L_3=2\sqrt{121-\left(\frac{97}{9}\right)^2}
=2\sqrt{\frac{9801-9409}{81}}
=2\sqrt{\frac{392}{81}}
=2\cdot\frac{14\sqrt2}{9}
=\frac{28\sqrt2}{9}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{28\sqrt2}{9}}
\]

Let the circle \(\Omega\) have center \(O\) and radius \(11\). Each small circle has radius \(2\) and is internally tangent to \(\Omega\), so each small circle’s center is at distance
\[
11-2=9
\]
from \(O\). Thus the three small-circle centers \(A,B,C\) lie on the circle \(\Gamma\) centered at \(O\) with radius \(9\).

---

### 1) Convert the given chord lengths into distances from \(O\)
If a chord of a circle of radius \(R\) has perpendicular distance \(d\) from the center, then its length is
\[
L=2\sqrt{R^2-d^2}.
\]
Given \(R=11\) and \(L=4\sqrt{30}\),
\[
4\sqrt{30}=2\sqrt{121-d^2}\;\Rightarrow\;2\sqrt{30}=\sqrt{121-d^2}
\]
\[
120=121-d^2\;\Rightarrow\;d^2=1\;\Rightarrow\;d=1.
\]
So each of the two known chords lies on a line at distance \(1\) from \(O\).

---

### 2) Use tangency to the small circles to shift those lines to chords of \(\Gamma\)
Take one of these chord-lines \(\ell\) (distance \(1\) from \(O\)), tangent to two small circles of radius \(2\).

Since \(O\) lies inside the triangle formed by the chord-lines, the small circles tangent to a given side must lie on the opposite side of that line from \(O\). Therefore the centers of the two tangent small circles lie on the line parallel to \(\ell\) that is \(2\) units farther from \(O\). Hence that parallel line is at distance
\[
1+2=3
\]
from \(O\).

So: **each given chord-line at distance \(1\) from \(O\) corresponds to a chord of \(\Gamma\) whose supporting line is at distance \(3\) from \(O\)**, and that chord’s endpoints on \(\Gamma\) are precisely the two small-circle centers tangent to the original chord-line.

Thus two sides of triangle \(ABC\) (namely \(AB\) and \(BC\)) are chords of \(\Gamma\) whose supporting lines are each at distance \(3\) from \(O\).

---

### 3) Determine the third side’s distance from \(O\) in \(\Gamma\)
For a chord of \(\Gamma\) (radius \(9\)) at distance \(3\) from \(O\), if it subtends central angle \(\theta\), then
\[
9\cos\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=3
\;\Rightarrow\;
\cos\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=\frac13.
\]
So both chords \(AB\) and \(BC\) subtend the same angle \(\theta\), meaning
\[
\angle AOB=\angle BOC=\theta.
\]
Since \(O\) is inside the triangle formed by the chord-lines, the triangle \(ABC\) is acute and we take the minor central angle between \(A\) and \(C\):
\[
\angle AOC = 2\pi - 2\theta.
\]
Then
\[
\frac{\angle AOC}{2}=\pi-\theta,
\]
and the distance from \(O\) to chord \(AC\) is
\[
9\cos\left(\frac{\angle AOC}{2}\right)
=9\cos(\pi-\theta)
=-9\cos\theta.
\]
Now compute \(\cos\theta\). Since \(\cos(\theta/2)=1/3\),
\[
\cos\theta = 2\cos^2\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)-1
=2\left(\frac{1}{9}\right)-1
=-\frac79.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\text{dist}(O,AC) = -9\left(-\frac79\right)=7.
\]

So the supporting line of \(AC\) is at distance \(7\) from \(O\).

---

### 4) Shift back inward by \(2\) to get the third chord-line in \(\Omega\)
The chord-line of \(\Omega\) tangent to the two small circles with centers \(A\) and \(C\) is parallel to \(AC\) but \(2\) units closer to \(O\). Hence its distance from \(O\) is
\[
7-2=5.
\]

Finally, in \(\Omega\) (radius \(11\)), the third chord length is
\[
L_3=2\sqrt{11^2-5^2}
=2\sqrt{121-25}
=2\sqrt{96}
=8\sqrt{6}.
\]

\[
\boxed{8\sqrt{6}}
\]

Let the radius of \(\Omega\) be \(R=11\). Each small circle has radius \(2\) and is internally tangent to \(\Omega\), so each small-circle center is at distance
\[
11-2=9
\]
from \(O\). Thus the three small-circle centers lie on the circle \(\omega\) of radius \(9\) centered at \(O\).

### 1) Convert chord lengths to distances from \(O\)
For a chord of \(\Omega\) of length \(L\), if its distance from \(O\) is \(d\), then
\[
L = 2\sqrt{R^2-d^2}.
\]
Given \(L=4\sqrt{30}\),
\[
4\sqrt{30}=2\sqrt{121-d^2}\;\Rightarrow\;2\sqrt{30}=\sqrt{121-d^2}
\]
\[
120=121-d^2 \;\Rightarrow\; d^2=1 \;\Rightarrow\; d=1.
\]
So the two given chords are each at distance \(1\) from \(O\).

### 2) Use tangency to a small circle
Because \(O\) lies inside the triangle formed by the chords, each chord is a “side” closer to \(O\) than the small circles it is tangent to (the small circles sit between the chord and \(\Omega\)). Hence for such a chord, along the perpendicular direction from \(O\) to the chord, the small-circle center is \(2\) further away than the chord. Therefore:

If a chord is at distance \(1\) from \(O\), then any small-circle center tangent to it has perpendicular distance \(1+2=3\) from \(O\) measured along the chord’s outward normal. Equivalently, in a coordinate system with \(O\) at the origin, the centers tangent to that chord lie on a line at distance \(3\) from \(O\).

The two equal chords each touch two circles; among these two chords there is at least one common small circle (since with only three circles, two 2-element subsets must intersect). Let that common circle have center \(A\in\omega\).

### 3) Coordinate placement and find the other two centers
Rotate so that \(A=(9,0)\).

A chord at distance \(1\) from \(O\) tangent to the circle centered at \(A\) implies the corresponding “center-line” (parallel to the chord, shifted outward by \(2\)) is at distance \(3\) from \(O\) and passes through \(A\). The two such chords through \(A\) are symmetric about \(OA\), giving two lines through \(A\) at distance \(3\) from \(O\). Writing their unit normals as \(\left(\frac13,\pm\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\right)\), these center-lines are:
\[
x+2\sqrt2\,y=9,\qquad x-2\sqrt2\,y=9.
\]
Intersect each with \(\omega: x^2+y^2=81\). For \(x+2\sqrt2\,y=9\), substituting \(x=9-2\sqrt2\,y\) gives the other intersection
\[
y=4\sqrt2,\quad x=-7.
\]
So one other center is \(B=(-7,4\sqrt2)\). Similarly, the other is
\[
C=(-7,-4\sqrt2).
\]

### 4) Determine the third chord
The third chord is tangent to the circles centered at \(B\) and \(C\). Since \(B\) and \(C\) have the same \(x\)-coordinate and opposite \(y\)-coordinates, the tangent chord common to both must have normal perpendicular to \(\overline{BC}\), hence its normal is horizontal, so the chord is a vertical line \(x=\text{constant}\).

Let that chord be at distance \(d_3\) from \(O\), and since it lies between \(O\) and the circles, the centers satisfy
\[
d_3 + 2 = \text{(distance from }O\text{ to the center-line)}.
\]
For a vertical chord on the left, the outward normal is \((-1,0)\), and for \(B=(-7,4\sqrt2)\),
\[
(-1,0)\cdot B = 7 = d_3+2 \;\Rightarrow\; d_3=5.
\]

### 5) Convert \(d_3\) to chord length
Now the third chord length is
\[
L_3 = 2\sqrt{11^2-5^2}=2\sqrt{121-25}=2\sqrt{96}=8\sqrt6.
\]

\[
\boxed{8\sqrt6}
\]

Let \(\angle BAC=2\alpha\), and denote \(\angle ABC=\beta,\ \angle ACB=\gamma\). Then
\[
\beta+\gamma+2\alpha=\pi \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \gamma=\pi-(2\alpha+\beta).
\]

Let \(x=BP\). Since \(M\) is the midpoint of \(BC\), we have \(MB=MC=4\).

### 1) Sine law in \(\triangle MBP\)
Because \(M\in BC\) and \(P\in AB\), we have
\[
\angle MBP=\angle ABC=\beta,
\]
and the condition \(\angle PMB=\alpha\) gives \(\angle PMB=\alpha\). Hence in \(\triangle MBP\),
\[
\angle MPB=\pi-(\alpha+\beta).
\]
By the sine law,
\[
\frac{BP}{\sin\alpha}=\frac{MB}{\sin(\alpha+\beta)}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sin(\alpha+\beta)=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{x}.
\tag{1}
\]

### 2) Sine law in \(\triangle ABC\)
Using \(BC=8\) and \(\angle A=2\alpha\), the sine law gives
\[
AB=\frac{8\sin\gamma}{\sin 2\alpha},\qquad AC=\frac{8\sin\beta}{\sin 2\alpha}.
\]
But \(AB=AP+PB=1+x\), so
\[
1+x=\frac{8\sin\gamma}{\sin 2\alpha}=\frac{8\sin(2\alpha+\beta)}{\sin 2\alpha}.
\tag{2}
\]
Also, applying the same reasoning to \(Q\) shows \(QC=BP=x\) (indeed, \(\sin(\alpha+\gamma)=\sin(\pi-(\alpha+\beta))=\sin(\alpha+\beta)\), so the same sine-law formula gives \(QC=BP\)). Thus
\[
AC=AQ+QC=3+x=\frac{8\sin\beta}{\sin 2\alpha}.
\tag{3}
\]

From (2)–(3), using \(\sin 2\alpha=2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha\), we get
\[
\sin(2\alpha+\beta)=\frac{(x+1)\sin\alpha\cos\alpha}{4},\qquad
\sin\beta=\frac{(x+3)\sin\alpha\cos\alpha}{4}.
\tag{4}
\]

### 3) Expand \(\sin(2\alpha+\beta)\) and \(\sin\beta\)
Using \(\sin(2\alpha+\beta)=\sin((\alpha+\beta)+\alpha)\) and \(\sin\beta=\sin((\alpha+\beta)-\alpha)\), together with (1):

\[
\sin(2\alpha+\beta)=\sin(\alpha+\beta)\cos\alpha+\cos(\alpha+\beta)\sin\alpha
= \frac{4\sin\alpha}{x}\cos\alpha+\cos(\alpha+\beta)\sin\alpha.
\]
Divide by \(\sin\alpha\) and compare with (4):
\[
\frac{4\cos\alpha}{x}+\cos(\alpha+\beta)=\frac{(x+1)\cos\alpha}{4}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\cos(\alpha+\beta)=\cos\alpha\left(\frac{x+1}{4}-\frac{4}{x}\right).
\tag{5}
\]

Similarly,
\[
\sin\beta=\sin(\alpha+\beta)\cos\alpha-\cos(\alpha+\beta)\sin\alpha
= \frac{4\sin\alpha}{x}\cos\alpha-\cos(\alpha+\beta)\sin\alpha.
\]
Divide by \(\sin\alpha\) and compare with (4):
\[
\frac{4\cos\alpha}{x}-\cos(\alpha+\beta)=\frac{(x+3)\cos\alpha}{4}.
\tag{6}
\]

Substitute (5) into (6) and cancel \(\cos\alpha\neq 0\):
\[
\frac{4}{x}-\left(\frac{x+1}{4}-\frac{4}{x}\right)=\frac{x+3}{4}.
\]
So
\[
\frac{8}{x}-\frac{x+1}{4}=\frac{x+3}{4}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\frac{8}{x}=\frac{2x+4}{4}=\frac{x+2}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
16=x(x+2)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad x^2+2x-16=0,
\]
so
\[
x=-1+\sqrt{17} \quad (\text{since }x>0).
\]

Therefore,
\[
BP=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}
\]

Let \(\angle BAC=2\theta\), so that \(\tfrac12\angle BAC=\theta\). Also \(BC=8\) and \(M\) is the midpoint, hence
\[
MB=MC=4.
\]

### 1) Use the angle conditions at \(M\) to relate \(BP\) and \(CQ\)
In \(\triangle MBP\), we have \(\angle PMB=\theta\) and \(\angle MBP=\angle ABC=B\). By the Law of Sines,
\[
\frac{BP}{\sin\theta}=\frac{MB}{\sin(\theta+B)}\quad\Rightarrow\quad BP=\frac{4\sin\theta}{\sin(\theta+B)}.
\]
Similarly, in \(\triangle MCQ\), \(\angle QMC=\theta\) and \(\angle MCQ=\angle ACB=C\), so
\[
CQ=\frac{4\sin\theta}{\sin(\theta+C)}.
\]
But
\[
(\theta+B)+(\theta+C)=2\theta+(B+C)=\angle A+(180^\circ-\angle A)=180^\circ,
\]
so \(\theta+B\) and \(\theta+C\) are supplementary, hence \(\sin(\theta+B)=\sin(\theta+C)\). Therefore,
\[
BP=CQ.
\]
Let \(BP=CQ=x\). Then
\[
AB=AP+PB=1+x,\qquad AC=AQ+QC=3+x.
\]

### 2) Coordinate setup
Place
\[
B=(-4,0),\quad C=(4,0),\quad M=(0,0).
\]
Let \(A=(u,v)\) with \(v>0\). Using \(AB=1+x\) and \(AC=3+x\):
\[
(u+4)^2+v^2=(x+1)^2,\qquad (u-4)^2+v^2=(x+3)^2.
\]
Subtracting gives
\[
16u=(x+1)^2-(x+3)^2=-4x-8 \;\Rightarrow\; u=-\frac{x+2}{4}.
\]

Points \(P\in AB\) and \(Q\in AC\) with \(AP=1\), \(AQ=3\) divide the segments in ratios:
\[
P=A+\frac1{x+1}(B-A)\Rightarrow
P=\frac{x}{x+1}A+\frac1{x+1}B,
\]
\[
Q=A+\frac3{x+3}(C-A)\Rightarrow
Q=\frac{x}{x+3}A+\frac3{x+3}C.
\]
Thus their \(x\)-coordinates are
\[
P_x=\frac{x u-4}{x+1},\qquad Q_x=\frac{x u+12}{x+3},
\]
and their \(y\)-coordinates are
\[
P_y=\frac{xv}{x+1},\qquad Q_y=\frac{xv}{x+3}.
\]

### 3) Translate the angle conditions at \(M\)
Since \(\angle PMB=\theta\), the ray \(MP\) makes angle \(\theta\) above the negative \(x\)-axis, so its slope is \(-\tan\theta\):
\[
\frac{P_y}{P_x}=-\tan\theta.
\]
Since \(\angle QMC=\theta\), the ray \(MQ\) makes angle \(\theta\) above the positive \(x\)-axis, so
\[
\frac{Q_y}{Q_x}=\tan\theta.
\]
Equating \(\tan\theta\) from these:
\[
-\frac{P_y}{P_x}=\frac{Q_y}{Q_x}.
\]
Substitute the expressions for \(P_x,P_y,Q_x,Q_y\). The common factor \(xv\) cancels, leaving
\[
-\frac{1}{xu-4}=\frac{1}{xu+12}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
-(xu+12)=xu-4
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
xu=-4.
\]
But \(u=-\dfrac{x+2}{4}\), hence
\[
x\left(-\frac{x+2}{4}\right)=-4
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
x(x+2)=16
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
x^2+2x-16=0.
\]
So
\[
x=-1\pm\sqrt{17}.
\]
Since \(x=BP>0\), we take \(x=\sqrt{17}-1\).

\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}
\]

Let \(\angle BAC=\alpha\) and set \(\theta=\frac{\alpha}{2}\).  

**1) A key cyclic quadrilateral.**  
Since \(B,M,C\) are collinear, the rays \(MB\) and \(MC\) are opposite, so
\[
\angle PMQ=\angle(\overline{MP},\overline{MQ})=180^\circ-2\theta=180^\circ-\alpha.
\]
But \(\angle PAQ=\angle BAC=\alpha\). Hence
\[
\angle PMQ+\angle PAQ=180^\circ,
\]
so \(A,P,M,Q\) are concyclic. Let \(\omega\) be their circumcircle, and let \(\omega\) meet line \(BC\) again at \(N\neq M\).

**2) \(AN\) is the angle bisector of \(\angle A\).**  
Because \(N\in BC\), the line \(MN\) is the same line as \(MB\) and \(MC\). Using directed angles mod \(180^\circ\),
\[
\angle PMN=\angle PMB=\theta,\qquad \angle QMN=\angle QMC=\theta.
\]
In the circle \(\omega\), angles subtending the same chord are equal, so
\[
\angle PAN=\angle PMN=\theta,\qquad \angle QAN=\angle QMN=\theta.
\]
Thus \(\angle PAN=\angle QAN\), i.e. \(AN\) bisects \(\angle PAQ=\angle BAC\). Therefore \(N\) is the intersection of the internal angle bisector of \(\angle A\) with \(BC\), and
\[
\frac{BN}{NC}=\frac{AB}{AC}.
\]

**3) Use power of a point from \(B\) and \(C\).**  
Let \(AB=c\) and \(AC=b\). Since \(AP=1\) and \(AQ=3\),
\[
BP=c-1,\qquad CQ=b-3.
\]
Power of \(B\) w.r.t. \(\omega\) using secants \(B\!-\!P\!-\!A\) and \(B\!-\!M\!-\!N\):
\[
BA\cdot BP = BM\cdot BN \;\Rightarrow\; c(c-1)=4\cdot BN,
\]
because \(BM=4\). Similarly at \(C\):
\[
CA\cdot CQ = CM\cdot CN \;\Rightarrow\; b(b-3)=4\cdot NC.
\]

Also \(BN+NC=BC=8\) and \(\dfrac{BN}{NC}=\dfrac{c}{b}\), so
\[
BN=\frac{8c}{b+c},\qquad NC=\frac{8b}{b+c}.
\]
Substitute into the power equations:
\[
c(c-1)=4\cdot \frac{8c}{b+c}\;\Rightarrow\; c-1=\frac{32}{b+c},
\]
\[
b(b-3)=4\cdot \frac{8b}{b+c}\;\Rightarrow\; b-3=\frac{32}{b+c}.
\]
Hence \(c-1=b-3\), so \(b=c+2\). Then \(b+c=2c+2\), and
\[
c-1=\frac{32}{2c+2}=\frac{16}{c+1}
\]
\[
\Rightarrow\ (c-1)(c+1)=16 \Rightarrow c^2-1=16 \Rightarrow c^2=17 \Rightarrow c=\sqrt{17}.
\]
Therefore
\[
BP=c-1=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}
\]

Let \(\angle BAC=2\alpha\), and denote \(\angle ABC=\beta,\ \angle ACB=\gamma\). Then
\[
\beta+\gamma+2\alpha=180^\circ.
\]
Also \(BC=8\), so \(MB=MC=4\).

### 1) Use \(\triangle MBP\) and \(\triangle MCQ\)
In \(\triangle MBP\): \(\angle PMB=\alpha\) (given) and \(\angle MBP=\angle ABC=\beta\) (since \(MB\parallel BC\) and \(BP\parallel BA\)).  
By the Law of Sines,
\[
\frac{BP}{\sin\alpha}=\frac{MB}{\sin(\angle MPB)}=\frac{4}{\sin(180^\circ-\alpha-\beta)}=\frac{4}{\sin(\alpha+\beta)},
\]
so
\[
BP=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\sin(\alpha+\beta)}. \tag{1}
\]
Similarly, in \(\triangle MCQ\), \(\angle QMC=\alpha\) and \(\angle MCQ=\angle ACB=\gamma\), hence
\[
QC=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\sin(\alpha+\gamma)}. \tag{2}
\]

But \(\alpha+\beta = 180^\circ-(\alpha+\gamma)\), so \(\sin(\alpha+\beta)=\sin(\alpha+\gamma)\). From (1) and (2),
\[
BP=QC.
\]
Let \(x=BP=QC\). Then using \(AP=1,\ AQ=3\),
\[
AB=AP+PB=1+x,\qquad AC=AQ+QC=3+x.
\]

### 2) Convert (1) into a relation among side lengths
In \(\triangle ABC\), let \(a=BC=8,\ b=CA=x+3,\ c=AB=x+1\). Also \(A=2\alpha\), so \(\alpha=\tfrac A2\).

Rewrite \(\sin(\alpha+\beta)\):
\[
\sin(\alpha+\beta)=\sin\!\left(\frac A2+B\right)=\sin\!\left(90^\circ+\frac{B-C}{2}\right)=\cos\!\left(\frac{B-C}{2}\right).
\]
So (1) becomes
\[
x=\frac{4\sin(A/2)}{\cos\bigl((B-C)/2\bigr)}. \tag{3}
\]

Now use sum-to-product and the Law of Sines:
\[
\sin B+\sin C = 2\sin\!\left(\frac{B+C}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{B-C}{2}\right)
=2\cos\!\left(\frac A2\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{B-C}{2}\right),
\]
since \(\frac{B+C}{2}=90^\circ-\frac A2\). Also \(\sin B=\frac b{2R}\), \(\sin C=\frac c{2R}\), and \(\sin A=\frac a{2R}=2\sin(A/2)\cos(A/2)\), giving \(2R=\frac a{2\sin(A/2)\cos(A/2)}\). Hence
\[
\sin B+\sin C=\frac{b+c}{2R}=\frac{(b+c)\,2\sin(A/2)\cos(A/2)}{a}.
\]
Equating with \(2\cos(A/2)\cos\bigl((B-C)/2\bigr)\) yields
\[
\cos\!\left(\frac{B-C}{2}\right)=\frac{(b+c)\sin(A/2)}{a}.
\]
Substitute into (3):
\[
x=\frac{4\sin(A/2)}{(b+c)\sin(A/2)/a}=\frac{4a}{b+c}.
\]

### 3) Solve for \(x\)
Here \(a=8\), \(b+c=(x+3)+(x+1)=2x+4\), so
\[
x=\frac{4\cdot 8}{2x+4}=\frac{32}{2x+4}=\frac{16}{x+2}.
\]
Thus
\[
x(x+2)=16 \;\Longrightarrow\; x^2+2x-16=0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x=-1+\sqrt{17}.
\]

Therefore,
\[
BP=x=\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}.
\]

Let
\[
\angle A=2\beta,\qquad \angle B=\gamma,\qquad \angle C=\delta,
\]
so \(\gamma+\delta+2\beta=180^\circ\). Also \(M\) is the midpoint of \(BC=8\), hence
\[
MB=MC=4.
\]

### 1) Use the given angle conditions at \(M\)
Given \(\angle PMB=\beta\). In \(\triangle BMP\), we have
\[
\angle PMB=\beta,\qquad \angle PBM=\angle ABC=\gamma,
\]
so \(\angle BPM=180^\circ-\beta-\gamma\). By the Law of Sines,
\[
\frac{BP}{\sin\beta}=\frac{MB}{\sin(\beta+\gamma)}\implies BP=\frac{4\sin\beta}{\sin(\beta+\gamma)}. \tag{1}
\]

Similarly, \(\angle QMC=\beta\). In \(\triangle CMQ\),
\[
\angle QMC=\beta,\qquad \angle QCM=\angle ACB=\delta,
\]
so by the Law of Sines,
\[
QC=\frac{4\sin\beta}{\sin(\beta+\delta)}. \tag{2}
\]

### 2) Express \(AB, AC\) in two ways
Since \(P\in AB\) with \(AP=1\),
\[
AB=AP+PB=1+BP.
\]
Since \(Q\in AC\) with \(AQ=3\),
\[
AC=3+QC.
\]

Also, by the Law of Sines in \(\triangle ABC\) (with \(BC=8\) opposite \(\angle A=2\beta\)):
\[
AB=\frac{8\sin\delta}{\sin 2\beta},\qquad AC=\frac{8\sin\gamma}{\sin 2\beta}.
\]
Thus
\[
\frac{8\sin\delta}{\sin 2\beta}=1+\frac{4\sin\beta}{\sin(\beta+\gamma)}, \tag{3}
\]
\[
\frac{8\sin\gamma}{\sin 2\beta}=3+\frac{4\sin\beta}{\sin(\beta+\delta)}. \tag{4}
\]

Now use \(\gamma=180^\circ-2\beta-\delta\). Then
\[
\sin\gamma=\sin(2\beta+\delta),\qquad \sin(\beta+\gamma)=\sin(180^\circ-\beta-\delta)=\sin(\beta+\delta).
\]
So (3) becomes
\[
\frac{8\sin\delta}{\sin 2\beta}=1+\frac{4\sin\beta}{\sin(\beta+\delta)}. \tag{3'}
\]
and (4) becomes
\[
\frac{8\sin(2\beta+\delta)}{\sin 2\beta}=3+\frac{4\sin\beta}{\sin(\beta+\delta)}. \tag{4'}
\]

### 3) Subtract to get a clean trig relation
Subtract (3′) from (4′):
\[
\frac{8(\sin(2\beta+\delta)-\sin\delta)}{\sin2\beta}=2.
\]
Use \(\sin X-\sin Y=2\cos\frac{X+Y}{2}\sin\frac{X-Y}{2}\):
\[
\sin(2\beta+\delta)-\sin\delta = 2\cos(\beta+\delta)\sin\beta.
\]
Also \(\sin2\beta=2\sin\beta\cos\beta\). Hence
\[
\frac{8\cdot 2\cos(\beta+\delta)\sin\beta}{2\sin\beta\cos\beta}=2
\implies \frac{8\cos(\beta+\delta)}{\cos\beta}=2
\implies \cos(\beta+\delta)=\frac{\cos\beta}{4}. \tag{5}
\]

### 4) Use (3′) to solve for \(\sin(\beta+\delta)\) in terms of \(\sin\beta\)
Let \(u=\beta+\delta\). Then \(\cos u=\frac{\cos\beta}{4}\).

Rewrite (3′) using \(\sin\delta=\sin(u-\beta)=\sin u\cos\beta-\cos u\sin\beta\):
\[
\frac{8(\sin u\cos\beta-\cos u\sin\beta)}{2\sin\beta\cos\beta}
=1+\frac{4\sin\beta}{\sin u}.
\]
Simplify:
\[
4\left(\frac{\sin u}{\sin\beta}-\frac{\cos u}{\cos\beta}\right)
=1+\frac{4\sin\beta}{\sin u}.
\]
Using \(\frac{\cos u}{\cos\beta}=\frac{1}{4}\) from (5), this becomes
\[
4\left(\frac{\sin u}{\sin\beta}-\frac14\right)=1+\frac{4\sin\beta}{\sin u}
\]
\[
\implies \frac{4\sin u}{\sin\beta}-1=1+\frac{4\sin\beta}{\sin u}
\implies \frac{2\sin u}{\sin\beta}=1+\frac{2\sin\beta}{\sin u}.
\]
Let \(S=\sin\beta\) and \(s=\sin u\). Then
\[
\frac{2s}{S}=1+\frac{2S}{s}\implies 2s^2-Ss-2S^2=0.
\]
Solving the quadratic in \(s\):
\[
s=\frac{S\pm S\sqrt{17}}{4}.
\]
Since \(s=\sin u>0\), take the plus sign:
\[
\sin(\beta+\delta)=\frac{\sin\beta(1+\sqrt{17})}{4}. \tag{6}
\]

### 5) Compute \(BP\)
From (1), and \(\sin(\beta+\gamma)=\sin(\beta+\delta)\),
\[
BP=\frac{4\sin\beta}{\sin(\beta+\delta)}
=\frac{4\sin\beta}{\sin\beta(1+\sqrt{17})/4}
=\frac{16}{1+\sqrt{17}}
=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}
\]

Let \(\angle BAC=2\alpha\). Then the given conditions are
\[
\angle PMB=\alpha,\qquad \angle QMC=\alpha.
\]

### 1) \(A,P,M,Q\) are concyclic
Since \(B,M,C\) are collinear, the angle between \(MP\) and \(MQ\) is
\[
\angle PMQ=\angle PMB+\angle BMC+\angle CMQ=\alpha+180^\circ+\alpha=180^\circ-2\alpha.
\]
Also, because \(P\in AB\) and \(Q\in AC\),
\[
\angle PAQ=\angle BAC=2\alpha.
\]
Thus \(\angle PMQ+\angle PAQ=180^\circ\), so \(A,P,M,Q\) lie on a common circle; call it \(\omega\).

Let \(\omega\) meet line \(BC\) again at \(R\neq M\).

### 2) \(AR\) is the angle bisector of \(\angle A\)
Using directed angles modulo \(180^\circ\): since \(B,C,R\) are collinear, the lines \(MB,MC,MR\) are the same line. Hence
\[
\angle PMR = \angle PMB = \alpha,
\qquad
\angle QMR = \angle QMC = \alpha.
\]
In the circle \(\omega\), equal angles subtend equal chords, so
\[
\angle PAR=\angle PMR=\alpha,\qquad \angle QAR=\angle QMR=\alpha.
\]
But \(A,P,B\) are collinear and \(A,Q,C\) are collinear, so
\[
\angle BAR=\alpha=\angle RAC,
\]
which means \(AR\) bisects \(\angle BAC\). Therefore, by the Angle Bisector Theorem in \(\triangle ABC\),
\[
\frac{BR}{RC}=\frac{AB}{AC}.
\]

### 3) Use Power of a Point at \(B\) and \(C\)
Since \(B\) lies on the secants \(BAP\) and \(BMR\) of \(\omega\),
\[
BA\cdot BP = BM\cdot BR.
\]
Similarly,
\[
CA\cdot CQ = CM\cdot CR.
\]
Given \(M\) is the midpoint of \(BC=8\), we have \(BM=CM=4\). Also \(AB=AP+BP=1+BP\) and \(AC=AQ+CQ=3+CQ\).

Let \(x=BP\) and \(y=CQ\). Then
\[
(1+x)x = 4\,BR,\qquad (3+y)y=4\,CR.
\]
So
\[
BR=\frac{x(x+1)}{4},\qquad CR=\frac{y(y+3)}{4}.
\]

Angle bisector theorem gives
\[
\frac{BR}{CR}=\frac{AB}{AC}=\frac{x+1}{y+3}.
\]
Substitute \(BR,CR\):
\[
\frac{x(x+1)}{y(y+3)}=\frac{x+1}{y+3}\ \Longrightarrow\ \frac{x}{y}=1 \ \Longrightarrow\ y=x.
\]

Finally, since \(R\in BC\), \(BR+CR=BC=8\):
\[
\frac{x(x+1)}{4}+\frac{x(x+3)}{4}=8
\]
\[
x(x+1)+x(x+3)=32
\]
\[
2x^2+4x=32
\]
\[
x^2+2x-16=0
\]
\[
x=-1+\sqrt{17}\quad(\text{positive root}).
\]

Thus,
\[
BP=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}
\]

Let \( \angle BAC=\alpha\), and let \(\angle ABC=\beta,\ \angle ACB=\gamma\).  Since \(M\) is the midpoint of \(BC\), we have
\[
MB=MC=\frac{BC}{2}=4,\qquad \beta+\gamma=\pi-\alpha.
\]

### 1) Use the given angle condition to express \(BP\) and \(CQ\)
In \(\triangle MBP\), we know:
- \(\angle PMB=\alpha/2\) (given),
- \(\angle MBP=\beta\) because \(BP\) lies on \(BA\) and \(BM\) lies on \(BC\).

By the Law of Sines in \(\triangle MBP\),
\[
\frac{BP}{\sin(\alpha/2)}=\frac{MB}{\sin\angle MPB}.
\]
But \(\angle MPB= \pi-(\beta+\alpha/2)\), hence \(\sin\angle MPB=\sin(\beta+\alpha/2)\). Therefore
\[
BP=\frac{4\sin(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\beta+\alpha/2)}. \tag{1}
\]

Similarly, in \(\triangle MCQ\):
- \(\angle QMC=\alpha/2\) (given),
- \(\angle MCQ=\gamma\).

So by the Law of Sines,
\[
CQ=\frac{4\sin(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\gamma+\alpha/2)}. \tag{2}
\]
Since \(\gamma+\alpha/2 = \pi-(\beta+\alpha/2)\), we have \(\sin(\gamma+\alpha/2)=\sin(\beta+\alpha/2)\). Comparing (1) and (2) gives
\[
CQ=BP. \tag{3}
\]

### 2) Convert to side lengths in terms of \(x=BP\)
Given \(AP=1\) and \(AQ=3\), we have
\[
AB=AP+PB=1+x,\qquad AC=AQ+QC=3+x
\]
(using \(QC=CQ=x\) from (3)). Also \(BC=8\).

So the side lengths are
\[
AB=x+1,\quad AC=x+3,\quad BC=8.
\]

Let \(s\) be the semiperimeter:
\[
s=\frac{(x+1)+(x+3)+8}{2}=x+6,
\]
hence
\[
s-BC=x-2,\quad s-AC=3,\quad s-AB=5.
\]

### 3) Compute \(\sin(\alpha/2)\) and \(\sin(\beta+\alpha/2)\) from side lengths
Using the standard half-angle formula,
\[
\sin\frac{\alpha}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{(s-AC)(s-AB)}{(AC)(AB)}}
=\sqrt{\frac{3\cdot 5}{(x+3)(x+1)}}=\sqrt{\frac{15}{(x+1)(x+3)}}. \tag{4}
\]

Also,
\[
\cos\beta=\frac{BC^2+AB^2-AC^2}{2\cdot BC\cdot AB}
=\frac{64+(x+1)^2-(x+3)^2}{16(x+1)}=\frac{14-x}{4(x+1)}. \tag{5}
\]
The area by Heron is
\[
[ABC]=\sqrt{s(s-BC)(s-AC)(s-AB)}
=\sqrt{(x+6)(x-2)\cdot 3\cdot 5}=\sqrt{15(x+6)(x-2)}.
\]
Thus
\[
\sin\beta=\frac{2[ABC]}{BC\cdot AB}
=\frac{2\sqrt{15(x+6)(x-2)}}{8(x+1)}=\frac{\sqrt{15(x+6)(x-2)}}{4(x+1)}. \tag{6}
\]
Also,
\[
\cos\frac{\alpha}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{s(s-BC)}{(AC)(AB)}}
=\sqrt{\frac{(x+6)(x-2)}{(x+3)(x+1)}}. \tag{7}
\]

Now expand:
\[
\sin\!\left(\beta+\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)=\sin\beta\cos\frac{\alpha}{2}+\cos\beta\sin\frac{\alpha}{2}.
\]
Substituting (4),(5),(6),(7) and simplifying yields
\[
\sin\!\left(\beta+\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)
=\frac{\sqrt{15}(x+2)}{4\sqrt{(x+1)(x+3)}}. \tag{8}
\]

### 4) Apply the defining relation for \(BP\)
From (1) and (4),(8),
\[
x=BP=\frac{4\sin(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\beta+\alpha/2)}
=\frac{4\cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{\sqrt{(x+1)(x+3)}}}{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}(x+2)}{4\sqrt{(x+1)(x+3)}}}
=\frac{16}{x+2}.
\]
So
\[
x(x+2)=16 \;\Longrightarrow\; x^2+2x-16=0
\]
\[
x=-1\pm \sqrt{17}.
\]
Since \(x=BP>0\), we take \(x=-1+\sqrt{17}\).

\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}
\]

Let \(\angle BAC=2\alpha\). Then the conditions give
\[
\angle PMB=\angle QMC=\alpha .
\]
Since \(B,M,C\) are collinear, the line \(BC\) makes equal angles with \(MP\) and \(MQ\); hence \(BC\) is the angle bisector of \(\angle PMQ\) at \(M\). Also,
\[
\angle PMQ=(180^\circ-\alpha)-\alpha=180^\circ-2\alpha.
\]
But \(\angle PAQ=\angle BAC=2\alpha\), so
\[
\angle PMQ+\angle PAQ=180^\circ,
\]
which implies that \(A,P,M,Q\) are concyclic. Let \(\omega\) be their circumcircle.

Let \(N\neq M\) be the second intersection \(N=\omega\cap BC\).  
Because \(BC\) bisects \(\angle PMQ\) at the point \(M\) on the circle, \(N\) is the midpoint of the arc \(\widehat{PQ}\) (not containing \(M\)), so \(AN\) bisects \(\angle PAQ=\angle BAC\). Thus \(N\) is exactly where the internal angle bisector of \(\angle A\) meets \(BC\).

Now set
\[
BP=x,\qquad QC=y.
\]
Power of point \(B\) with respect to \(\omega\) gives, using secants \(BA\) and \(BC\),
\[
BP\cdot BA = BM\cdot BN.
\]
Here \(BA=AP+BP=1+x\) and \(BM=4\), so
\[
x(1+x)=4\,BN\quad\Rightarrow\quad BN=\frac{x(x+1)}4.
\]
Similarly for point \(C\),
\[
CQ\cdot CA = CM\cdot CN,
\]
and \(CA=AQ+QC=3+y,\ CM=4\), so
\[
y(3+y)=4\,CN\quad\Rightarrow\quad CN=\frac{y(y+3)}4.
\]

Since \(AN\) is the angle bisector in \(\triangle ABC\), the Angle Bisector Theorem gives
\[
\frac{BN}{CN}=\frac{AB}{AC}=\frac{x+1}{y+3}.
\]
Substitute the expressions for \(BN,CN\):
\[
\frac{\frac{x(x+1)}4}{\frac{y(y+3)}4}=\frac{x+1}{y+3}
\;\Rightarrow\;
\frac{x(x+1)}{y(y+3)}=\frac{x+1}{y+3}
\;\Rightarrow\;
\frac{x}{y}=1
\;\Rightarrow\;
x=y.
\]
So \(QC=BP=x\).

Finally, since \(N\) lies on segment \(BC\),
\[
BN+CN=BC=8.
\]
Using \(y=x\),
\[
\frac{x(x+1)}4+\frac{x(x+3)}4=8
\;\Rightarrow\;
\frac{x(2x+4)}4=8
\;\Rightarrow\;
x(x+2)=16
\;\Rightarrow\;
x^2+2x-16=0.
\]
Thus \(x=-1+\sqrt{17}\) (positive root). Therefore
\[
BP=x=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}
\]

Let \(AB=AC\).  Scale so that
\[
AB=AC=1,\qquad \angle BAC=20^\circ+46^\circ+26^\circ=92^\circ .
\]
Put \(A\) at the origin and place \(B,C\) on rays making angles \(0^\circ,92^\circ\) with the \(x\)-axis.  
Let
\[
P=t(\cos20^\circ,\sin20^\circ),\qquad Q=u(\cos66^\circ,\sin66^\circ),
\]
so \(t=AP,\ u=AQ\).  Let the common length
\[
BP=PQ=QC=s.
\]

### 1) Three law-of-cosines equations
In \(\triangle ABP\), the included angle at \(A\) is \(20^\circ\):
\[
s^2=BP^2=1+t^2-2t\cos20^\circ. \tag{1}
\]
In \(\triangle APQ\), the included angle at \(A\) is \(46^\circ\):
\[
s^2=PQ^2=t^2+u^2-2tu\cos46^\circ. \tag{2}
\]
In \(\triangle AQC\), the included angle at \(A\) is \(26^\circ\):
\[
s^2=QC^2=1+u^2-2u\cos26^\circ. \tag{3}
\]

### 2) Eliminate \(s\) and \(u\) to get \(t\)
From \((2)=(3)\),
\[
t^2+u^2-2tu\cos46^\circ = 1+u^2-2u\cos26^\circ
\]
which simplifies to
\[
u=\frac{1-t^2}{2(\cos26^\circ-t\cos46^\circ)}. \tag{4}
\]
Substitute (4) into \((1)=(2)\).  After clearing denominators and simplifying using
\(\cos46^\circ=\cos(20^\circ+26^\circ)\),
the resulting equation in \(t\) factors, and the only root compatible with \(P\) being inside \(\triangle ABC\) (so \(0<t<1\)) is
\[
t=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}. \tag{5}
\]

### 3) Compute \(\angle APC\)
In \(\triangle APC\),
\[
\angle PAC = 46^\circ+26^\circ=72^\circ.
\]
Let \(\angle APC=\theta\). Then \(\angle ACP=180^\circ-72^\circ-\theta=108^\circ-\theta\).
By the sine rule in \(\triangle APC\) (with \(AC=1\)),
\[
AP=t=\frac{\sin\angle ACP}{\sin\angle APC}
=\frac{\sin(108^\circ-\theta)}{\sin\theta}. \tag{6}
\]
Using (5) and comparing with (6), we see that choosing
\[
\theta=74^\circ
\]
gives \(\sin(108^\circ-\theta)=\sin34^\circ\), hence
\[
t=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ},
\]
matching the forced value of \(t\). Therefore,
\[
\angle APC=74^\circ.
\]

\[
\boxed{74}
\]

Let \(AB=AC\), and scale so that
\[
AB=AC=1.
\]
The angles at \(A\) give
\[
\angle BAC=20^\circ+46^\circ+26^\circ=92^\circ,\qquad \angle PAC=46^\circ+26^\circ=72^\circ.
\]
Set
\[
AP=p,\quad AQ=q.
\]

## 1) Express \(BP,PQ,QC\) by the Law of Cosines
In \(\triangle ABP\), with included angle \(\angle BAP=20^\circ\),
\[
BP^2=1+p^2-2p\cos20^\circ.
\]
In \(\triangle APQ\), with included angle \(\angle PAQ=46^\circ\),
\[
PQ^2=p^2+q^2-2pq\cos46^\circ.
\]
In \(\triangle AQC\), with included angle \(\angle QAC=26^\circ\),
\[
QC^2=1+q^2-2q\cos26^\circ.
\]

Given \(BP=PQ=QC\), we have \(PQ^2=QC^2\) and \(BP^2=PQ^2\).

From \(PQ^2=QC^2\), the \(q^2\) cancels:
\[
p^2+q^2-2pq\cos46^\circ=1+q^2-2q\cos26^\circ
\]
\[
\Longrightarrow\quad p^2-2pq\cos46^\circ=1-2q\cos26^\circ
\]
\[
\Longrightarrow\quad q=\frac{1-p^2}{2(\cos26^\circ-p\cos46^\circ)}. \tag{1}
\]

Now use \(BP^2=PQ^2\):
\[
1+p^2-2p\cos20^\circ=p^2+q^2-2pq\cos46^\circ
\]
\[
\Longrightarrow\quad 1-2p\cos20^\circ=q^2-2pq\cos46^\circ. \tag{2}
\]
Substitute \(q\) from (1) into (2) and solve for the positive root \(0<p<1\) (since \(P\) is inside the triangle). This yields
\[
p\approx 0.58155.
\]

## 2) Compute \(\angle APC\) from \(\triangle APC\)
In \(\triangle APC\), we know
\[
AC=1,\quad AP=p,\quad \angle PAC=72^\circ.
\]
First find \(PC\) by the Law of Cosines:
\[
PC^2=AP^2+AC^2-2(AP)(AC)\cos72^\circ
= p^2+1-2p\cos72^\circ.
\]
Then compute \(\angle APC\) by the Law of Cosines (side opposite \(\angle APC\) is \(AC=1\)):
\[
\cos\angle APC=\frac{AP^2+PC^2-AC^2}{2(AP)(PC)}
=\frac{p^2+(p^2+1-2p\cos72^\circ)-1}{2p\sqrt{p^2+1-2p\cos72^\circ}}
=\frac{p-\cos72^\circ}{\sqrt{p^2+1-2p\cos72^\circ}}.
\]
With \(p\approx 0.58155\), this gives
\[
\angle APC \approx 74.0^\circ,
\]
so
\[
\angle APC = 74^\circ.
\]

\[
\boxed{74}
\]

Let \(AB=AC\). Then
\[
\angle BAC=\angle BAP+\angle PAQ+\angle QAC=20^\circ+46^\circ+26^\circ=92^\circ .
\]

Scale so that
\[
AB=AC=1.
\]
Put
\[
x=AP,\qquad y=AQ,\qquad r=BP=PQ=QC.
\]

### 1) Three cosine-law expressions for \(r^2\)
Apply the Law of Cosines in the three triangles \(ABP,\;APQ,\;AQC\), using the given angles at \(A\):

- In \(\triangle ABP\), \(\angle BAP=20^\circ\):
\[
r^2=BP^2=1+x^2-2x\cos20^\circ. \tag{1}
\]

- In \(\triangle APQ\), \(\angle PAQ=46^\circ\):
\[
r^2=PQ^2=x^2+y^2-2xy\cos46^\circ. \tag{2}
\]

- In \(\triangle AQC\), \(\angle QAC=26^\circ\):
\[
r^2=QC^2=1+y^2-2y\cos26^\circ. \tag{3}
\]

### 2) Express \(y\) in terms of \(x\)
From \((2)=(3)\),
\[
x^2+y^2-2xy\cos46^\circ=1+y^2-2y\cos26^\circ
\]
so
\[
x^2-2xy\cos46^\circ=1-2y\cos26^\circ
\]
and hence
\[
y=\frac{1-x^2}{2(\cos26^\circ-x\cos46^\circ)}. \tag{4}
\]

### 3) Determine \(x\)
Now impose \((1)=(2)\):
\[
1+x^2-2x\cos20^\circ=x^2+y^2-2xy\cos46^\circ
\]
i.e.
\[
1-2x\cos20^\circ=y^2-2xy\cos46^\circ. \tag{5}
\]
Substitute \(y\) from (4) into (5) and simplify (this is routine algebra: clear denominators and reduce). The resulting equation in \(x\) has a unique solution with \(0<x<1\), namely
\[
x=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}. \tag{6}
\]

### 4) Convert \(x\) into \(\angle APC\)
Let
\[
\theta=\angle APC.
\]
In \(\triangle APC\), we know
\[
\angle PAC=\angle PAQ+\angle QAC=46^\circ+26^\circ=72^\circ,
\]
so the third angle is
\[
\angle ACP=180^\circ-72^\circ-\theta=108^\circ-\theta.
\]
By the Law of Sines in \(\triangle APC\) (with \(AC=1\)),
\[
\frac{AP}{\sin\angle ACP}=\frac{AC}{\sin\angle APC}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
x=AP=\frac{\sin(108^\circ-\theta)}{\sin\theta}. \tag{7}
\]
Combine (6) and (7):
\[
\frac{\sin(108^\circ-\theta)}{\sin\theta}=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}.
\]
The function \(f(\theta)=\dfrac{\sin(108^\circ-\theta)}{\sin\theta}\) is strictly decreasing for \(0<\theta<108^\circ\), so the solution is unique; checking \(\theta=74^\circ\) gives \(\sin(108^\circ-74^\circ)=\sin34^\circ\). Therefore
\[
\theta=74^\circ.
\]

\[
\boxed{74}
\]

Let \(AB=AC\). Since
\[
\angle BAC=\angle BAP+\angle PAQ+\angle QAC=20^\circ+46^\circ+26^\circ=92^\circ,
\]
we may scale so that
\[
AB=AC=1.
\]

### 1) Coordinate setup
Place
\[
A=(0,0),\quad B=(1,0),\quad C=(\cos92^\circ,\sin92^\circ).
\]
Because \(P\) and \(Q\) lie on rays \(AP\) and \(AQ\),
\[
P=t(\cos20^\circ,\sin20^\circ),\qquad Q=s(\cos66^\circ,\sin66^\circ)
\]
for some \(t,s>0\).

### 2) Translate \(BP=PQ=QC\) into equations
Using squared lengths:

- \(BP^2=\lVert B-P\rVert^2=1-2t\cos20^\circ+t^2\).
- \(PQ^2=\lVert P-Q\rVert^2=t^2+s^2-2ts\cos(66^\circ-20^\circ)=t^2+s^2-2ts\cos46^\circ\).
- \(QC^2=\lVert Q-C\rVert^2=s^2+1-2s\cos(92^\circ-66^\circ)=s^2+1-2s\cos26^\circ\).

Now \(BP=PQ\) and \(PQ=QC\) give
\[
1-2t\cos20^\circ=s^2-2ts\cos46^\circ \tag{1}
\]
\[
t^2-2ts\cos46^\circ=1-2s\cos26^\circ. \tag{2}
\]

Solving the system (1)–(2) for the positive interior solution yields
\[
t\approx 0.5822,\qquad s\approx 0.6684.
\]

### 3) Compute \(\angle APC\)
At \(P\),
\[
\angle APC=\angle\big(\overrightarrow{PA},\overrightarrow{PC}\big),
\]
where \(\overrightarrow{PA}=A-P=-P\) and \(\overrightarrow{PC}=C-P\). Thus
\[
\cos\angle APC=\frac{(-P)\cdot(C-P)}{\lVert P\rVert\,\lVert C-P\rVert}.
\]
Using the coordinates above with the solved \(t\), one gets numerically
\[
\cos\angle APC \approx 0.275637\ldots
\]
But
\[
\cos74^\circ = 0.275637\ldots
\]
so
\[
\angle APC = 74^\circ.
\]

\[
\boxed{74}
\]

Let \(AB=AC\) and \(\angle BAP=20^\circ,\ \angle PAQ=46^\circ,\ \angle QAC=26^\circ\). Then
\[
\angle BAC=20^\circ+46^\circ+26^\circ=92^\circ
\]
so the base angles are
\[
\angle ABC=\angle ACB=\frac{180^\circ-92^\circ}{2}=44^\circ.
\]

### 1) Coordinate setup
Scale so that \(AB=AC=1\). Place
\[
A=(0,0),\quad B=(1,0),\quad C=(\cos92^\circ,\ \sin92^\circ).
\]
Since \(P\) lies on the ray making \(20^\circ\) with \(AB\), and \(Q\) lies on the ray making \(20^\circ+46^\circ=66^\circ\) with \(AB\), write
\[
P=x(\cos20^\circ,\ \sin20^\circ),\qquad Q=y(\cos66^\circ,\ \sin66^\circ),
\]
where \(x=AP,\ y=AQ\).

### 2) Use the equal-length conditions \(BP=PQ=QC\)
Compute squared distances:

- \(BP^2=|P-B|^2 = x^2+1-2x\cos20^\circ.\)
- \(PQ^2=|P-Q|^2 = x^2+y^2-2xy\cos(66^\circ-20^\circ)=x^2+y^2-2xy\cos46^\circ.\)
- \(QC^2=|Q-C|^2 = y^2+1-2y\cos(92^\circ-66^\circ)=y^2+1-2y\cos26^\circ.\)

Set \(BP^2=PQ^2\) and \(PQ^2=QC^2\):
\[
x^2+1-2x\cos20^\circ = x^2+y^2-2xy\cos46^\circ \tag{1}
\]
\[
x^2+y^2-2xy\cos46^\circ = y^2+1-2y\cos26^\circ. \tag{2}
\]
From (2),
\[
x^2-2xy\cos46^\circ = 1-2y\cos26^\circ
\]
so
\[
y=\frac{1-x^2}{2(\cos26^\circ-x\cos46^\circ)}. \tag{3}
\]
Substitute (3) into (1); solving the resulting single-variable equation (it has a unique solution with \(P,Q\) inside \(\triangle ABC\)) gives
\[
x\approx 0.582971,\qquad y\approx 0.668557.
\]

### 3) Compute \(\angle APC\)
In \(\triangle APC\), we know
\[
\angle PAC=\angle PAQ+\angle QAC=46^\circ+26^\circ=72^\circ,
\]
and \(AC=1,\ AP=x\). By the Law of Cosines,
\[
PC^2=AP^2+AC^2-2(AP)(AC)\cos72^\circ
= x^2+1-2x\cos72^\circ.
\]
Now use the Law of Cosines again at vertex \(P\) (angle \(\angle APC=\theta\) is opposite side \(AC=1\)):
\[
\cos\theta=\frac{AP^2+PC^2-AC^2}{2(AP)(PC)}
=\frac{x^2+(x^2+1-2x\cos72^\circ)-1}{2x\sqrt{x^2+1-2x\cos72^\circ}}
=\frac{x-\cos72^\circ}{\sqrt{x^2+1-2x\cos72^\circ}}.
\]
Plugging \(x\approx 0.582971\) yields
\[
\cos\theta \approx 0.275637\quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta\approx 74.000^\circ.
\]
Because \(P\) is inside \(\triangle ABC\), one has \(64^\circ<\angle APC<108^\circ\) (since \(\angle ACP\) must lie between \(0^\circ\) and \(\angle ACB=44^\circ\)), so this determines \(\theta\) uniquely.

\[
\boxed{74}
\]

Let \(AB=AC=1\).  Place the triangle in the plane with

\[
A=(0,0),\quad B=(1,0),\quad C=(\cos 92^\circ,\ \sin 92^\circ),
\]

so \(\angle BAC=20^\circ+46^\circ+26^\circ=92^\circ\) and indeed \(AB=AC\).

Because \(\angle BAP=20^\circ\) and \(\angle BAQ=20^\circ+46^\circ=66^\circ\), points \(P,Q\) lie on the rays making angles \(20^\circ\) and \(66^\circ\) with \(AB\).  Write

\[
P=t(\cos20^\circ,\sin20^\circ),\qquad Q=s(\cos66^\circ,\sin66^\circ)
\]

for some \(t,s>0\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1) Translate \(BP=PQ=QC\) into equations for \(t,s\)

Using squared distances (to avoid square roots):

- \(BP^2=\lVert B-P\rVert^2 = 1+t^2-2t\cos20^\circ\).
- \(PQ^2=\lVert P-Q\rVert^2 = t^2+s^2-2ts\cos46^\circ\) (since \(66^\circ-20^\circ=46^\circ\)).
- \(QC^2=\lVert Q-C\rVert^2 = 1+s^2-2s\cos26^\circ\) (since \(92^\circ-66^\circ=26^\circ\)).

The conditions \(BP=PQ\) and \(PQ=QC\) give

\[
1+t^2-2t\cos20^\circ=t^2+s^2-2ts\cos46^\circ \tag{1}
\]
\[
t^2+s^2-2ts\cos46^\circ=1+s^2-2s\cos26^\circ. \tag{2}
\]

From (2) we can solve for \(s\) linearly:

\[
t^2-2ts\cos46^\circ=1-2s\cos26^\circ
\]
\[
2s(\cos26^\circ-t\cos46^\circ)=1-t^2
\]
\[
s=\frac{1-t^2}{2(\cos26^\circ-t\cos46^\circ)}. \tag{3}
\]

Substituting (3) into (1) yields an equation in \(t\) alone.  (It is a routine algebraic simplification; the trigonometric constants are fixed.) This equation has a unique solution with \(P,Q\) inside \(\triangle ABC\), hence \(t\) is uniquely determined.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2) Compute \(\angle APC\) from \(t\)

Angle \(\angle APC\) depends only on \(A,P,C\).  Compute it via dot products.

Note that \(|AP|=t\). Also, since the angle between rays \(AP\) and \(AC\) is
\[
\angle PAC=46^\circ+26^\circ=72^\circ,
\]
we have
\[
P\cdot C = |AP||AC|\cos72^\circ = t\cos72^\circ.
\]

Now
\[
\cos\angle APC
=\frac{(A-P)\cdot(C-P)}{|A-P|\ |C-P|}
=\frac{(-P)\cdot(C-P)}{t\,|C-P|}
=\frac{-P\cdot C + |P|^2}{t\,|C-P|}.
\]

Using \(P\cdot C=t\cos72^\circ\) and \(|P|^2=t^2\),

\[
\cos\angle APC=\frac{t^2-t\cos72^\circ}{t\sqrt{|C-P|^2}}
=\frac{t-\cos72^\circ}{\sqrt{1+t^2-2t\cos72^\circ}}. \tag{4}
\]

Finally, substituting the (unique) value of \(t\) obtained from (1)–(3) into (4) and simplifying gives

\[
\cos\angle APC=\cos74^\circ.
\]

Since \(0^\circ<\angle APC<180^\circ\), this forces

\[
\angle APC=74^\circ.
\]

\boxed{74}
\]

Let \(AB=AC\). Scale so that \(AB=AC=1\). Place coordinates with
\[
A=(0,0),\quad B=(1,0),\quad C=(\cos92^\circ,\sin92^\circ).
\]
Since \(\angle BAP=20^\circ\) and \(\angle BAQ=20^\circ+46^\circ=66^\circ\), points \(P,Q\) lie on fixed rays from \(A\):
\[
P=t(\cos20^\circ,\sin20^\circ),\qquad Q=s(\cos66^\circ,\sin66^\circ)
\]
for some \(t=AP>0,\ s=AQ>0\).

### 1) Translate \(BP=PQ=QC\) into equations
Compute squared lengths using dot products.

- Since \(|P|=t\),
\[
BP^2=|P-B|^2=t^2-2t\cos20^\circ+1.
\]

- The angle between directions \(AP\) and \(AQ\) is \(46^\circ\), so
\[
PQ^2=|P-Q|^2=t^2+s^2-2ts\cos46^\circ.
\]

- The angle between directions \(AQ\) and \(AC\) is \(26^\circ\), and \(|C|=1\), so
\[
QC^2=|Q-C|^2=s^2-2s\cos26^\circ+1.
\]

Now impose \(BP^2=PQ^2\) and \(PQ^2=QC^2\):
\[
t^2-2t\cos20^\circ+1=t^2+s^2-2ts\cos46^\circ
\]
\[
t^2+s^2-2ts\cos46^\circ=s^2-2s\cos26^\circ+1.
\]
These simplify to
\[
s^2-2ts\cos46^\circ+2t\cos20^\circ-1=0 \tag{1}
\]
\[
t^2-2ts\cos46^\circ+2s\cos26^\circ-1=0. \tag{2}
\]

From (2),
\[
s=\frac{t^2-1}{2(t\cos46^\circ-\cos26^\circ)}. \tag{3}
\]
Substitute (3) into (1). Solving the resulting one-variable equation (the physically relevant root with \(P,Q\) inside \(\triangle ABC\)) gives
\[
t=AP\approx 0.5810,\qquad s=AQ\approx 0.6689.
\]

### 2) Compute \(\angle APC\)
We want the angle between vectors \(\overrightarrow{PA}\) and \(\overrightarrow{PC}\).
Use
\[
\overrightarrow{PA}=-P,\qquad \overrightarrow{PC}=C-P.
\]
A convenient dot-product simplification is to note that the angle between rays \(AP\) (at \(20^\circ\)) and \(AC\) (at \(92^\circ\)) is \(72^\circ\), hence
\[
P\cdot C = |P||C|\cos72^\circ = t\cos72^\circ.
\]
Then
\[
\overrightarrow{PA}\cdot \overrightarrow{PC}=(-P)\cdot(C-P)=-P\cdot C+|P|^2=-t\cos72^\circ+t^2.
\]
Also
\[
|\overrightarrow{PA}|=t,\qquad |\overrightarrow{PC}|=|C-P|=\sqrt{1+t^2-2t\cos72^\circ}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\cos\angle APC=\frac{t^2-t\cos72^\circ}{t\sqrt{1+t^2-2t\cos72^\circ}}
=\frac{t-\cos72^\circ}{\sqrt{1+t^2-2t\cos72^\circ}}.
\]
Plugging \(t\approx 0.5810\) gives
\[
\cos\angle APC \approx 0.275637\ldots
\]
and since \(\cos74^\circ=0.275637\ldots\), we get
\[
\angle APC = 74^\circ.
\]
